description: The bla gene encodes TEM-1 beta-lactamase, conferring resistance to ampicillin and related beta-lactam antibiotics. It is the most widely used selectable marker in bacterial cloning, allowing selection of plasmid-containing colonies on ampicillin-supplemented media. Nearly ubiquitous in cloning vectors such as pUC19, pBR322, pBluescript, and pGEM series.
mechanism: TEM-1 beta-lactamase is a serine protease secreted to the gram-negative periplasm that hydrolyzes the beta-lactam ring of ampicillin and related penicillins, inactivating the antibiotic before it reaches its transpeptidase target. The enzyme acts catalytically and can deplete extracellular ampicillin over time, leading to satellite colony formation in prolonged cultures.
known_variants: TEM-2 (Q39K substitution, functionally equivalent for lab use)
Codon-optimized bla for improved expression in specific hosts
Extended-spectrum TEM variants (ESBLs, e.g. TEM-3 through TEM-29) — rarely in lab vectors but found in clinical isolates
Truncated bla lacking signal peptide (cytoplasmic retention, reduced activity)
known_misannotations: AmpR promoter annotated as part of the AmpR CDS rather than as a separate feature
bla (gene symbol) and AmpR (resistance phenotype) used interchangeably without distinguishing CDS boundary
Signal peptide region (N-terminal 23 aa) sometimes excluded from annotated CDS coordinates
SHV-type and OXA-type beta-lactamases mislabeled as AmpR or bla(TEM-1)
Annotation spanning only the ORF without the native promoter, causing length discrepancy across databases
notes: AmpR selection is notoriously unstable in overnight or high-density cultures: secreted beta-lactamase accumulates in medium and degrades ampicillin, permitting growth of plasmid-free satellite colonies. Carbenicillin (a more stable ampicillin analog) is preferred for sustained selection. AmpR is not suitable as a mammalian selectable marker. The TEM-1 enzyme is encoded on the bla gene of transposon Tn3, originally mobilized from R plasmids; its promoter is strong and constitutive in E. coli. CDS alone is 861 bp (encoding 286 aa including signal peptide); full functional cassette including native promoter is ~1,000–1,100 bp.

description: KanR encodes an aminoglycoside 3'-phosphotransferase that confers resistance to kanamycin, neomycin, and the related aminoglycoside G418 (geneticin). It is one of the most widely used selectable markers in molecular biology, enabling selection of successfully transformed bacteria on kanamycin-containing media and of eukaryotic cells using G418. The gene originates from bacterial transposons (Tn903 or Tn5) and has been incorporated into hundreds of expression, cloning, and gene-targeting vectors.
mechanism: The APH(3') enzyme transfers a phosphate group from ATP to the 3'-hydroxyl of the aminoglycoside ring, converting kanamycin (and related compounds) into an inactive phosphorylated derivative that can no longer bind the 30S ribosomal subunit and inhibit translation.
known_variants: aph(3')-Ia from Tn903 (E. coli) — original bacterial kanamycin resistance determinant
aph(3')-IIa from Tn5 (Klebsiella) — also called nptII; most common in plant transformation and many modern vectors
codon-optimized nptII for plant expression (used in Agrobacterium-mediated transformation)
codon-optimized variant for mammalian G418 selection
promoter-swapped variants (e.g., driven by PGK or CMV promoter for eukaryotic selection)
known_misannotations: Conflation of aph(3')-Ia (Tn903) and aph(3')-IIa (Tn5) — distinct genes with the same activity, often both labeled 'KanR' or 'neo' interchangeably
Labeled 'neo' or 'neoR' in plant and mammalian vector maps even when the selection antibiotic is kanamycin, not neomycin
Labeled 'KmR' using the pharmacological abbreviation 'Km' for kanamycin, causing confusion with Michaelis constant Km
Entire resistance cassette (promoter + ORF + terminator) annotated as a single 'KanR' feature, obscuring sub-feature boundaries
Incorrectly annotated as AphA or aphA — a structurally unrelated aminoglycoside phosphotransferase
notes: Standard E. coli selection uses 25–50 µg/mL kanamycin sulfate. Mammalian and yeast cells require G418 (geneticin) at 200–1000 µg/mL because kanamycin itself does not penetrate eukaryotic cells effectively. Plant selection typically uses 100 mg/L kanamycin on MS agar. KanR is compatible for co-maintenance with AmpR plasmids when they carry different origins of replication (e.g., ColE1 + p15A). The Tn5-derived nptII (aph(3')-IIa) is the standard for plant transgenics and is also present in pET-28a and many other commercial vectors, despite being labeled simply 'KanR'.

description: The NeoR/KanR gene encodes aminoglycoside 3'-phosphotransferase type II (APH(3')-II), originally derived from the bacterial transposon Tn5, conferring resistance to kanamycin in prokaryotes and to G418 (geneticin) and neomycin in eukaryotes. It is one of the most widely used dual-purpose selectable markers in molecular biology, enabling kanamycin selection in bacterial cloning hosts and G418/neomycin selection for stable mammalian and plant cell line generation. Its broad host range and compatibility with shuttle vector strategies make it a foundational tool in synthetic biology and gene delivery.
mechanism: APH(3')-II phosphorylates the 3'-hydroxyl group of neomycin-class aminoglycoside antibiotics — including kanamycin, neomycin, and G418 — using ATP as the phosphate donor, producing inactive phosphorylated derivatives that can no longer bind the 30S ribosomal subunit and inhibit translation.
known_variants: codon-optimized for mammalian expression (higher G418 resistance levels in mammalian cells)
nptI (Tn903-derived, aphA1) — distinct gene sharing the KanR phenotype, frequently conflated with nptII
nptIII — a third family member with overlapping substrate range but distinct sequence
truncated/partial neo ORFs present as non-functional vestiges in older vector backbones
known_misannotations: nptI (Tn903, aphA1) and nptII (Tn5, aphA2) are distinct genes but both labeled 'KanR' interchangeably in many databases
Same gene annotated as 'KanR' in bacterial vectors and 'NeoR' in mammalian vectors with no cross-reference, obscuring identity
Confusion with aphA1 (nptI), which also confers kanamycin resistance but is ~50% identical at the protein level
G418 resistance sometimes attributed exclusively to codon-optimized mammalian variants, when the native Tn5 sequence also confers it at lower efficiency
notes: NeoR (nptII, Tn5-derived) and KanR often refer to the same gene used in different host contexts: 'KanR' denotes bacterial kanamycin selection (25–50 µg/mL) while 'NeoR' denotes mammalian G418/neomycin selection (200–800 µg/mL G418 depending on cell line). Some databases reserve 'KanR' for the nptI gene from Tn903, which is a related but biochemically distinct enzyme — sequence verification is recommended when comparing features across plasmid repositories. The canonical ORF is 816 bp (271 aa); annotated lengths in full expression cassettes vary widely depending on the included promoter and polyadenylation signal. Plant Agrobacterium-mediated transformation frequently uses this gene with the nopaline synthase promoter for kanamycin selection of transformed calli.

description: CmR encodes chloramphenicol acetyltransferase (CAT), an enzyme that inactivates the antibiotic chloramphenicol by acetylation. It is widely used as a selectable marker in bacterial cloning, and is particularly valued in dual-plasmid systems because vectors carrying CmR (typically on a p15A backbone) are stably compatible with ColE1-origin plasmids such as pUC or pBR322 derivatives.
mechanism: CAT catalyzes transfer of an acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to the 3-hydroxyl group of chloramphenicol, producing an acetylated derivative that is unable to bind the 50S ribosomal subunit and therefore cannot inhibit peptidyl transferase activity.
known_variants: Type I CAT (catA1, Tn9-derived) — most common variant in E. coli cloning vectors
Type II CAT — distinct protein family with different sequence but same activity
Type III CAT — found in Staphylococcus aureus transposons, used in gram-positive work
Codon-optimized CAT variants for improved expression in heterologous hosts
known_misannotations: Confused with cmlA/CmlR (chloramphenicol efflux transporter — a completely unrelated resistance mechanism)
Inconsistent use of gene symbol 'cat' vs. phenotype label 'CmR' across databases and vector maps
Variable annotation boundaries: some records include the upstream promoter, inflating reported length to ~800–900 bp
Occasionally mislabeled as 'CamR' or 'CAMr' in older literature and vector maps
notes: Standard selection in E. coli uses 25–35 μg/mL chloramphenicol; chloramphenicol is bacteriostatic rather than bactericidal, so prolonged selection can yield satellite colonies and resistant escapers if inoculum is too dense. The CAT enzyme is also used independently as a classic reporter in CAT assays. pDONR221 uses a ccdB/CmR cassette for negative selection against non-recombinant Gateway products, a distinct application from positive antibiotic selection.

description: HygR encodes hygromycin B phosphotransferase (HPT), an enzyme that confers resistance to the aminocyclitol antibiotic hygromycin B. It is widely used as a selectable marker across prokaryotic and eukaryotic systems because hygromycin B inhibits translation in both kingdoms, making HygR one of the most broadly applicable selection markers in molecular biology. It is commonly used to establish stable cell lines, select transformants in plant tissue culture, and as a second selectable marker in dual-selection strategies.
mechanism: Hygromycin B phosphotransferase transfers the gamma-phosphate of ATP to the C-4' hydroxyl group of hygromycin B, inactivating the antibiotic through phosphorylation. Unmodified hygromycin B inhibits protein synthesis by blocking translocation on the ribosome and causing misreading of mRNA in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes.
known_variants: Codon-optimized for mammalian expression (higher expression in human/mouse cells)
Codon-optimized for plant expression (used in Agrobacterium T-DNA vectors)
Codon-optimized for yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) expression
Fusion constructs: HygR-EGFP (dual reporter-selection cassette)
Truncated or promoter-swap variants with minimal CMV or PGK promoters
known_misannotations: Gene symbol confusion: hph (E. coli origin) vs. hpt (plant literature) refer to the same gene product
Enzyme class name APH(4)-Ia sometimes used as the feature name instead of the gene symbol
Labeled as 'hygromycin kinase' — incorrect; the enzyme is a phosphotransferase, not a kinase in the classical sense
Sometimes annotated as the full hygromycin resistance cassette (promoter + CDS + polyA) rather than just the CDS, inflating apparent length
Confused with the Streptomyces hygroscopicus self-resistance gene, which is a distinct sequence from the commonly used E. coli-derived hph
notes: HygR is unusual among common resistance markers in that hygromycin B is active in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes, making this marker effective across a uniquely broad host range without modification. Selection concentrations vary widely by host: ~50–200 µg/mL in E. coli, ~100–400 µg/mL in mammalian cells, ~20–50 µg/mL in yeast, and ~15–50 mg/L in plant tissue culture. The E. coli-derived hph gene (Gritz & Davies, 1983) is the predominant source used in expression vectors; it should not be confused with the aph(4) gene family from Streptomyces. Because hygromycin B is cytotoxic to untransformed mammalian cells at low concentrations, selection can be stringent and fast, but titration of concentration is important to avoid excessive non-specific cell death.

description: PuroR encodes puromycin N-acetyltransferase (PAC), originally derived from Streptomyces alboniger, and confers resistance to the aminonucleoside antibiotic puromycin. It is widely used as a selectable marker in mammalian cell culture because puromycin kills sensitive cells rapidly (within 1–3 days), enabling efficient selection of stable transfectants, transductants, or CRISPR-edited clones. Its fast selection kinetics and small gene size make it a preferred choice over neomycin (G418) in many mammalian systems.
mechanism: PAC catalyzes the transfer of an acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to the amine of puromycin, producing N-acetylpuromycin. The acetylated form cannot be incorporated into elongating ribosomes and is therefore non-toxic, allowing resistant cells to survive in puromycin-containing media.
known_variants: codon-optimized for human/mammalian expression (reduced GC content, ~52%)
codon-optimized for yeast (S. cerevisiae) expression
2A peptide fusion forms (e.g., T2A-Puro, P2A-Puro) for bicistronic co-expression
fusion to fluorescent proteins (e.g., Puro-IRES-GFP) for dual selection/visualization
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'pac' (the bacterial gene symbol) rather than 'PuroR' (phenotypic resistance name), causing name collisions with unrelated pac genes in other organisms
CDS length reported as 597 bp (coding only) versus 600 bp (coding + stop codon) depending on annotation convention
Occasionally mislabeled as 'PGK-Puro' when the promoter is included in the annotated feature span
Sometimes conflated with blasticidin resistance (bsr/BSD) in poorly curated databases
notes: Effective puromycin concentrations in mammalian cell culture typically range from 1–10 μg/mL depending on cell type; titration is recommended. The native S. alboniger pac gene has high GC content (~65%) characteristic of Streptomyces; humanized codon-optimized versions have significantly lower GC (~50–54%) and improved expression in mammalian cells. Puromycin is not used routinely for bacterial selection due to cost and the need for anaerobic conditions in some strains, though PAC does confer resistance in E. coli. The gene is small (~600 bp), making it easy to incorporate into size-constrained viral vectors such as AAV.

description: BleoR (Sh ble) encodes a small metal-free bleomycin-binding protein originally isolated from Streptoalloteichus hindustanus that confers resistance to the bleomycin family of antibiotics, including zeocin and phleomycin. It is widely used as a dominant selectable marker across bacteria, yeast, mammalian cells, and plants because a single copy confers robust resistance when expressed at sufficient levels. Its broad host compatibility makes it one of the few truly universal selection markers in plasmid engineering.
mechanism: The Sh ble protein sequesters zeocin/bleomycin in a stoichiometric 1:1 complex, directly binding the antibiotic before it can intercalate into DNA and generate double-strand breaks. Resistance is therefore proportional to protein expression level rather than enzymatic turnover.
known_variants: Codon-optimized for mammalian expression (reduced GC content, ~50%)
Codon-optimized for yeast (S. cerevisiae / P. pastoris) expression
EM7 promoter-fused version for constitutive E. coli expression
TEF1 promoter-driven version for constitutive yeast expression
known_misannotations: Labeled 'ZeoR' when the protein confers resistance to the entire bleomycin/phleomycin/zeocin family, not zeocin exclusively
Confused with the unrelated ble gene from transposon Tn5, which encodes a different bleomycin-binding protein
The full expression cassette (promoter + ORF + terminator) annotated as 'BleoR' rather than the ORF alone, inflating apparent feature length
Confused with bleomycin hydrolase (BleD/BleH) genes from Bacillus or mammalian sources, which inactivate bleomycin enzymatically via a different mechanism
notes: Because resistance is stoichiometric rather than catalytic, cells must maintain high Sh ble expression; working zeocin concentrations must be empirically optimized per cell type and passage number (zeocin degrades at 37°C and in light). Recommended working concentrations vary widely: ~25–100 µg/mL for E. coli, ~100–1000 µg/mL for mammalian cells, ~100–500 µg/mL for P. pastoris. The original S. hindustanus gene has ~66–68% GC content; codon-optimized versions used in commercial vectors (e.g., Invitrogen/ThermoFisher) have substantially lower GC and will differ significantly in sequence identity from the native gene.

description: The BSD gene from Aspergillus terreus encodes blasticidin S deaminase, an enzyme that confers resistance to the aminonucleoside antibiotic blasticidin S. It is widely used as a selectable marker for generating stable mammalian cell lines and in dual-selection strategies alongside other resistance markers. Blasticidin selection is particularly valued because it acts rapidly (selection complete in 5–7 days) and is effective across a broad range of cell types.
mechanism: Blasticidin S inhibits protein synthesis by interfering with peptide bond formation at the ribosome. Blasticidin S deaminase inactivates the antibiotic through hydrolytic deamination, converting blasticidin S to deaminohydroxyblasticidin, which lacks antibiotic activity.
known_variants: Codon-optimized for mammalian expression (reduced GC content)
Codon-optimized for yeast expression
BSD fused to fluorescent reporter (e.g., GFP-BSD) for combined selection and visualization
known_misannotations: Confused with bsr (blasticidin S resistance gene from Bacillus cereus), which is a structurally unrelated gene with a different mechanism but the same phenotype
Generic labeling as 'blasticidin resistance' or 'BlastR' without distinguishing BSD from bsr
Annotated as 'blast' which collides with the BLAST sequence alignment tool abbreviation in informal contexts
notes: BSD (from Aspergillus terreus) and bsr (from Bacillus cereus) are functionally interchangeable selection markers but encode different enzymes — bsr encodes a blasticidin S acetyltransferase or related inactivating enzyme, not a deaminase. BSD is the more commonly used mammalian marker and is the basis for Thermo Fisher's pcDNA6 series. Typical blasticidin working concentrations are 1–10 µg/mL depending on cell type. BSD is compatible with other common selection markers (neomycin, puromycin, hygromycin, zeocin) for multi-plasmid stable integration strategies.

description: ZeoR encodes the Sh ble protein from Streptoalloteichus hindustanus, conferring resistance to zeocin, a copper-chelated glycopeptide antibiotic of the bleomycin/phleomycin family that causes DNA double-strand breaks. It is widely used as a dominant selection marker across prokaryotes, yeast, insect, and mammalian systems due to its unusually broad host-range compatibility. Unlike most antibiotic resistance genes, resistance is stoichiometric rather than enzymatic, requiring sufficient Sh ble expression to titrate intracellular zeocin.
mechanism: The Sh ble protein binds zeocin in a 1:1 stoichiometric ratio, directly sequestering antibiotic molecules and preventing them from intercalating into DNA and generating lethal double-strand breaks. Because drug is neutralized by binding rather than degradation, effective selection requires promoter-driven expression levels that exceed the intracellular zeocin concentration.
known_variants: Codon-optimized Sh ble for mammalian expression (humanized, elevated GC content)
Codon-optimized Sh ble for plant expression
Codon-optimized Sh ble for Pichia pastoris (Komagataella phaffii)
Sh ble fused to fluorescent protein reporters for dual selection/visualization
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'ble' without species qualifier, conflating it with the structurally related but distinct Tn5 bleomycin resistance gene (BleR from Tn5 is homologous but not identical)
Annotated as 'phleomycin resistance' — phleomycin and zeocin are related compounds but Sh ble is specifically marketed as ZeoR by Invitrogen/Thermo
Entire selection cassette (promoter + Sh ble ORF + terminator) annotated as a single 'ZeoR' feature, obscuring the ORF boundaries
Confused with the unrelated 'ZeoR' zeolite resistance loci found in environmental bacterial genomes
notes: Zeocin is light-sensitive; selection plates must be prepared in darkness and stored foil-wrapped, as photodegradation inactivates the drug. Typical working concentrations differ substantially by host: 25–50 μg/mL in E. coli, 100–500 μg/mL in yeast, and 100–1000 μg/mL in mammalian cells. Because resistance is stoichiometric, weak or leaky promoters can result in incomplete protection and false-positive colonies. In bacteria, ZeoR selection is generally considered less robust than KanR or AmpR and may require concentration optimization. The gene is functional in both orientations relative to the replication origin, and dual-selection schemes pairing ZeoR with a second marker are common in yeast integration workflows.

description: SpecR (aadA) encodes spectinomycin/streptomycin 3"-adenylyltransferase, an enzyme that inactivates both spectinomycin and streptomycin by covalent AMP transfer, conferring high-level resistance to both antibiotics. It is widely used as a selectable marker in plasmid and integron contexts, and is the standard selectable marker for plant plastid (chloroplast) transformation.
mechanism: The aadA enzyme catalyzes ATP-dependent adenylylation of the 3"-hydroxyl group of spectinomycin and the 3"-amino group of streptomycin, chemically inactivating both drugs and preventing them from binding to the 16S rRNA of the 30S ribosomal subunit.
known_variants: aadA1 — canonical Tn21-derived allele; most widely used in laboratory vectors
aadA2 — second allele from integron In4/Tn1696; ~76% amino acid identity to aadA1
Ω (omega) cassette — aadA flanked by rrnB transcriptional terminators; used to create polar insertion mutations in bacterial operons
Chloroplast-codon-optimized aadA — redesigned for expression in plant plastids; used in tobacco and Chlamydomonas transformation
known_misannotations: Labeled solely as 'streptomycin resistance' — aadA confers dual resistance to both spectinomycin and streptomycin; the two phenotypes arise from the same gene and should not be treated as distinct features
Ω element annotated as plain 'aadA' — omits the flanking transcriptional terminators that are functionally critical for polar operon disruption
Confused with unrelated spc/SPC genes from Staphylococcus or Streptococcus (spectinomycin phosphotransferases; different enzyme class)
aadA2 and aadA1 used interchangeably despite meaningful sequence divergence affecting primer binding in diagnostic PCR
notes: SpecR/aadA is the canonical selectable marker for plant chloroplast transformation and is essential in that context because chloroplasts lack the β-lactamase activity needed to protect against ampicillin. It is also commonly encountered in class 1 integrons in clinical gram-negative isolates, which can complicate interpretation of resistance profiles in environmental samples. Spectinomycin selection is not effective in mammalian or yeast cells. The omega (Ω) cassette form additionally serves as a tool for studying transcriptional polarity in bacterial operons.

description: Tetracycline resistance coding sequence, most commonly derived from transposon Tn10, encoding either the TetA inner-membrane efflux pump (which directly confers resistance) or the TetR repressor protein that regulates tetA expression. In plasmid engineering, TetR CDS as a resistance marker typically refers to the tetA gene product, an active transporter that exports tetracycline from the cytoplasm and allows selection on tetracycline-containing media. It is widely used in E. coli and other gram-negative bacteria as an alternative or complementary selection marker to ampicillin resistance.
mechanism: TetA functions as a proton-dependent antiporter in the bacterial inner membrane, coupling inward proton translocation to efflux of tetracycline–divalent metal cation complexes (primarily Mg²⁺–Tc) from the cytoplasm to the periplasm. In the native Tn10 system, the TetR repressor binds tet operator sequences to silence tetA transcription until tetracycline binds TetR and releases repression, inducing pump expression.
known_variants: Tet(A) — original Tn10-derived efflux pump, most common in lab plasmids (~1,197 bp CDS)
Tet(B) — broader tetracycline analog substrate range, found in clinical Gram-negative isolates
Tet(C), Tet(D) — structurally related efflux pumps from distinct chromosomal origins
Tet(M), Tet(O) — ribosomal protection class; mechanistically distinct, not efflux-based
Codon-optimized variants for improved expression in gram-positive hosts
known_misannotations: TetR CDS is frequently annotated as the tetR repressor gene (~630 bp) rather than the tetA efflux pump (~1,197 bp) — both appear in resistance loci and share the TetR label
Conflation of tetA (resistance efflux pump) and tetR (transcriptional repressor) under a single 'TetR' feature label in many plasmid maps
Some annotations capture only a partial tet locus, omitting the regulatory elements (operator, promoter) needed for inducible, full-level resistance
Resistance cassettes from distinct transposons (Tn10, Tn1721, Tn4) annotated identically despite meaningful sequence divergence between classes
notes: The label 'TetR' is inherently ambiguous: in the molecular biology literature it most often denotes the tetracycline repressor protein (tetR gene, ~630 bp), which is widely exploited in Tet-On/Tet-Off inducible expression systems. As a resistance_marker annotation, 'TetR CDS' typically encodes the tetA efflux pump (~1,197 bp). Users should verify which gene is actually present by checking CDS length and protein product annotation. Tetracycline resistance is generally avoided as the primary selectable marker in mammalian cell work due to antibiotic effects on mitochondrial ribosomes; it remains most practical in bacterial systems. Tetracycline is bacteriostatic, so colony sizes on selective plates can be smaller and selection less stringent than with bactericidal markers such as ampicillin.

description: GentR encodes an aminoglycoside 3-N-acetyltransferase (most commonly AAC(3)-Ia, encoded by aacC1) that confers resistance to gentamicin and related 4,6-disubstituted aminoglycoside antibiotics. It is widely used as a selectable marker in gram-negative bacteria — particularly Pseudomonas, Agrobacterium, Burkholderia, and Rhizobium — where AmpR or KanR selection is less practical. The Ω-Gm (omega-gentamicin) cassette variant is additionally used for non-polar chromosomal gene disruptions.
mechanism: The enzyme catalyzes N-acetylation of the 3-amino group of gentamicin (and related aminoglycosides including tobramycin, sisomicin, and netilmicin) using acetyl-CoA as the acetyl donor, sterically blocking the aminoglycoside from binding to helix 44 of the 16S rRNA in the 30S ribosomal subunit.
known_variants: aacC1 (AAC(3)-Ia) — most common variant in plasmid engineering, originally from transposon Tn1696
aacC2 (AAC(3)-IIa) — alternative acetyltransferase with identical resistance phenotype but distinct sequence
aacA4 (AAC(6')-Ib) — acetylates at the 6'-amino position; broader aminoglycoside spectrum including amikacin
Ω-Gm (omega cassette) — flanked by bidirectional transcriptional terminators from pHP45Ω for non-polar insertions
Synthetic codon-optimized versions for expression in plant or mammalian cells under eukaryotic promoters
known_misannotations: Annotated generically as 'aminoglycoside resistance' without specifying the acetyltransferase class or acetylation position
aacC1 and aacC2 conflated — different gene sequences and substrate spectra, identical gentamicin phenotype
Confused with aph(3')-Ia (kanamycin/neomycin phosphotransferase), which shares some aminoglycoside overlap
Ω-Gm cassette annotated as plain 'GentR', obscuring the flanking terminators that suppress read-through into adjacent genes
Labeled 'GnR' or 'GmR' interchangeably without indicating which aac gene is present
notes: Typical selection concentration in E. coli is 10–15 µg/mL; Pseudomonas and Burkholderia often require 30–50 µg/mL. GentR is the preferred marker for broad-host-range work in gram-negative non-enterics because these species frequently carry intrinsic AmpR and KanR determinants. Not functionally expressed in mammalian cells without a eukaryotic promoter and poly-A signal. The Ω-Gm cassette (pHP45Ω-Gm origin) is the standard tool for non-polar disruptions in gram-negative chromosomes. aacC1 is encoded on Tn1696, which also carries mercury resistance, complicating interpretation in environmental strains.

description: ccdB encodes a bacterial toxin that poisons DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II), causing lethal double-strand DNA breaks in susceptible E. coli. It is the core negative selection marker in Gateway recombination cloning: vectors with an intact ccdB cassette kill non-recombinant host cells, while successful recombinants that have replaced ccdB with the insert survive. Propagation of ccdB-containing vectors requires a gyrase-resistant host strain such as DB3.1 or ccdB Survival.
mechanism: CcdB stabilizes the covalent DNA gyrase–DNA cleavage complex by binding the GyrA subunit, blocking the religation step and causing accumulation of irreversible double-strand breaks that are lethal to wild-type E. coli. The gyrA462 allele (Trp→Arg at position 462) confers resistance.
known_variants: codon-optimized ccdB used in some commercial Gateway vectors for enhanced toxicity
attenuated variants used to tune selection stringency in combinatorial library screens
known_misannotations: Confused with ccdA (the cognate antitoxin on the same operon)
Labeled as generic 'negative selection marker' or 'counterselection' without the gene name
Annotated as 'ccd' without specifying the B (toxin) subunit vs. A (antitoxin) subunit
Occasionally mislabeled as 'ccdb' in lowercase without capitalization convention
notes: Functional ccdB expression requires an E. coli host with wild-type gyrA; strains DB3.1 (Life Technologies) and ccdB Survival 2 T1R (Thermo Fisher) carry gyrA462 and are the standard propagation hosts. The ccdB/ccdA locus is native to the F plasmid ccd operon. In Gateway cloning, recombination between attB-flanked insert and attP-flanked ccdB cassette replaces the toxin gene, making selection stringent and highly efficient. ccdB is not functional in eukaryotic systems (no DNA gyrase target), so it serves as a prokaryotic-specific negative selection tool only.

description: SacB encodes levansucrase from Bacillus subtilis, an enzyme that polymerizes the fructose moiety of sucrose into levan (a β-2,6-linked fructan), which accumulates to toxic levels in gram-negative bacteria. It is used as a counterselection marker in allelic exchange, gene deletion, and plasmid-curing protocols: bacteria retaining the sacB gene are killed on sucrose-containing media, while those that have lost the construct (e.g., via homologous recombination) survive. This enables positive selection for loss of a suicide vector or replacement of a chromosomal sequence.
mechanism: Levansucrase catalyzes the transfer of fructosyl units from sucrose to an acceptor, producing sucrose-derived levan polymer; in gram-negative hosts the accumulated levan causes osmotic stress and membrane disruption, leading to cell death at 5–10% sucrose concentrations.
known_variants: Truncated sacB lacking the native signal peptide (used in some vectors to reduce basal levan export)
sacB driven by constitutive promoters (e.g., Ptac or Para) for tighter sucrose-dependent killing
sacBII — a point-mutant variant with altered substrate specificity used in specific Pseudomonas protocols
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'sucrase' rather than 'levansucrase', conflating it with invertase (sacA) activity
Confused with sacC (endo-levanase) or sacA (sucrose-6-phosphate hydrolase) from the same B. subtilis sac operon
Signal sequence (N-terminal ~33 aa) sometimes omitted from annotation, causing apparent length discrepancy vs. reference
Incorrectly used in gram-positive hosts (e.g., Bacillus), where levan is not toxic and counterselection fails
notes: SacB counterselection is highly effective in E. coli and many gram-negative pathogens (Vibrio, Pseudomonas, Salmonella, Yersinia) but does NOT work in gram-positive bacteria including the native host B. subtilis, nor in many Burkholderia species which are intrinsically sucrose-tolerant. Sucrose concentration for selection is typically 5–10% (w/v); below 5% escape mutants are frequent. Spontaneous sacB loss-of-function mutations (frameshifts, insertion sequences) can generate false-positive survivors — confirm allelic exchange by colony PCR or sequencing.

description: The pMB1 ori is the origin of replication from the naturally occurring plasmid pMB1, isolated from Escherichia coli. It is functionally nearly identical to the ColE1 origin (differing by only a few nucleotides) and is the progenitor of the replication origins found in pBR322 and the pUC series of cloning vectors. It supports autonomous replication in E. coli and closely related gram-negative bacteria, and is among the most widely used origins in molecular cloning.
mechanism: Replication initiates via a constitutively transcribed RNA (RNAII) that hybridizes to the template strand and is processed by RNase H to generate the primer for leading-strand synthesis by DNA Pol I. Copy number is controlled by RNAI, a short antisense RNA that base-pairs with RNAII to block primer formation; the plasmid-encoded ROP protein enhances RNAI–RNAII pairing to maintain a low-copy (15–20 copies/cell) phenotype in the native configuration.
known_variants: High-copy variant: point mutation in RNAII stem-loop (found in pUC vectors) increases copy number to ~500–700/cell
rop-deleted variant: deletion of the rop gene removes ROP-mediated copy number repression, elevating copy number ~3-fold
pUC ori: pMB1 ori carrying both the RNAII mutation and rop deletion, commonly annotated separately in modern vectors
known_misannotations: Frequently annotated as 'ColE1 ori' even when the sequence is specifically derived from pMB1 (the two differ by <5 bp but are distinct plasmids)
pUC-derived high-copy versions are sometimes annotated as 'pMB1 ori' despite carrying a copy-number-altering mutation that distinguishes them functionally
Occasionally confused with the p15A ori (from pACYC184), which is compatible with pMB1/ColE1 origins but unrelated in sequence
notes: pMB1 ori and ColE1 ori are so closely related that they are incompatible with each other (same incompatibility group, IncFII/ColE1) — plasmids carrying both cannot be stably co-maintained in the same cell. The pMB1 designation is most common in annotations of pBR322 and its direct derivatives; the term 'ColE1 ori' is more often used for pUC-series and later vectors. Both terms refer to the same functional class. Replication is independent of the plasmid-encoded Rep protein and depends entirely on host machinery (E. coli Pol I, RNase H), limiting its functional host range to organisms that provide compatible enzymes.

description: The ColE1 origin of replication is a ~590 bp cis-acting sequence derived from the naturally occurring ColE1 plasmid that drives autonomous replication in Escherichia coli and related gram-negative bacteria. Wild-type ColE1 ori maintains a moderate copy number of 15–20 plasmid copies per cell and is the basis for many foundational cloning vectors including pBR322. A point-mutant high-copy derivative found in pUC vectors increases copy number to 500–700 copies per cell by disrupting negative regulation.
mechanism: Replication is initiated by RNA II, a plasmid-encoded transcript that forms a persistent RNA–DNA hybrid at the origin and is processed by host RNase H to generate the primer for DNA Polymerase I-driven leading-strand synthesis. Copy number is negatively regulated by RNA I, a 108-nt antisense transcript that base-pairs with and inhibits RNA II, with the Rop/ROM protein acting as a chaperone to accelerate RNA I–RNA II duplex formation and suppress copy number.
known_variants: pUC ori — single point mutation in the RNA II stem-loop that disrupts Rop/ROM-mediated regulation, yielding ~500–700 copies/cell; present in pUC18, pUC19, and most modern cloning vectors
pMB1 ori — direct progenitor of pBR322 ori, derived from ColE1 and differing by a single nucleotide; functionally identical and in the same incompatibility group
ColE1 Δrop — deletion of the rop gene (encoding Rop/ROM protein) without altering the origin sequence; raises copy number to ~30–40 copies/cell
known_misannotations: pMB1 ori and ColE1 ori used interchangeably — they are nearly identical but technically distinct plasmid origins from different source plasmids
pUC ori mislabeled as ColE1 ori — the pUC origin is a high-copy point-mutant derivative, not the wild-type ColE1 origin; copy number differs ~25-fold
p15A ori confused with ColE1 ori — p15A is a distinct, compatible origin (different incompatibility group) used in two-plasmid systems; the two are unrelated in sequence

Annotation boundary inconsistency — some depositors annotate only the minimal ~590 bp replication region while others include the flanking rop gene or extended regulatory sequences, causing length variation in databases
notes: All ColE1-type origins (ColE1, pMB1, pUC) belong to the ColE1 incompatibility group and cannot stably coexist in the same cell; use p15A ori (e.g., pACYC184) for compatible two-plasmid systems. Replication strictly requires host-encoded DNA Pol I and RNase H and does not function in gram-positive bacteria, yeast, or eukaryotes. The rop gene, located ~450 bp downstream of the origin, is often co-annotated but is a separate feature; its deletion or disruption phenocopies the pUC high-copy mutation. Temperature sensitivity at >37°C can reduce plasmid stability in some ColE1 derivatives.

description: The p15A ori is a bacterial origin of replication derived from plasmid p15A, maintaining approximately 10–20 copies per cell in E. coli under standard growth conditions. It is widely used in synthetic biology and plasmid engineering because it belongs to a different incompatibility group than ColE1-type origins (pMB1/pUC), allowing two plasmids carrying different selection markers to be stably co-maintained in the same host cell. Classic vectors carrying this origin include pACYC177 and pACYC184.
mechanism: Replication initiates bidirectionally from the ori locus using host DNA polymerase III after an RNA primer (RNAII) is processed by RNase H; copy number is regulated by a cis-acting antisense RNAI transcript that does not cross-react with ColE1 RNAI, which is the molecular basis for compatibility between p15A and ColE1 plasmid families.
known_variants: High-copy mutant p15A ori with altered RNAI-RNAII interaction region (~30–50 copies per cell)
Minimal truncation retaining full replication competence at ~500 bp
known_misannotations: Annotated simply as 'ori' without p15A designation, causing confusion with ColE1 ori in sequence databases
Boundary coordinates inconsistent across databases; some annotations include flanking sequences from the original p15A vector backbone
Occasionally mislabeled as 'ColA ori' in older literature — ColA and p15A are related but distinct origins
Some tools annotate the entire pACYC replication region (~1.5 kb including flanking genes) rather than the minimal functional ori
notes: p15A ori functions exclusively in gram-negative bacteria and requires no plasmid-encoded replication proteins — all factors are supplied in trans by the E. coli host. Its low copy number (~15 copies/cell) is deliberately exploited when high-level gene expression from a co-resident ColE1 plasmid would be toxic, allowing p15A-based plasmids to deliver a second cassette at moderate dosage. The p15A and ColE1 ori families are the two most commonly paired origins in two-plasmid E. coli expression systems.

description: The pSC101 origin of replication is a low-copy-number origin derived from the Escherichia coli plasmid pSC101 (Stanford Collection 101), maintaining plasmids at approximately 3–5 copies per cell. It is widely used in plasmid engineering when low-copy maintenance is required, such as for expressing toxic genes, stable genomic editing constructs, or when minimizing metabolic burden on the host. Compatibility with ColE1-type origins makes it useful for dual-plasmid systems.
mechanism: Replication initiates at the oriR region through sequence-specific binding of the plasmid-encoded RepA protein to iterons (direct repeats), which recruits host DnaA and the replication machinery. The iteron-handcuffing model of RepA explains its stringent copy-number control: RepA dimers bridge plasmid molecules, inhibiting reinitiation when copy number is sufficient.
known_variants: pSC101(ts) — temperature-sensitive repA mutant; replicates at 30°C but not at 37–43°C, enabling plasmid curing after use (used in lambda Red recombineering vectors pKD46, pKD32)
pSC101* (ori*) — point mutation in iterons that relaxes copy-number control, raising copy number to ~15–20 copies/cell while retaining incompatibility group IncW
Minimal ori fragment — ~500 bp core sequence lacking repA; requires RepA supplied in trans
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'p15A ori' in some older vectors — pSC101 and p15A are both low-copy but belong to different incompatibility groups (IncW vs IncFII) and are not interchangeable
Temperature-sensitive variant (ts) sometimes annotated simply as 'pSC101 ori' without indicating the ts phenotype, causing confusion about expected copy number and growth temperature
repA gene sometimes annotated separately from the ori, or omitted, even though RepA is required in cis for replication initiation from most pSC101-based constructs
notes: pSC101 ori belongs to incompatibility group IncW and is compatible with ColE1 (pMB1), p15A, and pBBR1 origins, making it suitable for multi-plasmid systems. The ~3–5 copy number is useful for stable, low-burden maintenance but requires selection pressure for reliable retention. The temperature-sensitive allele (repA101ts) is extensively used in recombineering and CRISPR editing workflows where transient plasmid presence followed by thermal curing is desired. pSC101 requires the host-encoded ParA/ParB-like Par system for stable segregation at low copy number.

description: The vegetative origin of replication (oriV) from the broad-host-range IncP-1α plasmid RK2 (also isolated independently as RP1, RP4, and R68). It enables autonomous plasmid replication across a remarkably wide range of gram-negative bacteria, making it the most widely used broad-host-range origin in synthetic biology, environmental microbiology, and studies of non-enteric bacteria. Vectors carrying RK2 oriV are maintained at low copy number (approximately 4–7 copies per chromosome equivalent in E. coli).
mechanism: Replication initiation requires the plasmid-encoded TrfA protein, which binds to five 17-mer direct-repeat iterons within oriV and recruits the host DnaB helicase and replisome. TrfA functions both as an activator (iteron binding) and as a copy-number autoregulator through iteron handcuffing, in which TrfA-bound iteron arrays on separate plasmid molecules are paired to inhibit excess initiations.
known_variants: Minimal oriV core (~400 bp; contains iterons and AT-rich unwinding element only, requires TrfA supplied in trans from a helper or chromosomally integrated copy)
Self-replicating oriV + trfA cassette (~3.5 kb; TrfA provided in cis, no helper required)
Thermosensitive trfA allele variants (tsA mutants; conditional replication at 30°C but not 37°C, widely used for allelic-exchange and gene-deletion vectors in Pseudomonas and related genera)
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'oriT' (conjugative transfer origin) when 'oriV' (vegetative replication origin) is intended — oriT and oriV are distinct, non-overlapping elements on RK2
Feature annotated as 'RK2 ori' when only the trfA coding sequence or the iteron array (not the full functional oriV) is present
Confused with pBBR1 ori in broad-host-range vectors — pBBR1 ori is unrelated, has higher copy number, and requires the Rep protein rather than TrfA
RK2, RP4, RP1, and R68 are the same plasmid isolated independently in different laboratories; all four names refer to an identical replicon, and annotations using any of these names are equivalent
notes: RK2 oriV replicates in a broad range of gram-negative Proteobacteria including Pseudomonas, Agrobacterium, Rhizobium, Caulobacter, Ralstonia, and many Enterobacteriaceae; it does not function in gram-positive organisms or eukaryotes. RK2-based vectors are incompatible with other IncP-group plasmids. Delivery to non-E. coli hosts is typically accomplished by triparental mating using pRK2013 as a mobilizing helper, which provides oriT nicking and conjugative machinery in trans. The full replicon requires TrfA; minimal-oriV vectors without a trfA gene will not replicate unless TrfA is supplied from another source.

description: The origin of replication from pBBR1, a broad-host-range plasmid originally isolated from Bordetella bronchiseptica. It confers replication capability across a remarkably wide range of gram-negative bacteria, including Pseudomonas, Rhizobium, Agrobacterium, Caulobacter, and E. coli. It is widely used in synthetic biology and metabolic engineering when expression in non-enteric gram-negative hosts is required.
mechanism: Replication is initiated by the plasmid-encoded Rep protein, which binds to iterons within the origin and recruits host replication machinery for strand synthesis. The Rep protein is essential and must be present in cis; the ori is not functional without the rep gene.
known_variants: High-copy variants with mutations in the Rep protein or iteron sequences
Minimal ori constructs containing only the rep gene and essential iteron region
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'rep' only (the replication protein gene), omitting the flanking iteron sequences required for ori function
Confused with RK2/RP4 ori, which is a different incompatibility group broad-host-range origin
Sometimes labeled 'ColE1' by automated tools in non-E. coli contexts despite being a distinct replicon
notes: pBBR1 ori belongs to incompatibility group BHRA and is compatible with IncP, IncQ, IncW, and ColE1 replicons, making it stackable with common lab plasmids. Copy number is typically 10–20 per chromosome in E. coli. The Rep protein contains a rolling-circle-replication motif but pBBR1 replicates via a theta mechanism. Mobilizable by RP4/RK2 Tra functions but does not self-transmit.

description: The R6K gamma (γ) origin is a conditional origin of replication from plasmid R6K that requires the trans-acting π (pi) initiator protein, encoded by the pir gene, to replicate. It is widely used in suicide vectors, transposon delivery systems, and Gateway cloning entry vectors because plasmids bearing this origin are stably maintained only in pir-expressing E. coli strains and lost upon transfer into wild-type hosts. This conditional replication makes it invaluable for allelic exchange, chromosomal integration, and other genetic manipulation strategies.
mechanism: The π protein (pir gene product) binds seven direct repeat (iteron) sequences within the γ origin and recruits host replication machinery; without π, replication initiation cannot occur. Copy number is ~15 per cell with wild-type pir and ~250 per cell with the relaxed pir116 allele, enabling both stringent conditional maintenance and high-copy amplification.
known_variants: pir116-dependent high-copy variant (~250 copies/cell vs ~15 with wild-type pir)
Minimal γ origin (~500 bp core iteron region) vs extended versions with flanking regulatory sequences
α origin and β origin of R6K (less commonly used; all three are present in the native R6K plasmid)
known_misannotations: Often annotated simply as 'R6K ori' without specifying which of the three R6K origins (α, β, or γ) is present — the γ origin is almost always the one used in molecular biology tools
Occasionally mislabeled as a ColE1-type origin in databases due to similar plasmid copy number range
γ/gamma notation inconsistency: 'R6Kγ', 'R6K-gamma', 'oriγ', and 'ori R6K' all refer to the same element
notes: Functional replication strictly requires a pir+ host strain (e.g., BW23473, BW23474, PIR1, PIR2, or λpir lysogens). Plasmids with R6K ori are used as suicide vectors in wild-type E. coli and many other gram-negative bacteria: integration or loss of the plasmid is selected by antibiotic markers. The pir116 allele enables ~250-copy amplification, useful for large-scale plasmid prep of entry clones. R6K ori is the basis of the Invitrogen Gateway att-site entry vector system (pDONR series).

description: The f1 ori is the origin of replication derived from filamentous bacteriophage f1 (an Ff-family phage). When incorporated into a plasmid (creating a phagemid), it enables production of single-stranded circular DNA upon superinfection of host E. coli with f1 or M13 helper phage. This ssDNA is packaged into phage particles secreted into the culture medium, making it valuable for site-directed mutagenesis, phage display, and sequencing template preparation.
mechanism: The f1 ori contains a (+) strand origin and a (−) strand origin; upon helper phage superinfection, the phage-encoded gene II protein nicks the (+) strand at the (+) strand origin, initiating rolling-circle replication to produce ssDNA circles that are coated and secreted as phage particles. The orientation of the f1 ori in the plasmid determines which strand of the insert is packaged.
known_variants: Minimal f1 ori (~454 bp): trimmed to the smallest functional replication signal
Inverted orientation: determines strand polarity packaged into phage particles
M13 ori: functionally interchangeable; from closely related phage M13 rather than f1
known_misannotations: Frequently labeled 'M13 ori' or 'M13/f1 ori' interchangeably, though f1 and M13 are distinct phages with highly similar but not identical ori sequences
The (+) strand origin and (−) strand origin sub-elements are sometimes annotated as separate features or conflated into a single undifferentiated region
Orientation is often omitted from annotations despite being functionally critical for determining which plasmid strand is packaged
notes: The f1 ori is non-functional for ssDNA production without helper phage (e.g., M13K07, VCSM13, or R408); it has no effect on plasmid copy number or replication in the absence of helper phage infection. The host must be an F-pilus-bearing E. coli strain (F+ or Hfr) for helper phage infection to occur. Phagemids containing f1 ori are maintained as normal double-stranded plasmids under standard growth conditions.

description: The RSF1010 origin of replication derives from RSF1010, a naturally occurring IncQ (IncP-4) broad-host-range plasmid that replicates autonomously across a remarkably wide range of gram-negative bacteria. The functional replicon encompasses the vegetative origin (oriV), which contains iteron sequences, and three plasmid-encoded replication proteins (RepA helicase, RepB primase, RepC initiator) that together substitute for host replication factors. It is extensively used in broad-host-range expression vectors for genetic engineering of Pseudomonas, Agrobacterium, Sinorhizobium, Burkholderia, and other gram-negative organisms where ColE1-derived origins fail to replicate.
mechanism: RepC binds iterons within oriV to initiate strand separation; RepA (helicase) unwinds the duplex and RepB (primase) synthesizes RNA primers, allowing host DNA polymerase III to extend; because all three Rep proteins are plasmid-encoded, RSF1010 is host-range-independent and does not require host-specific DnaA or primase.
known_variants: oriV-only fragment (without rep genes; requires trans-complementation by RepA/B/C supplied in cis on helper or chromosome)
High-copy mutant oriV with altered iteron spacing affecting copy number
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'IncQ ori' without clarifying whether the annotation covers only oriV (~450 bp) or the full minimal replicon including repA/B/C (~3.5–4.5 kb), leading to inconsistent feature lengths across databases
Confused with pBBR1 ori, another broad-host-range gram-negative origin; they are unrelated replicons with distinct incompatibility groups and Rep proteins
Rep genes (repA, repB, repC) sometimes collapsed into the ori annotation and sometimes annotated as separate CDS features, making automated detection unreliable
Occasionally mislabeled as 'IncP ori' or 'RK2 ori'; RSF1010 is IncQ, not IncP
notes: RSF1010 maintains a relatively high copy number (~10–30 copies/cell) compared to RK2/IncP vectors (~5 copies). The plasmid is mobilizable but not self-transmissible; it can be mobilized by IncP plasmids (e.g., RK2013 helper). The broad host range makes it valuable for species refractory to ColE1 or p15A replicons, but incompatibility must be checked when co-transforming with other IncQ vectors. The oriV alone (without rep genes on the same molecule) is non-functional unless Rep proteins are supplied in trans.

description: oriT (origin of transfer) is the cis-acting DNA sequence required to initiate conjugative plasmid transfer between bacterial cells. The relaxase (Mob protein) nicks one strand at the nic site within oriT and pilots the linearized strand through a type IV secretion system into the recipient cell. In plasmid engineering, inserting oriT makes a non-conjugative plasmid mobilizable by a conjugative helper plasmid supplied in trans, enabling DNA delivery between strains without carrying the full conjugation gene complement.
mechanism: The relaxase binds oriT and introduces a site-specific nick at the nic site, covalently attaching to the 5′ end of the transferred strand. The resulting nucleoprotein relaxosome complex is recruited to a mating-pair formation channel, and the single-stranded DNA is unwound and translocated into the recipient cell where the complementary strand is resynthesized.
known_variants: RP4/RK2 oriT (IncP-1, ~250 bp minimal, broad-host-range, most widely used in tri-parental mating)
F factor oriT (IncF, ~500 bp, encodes an elaborate relaxosome; present in F-based BAC vectors)
ColE1/pMB1 oriT — called 'bom' (basis of mobilization) in pBR322/pUC derivatives; mobilized by RP4 Mob in trans
RSF1010 oriT (IncQ, self-mobilizable, functional across diverse Gram-negative genera)
R6K oriT (IncX, requires π protein; used in suicide-vector delivery systems)
known_misannotations: Routinely confused with 'ori' (origin of replication) — oriT has no role in autonomous DNA replication
The 'bom' sequence in pBR322/pUC plasmids is the ColE1 oriT homolog but is often annotated simply as 'oriT' without specifying incompatibility group or that it requires RP4 Mob functions in trans
Some annotations label the entire mob gene plus flanking oriT region as 'oriT'; strictly, oriT is only the cis-acting sequence — the mob gene is a separate feature
RP4 and RK2 oriT are used interchangeably in annotations because RP4 and RK2 are the same plasmid isolated in different laboratories
notes: Despite nominal similarity to 'ori', oriT is an origin of transfer, not replication, and its absence or presence has no effect on copy number. oriT function is incompatibility-group-specific: an RP4 oriT requires RP4-encoded Mob and Tra functions (or a helper like pRK2013), while RSF1010 oriT requires RSF1010 Mob proteins. The minimal RP4 oriT (~250 bp) is routinely inserted into cargo plasmids for tri-parental mating into Gram-negative organisms such as Agrobacterium, Pseudomonas, and Rhizobium. Many common cloning vectors (pBR322, pUC series) retain a vestigial bom/oriT from their ColE1 ancestry and can be mobilized at low frequency even without an intentionally added oriT.

description: The origin of replication derived from the endogenous Saccharomyces cerevisiae 2μ plasmid, a ~6.3 kb circular DNA element maintained at 40–60 copies per cell in most laboratory yeast strains. It enables episomal, high-copy-number replication in yeast and is the standard origin used in yeast shuttle vectors when high-level protein expression or gene dosage is required.
mechanism: Replication is initiated by the yeast Origin Recognition Complex (ORC) binding the ORI region of the 2μ plasmid. Stable segregation requires the plasmid-encoded REP1 and REP2 proteins, which act in trans to tether plasmid copies to chromosomes through the STB (stability locus) during mitotic division; without REP1/REP2, plasmid loss frequency rises dramatically.
known_variants: Minimal ORI fragment (~200–300 bp) lacking the STB locus — functional only in cir+ host strains that supply REP1/REP2 in trans from the endogenous 2μ plasmid
Extended fragment including flanking FLP recombinase recognition sites (FRT), enabling copy-number amplification via site-specific recombination
Synthetic codon-context-adjusted versions used in some optimized 2μ-based expression systems
known_misannotations: Confused with yeast chromosomal ARS elements (autonomously replicating sequences), which are structurally and mechanistically distinct and confer low copy number (~1–2 copies)
Some vectors annotate the entire 2μ plasmid insert rather than just the minimal ORI region, inflating the apparent feature size
Occasionally labeled '2μ ARS' despite the 2μ origin depending on REP/STB machinery absent from chromosomal ARS-based origins
Vectors carrying '2μ' in their name (e.g., some pUC-based hybrids) are sometimes mistaken for containing a functional 2μ ori
notes: Requires the host yeast strain to be cir+ (carrying the endogenous 2μ plasmid) for high-copy stable maintenance, since REP1 and REP2 are encoded on the endogenous plasmid; use in cir0 strains results in high plasmid loss unless REP1/REP2 are supplied in trans. Confers ~40–100 copies per cell under standard conditions, distinctly higher than ARS/CEN vectors (~1–2 copies) and integrating vectors (1 copy). Functional in Saccharomyces cerevisiae and closely related Saccharomyces species; generally non-functional in Pichia pastoris, Kluyveromyces lactis, or other non-Saccharomyces yeasts without species-specific modifications.

description: ARS1 is the first characterized autonomously replicating sequence from Saccharomyces cerevisiae, located on chromosome IV near the TRP1 locus. It enables episomal replication of plasmid DNA in yeast and is widely incorporated into yeast shuttle vectors for cloning, expression, and genetic manipulation. When combined with a centromere element (CEN), ARS1-containing plasmids replicate stably at 1–5 copies per cell; without CEN, they replicate at high copy but are mitotically unstable.
mechanism: ARS1 contains an 11-bp AT-rich ARS consensus sequence (ACS) that serves as the binding site for the hexameric Origin Recognition Complex (ORC), which recruits CDC6, CDT1, and the MCM2–7 helicase to license the origin for replication initiation. Flanking B-domain elements (B1, B2, B3) enhance ORC binding affinity and recruit additional replication factors including Abf1p (B3 domain), collectively ensuring efficient origin firing in S phase.
known_variants: Minimal ARS1 (core A-domain + B1 subdomain only, ~150 bp): functional but reduced efficiency
ARS1-ΔACS: ACS point-mutant used as replication-deficient negative control in replication assays
Extended TRP1-ARS1 fragment (~1.45 kb): includes full TRP1 selectable marker co-cloned with ARS1
known_misannotations: Vectors annotated 'ARS1' may actually carry ARSH4 (chromosome IV HindIII fragment ARS) or ARS209 — the names are used interchangeably in older literature
The generic term 'ARS' in vector maps sometimes refers to the 2-micron circle ORI, not ARS1
ARS1 is occasionally mislabeled 'CEN/ARS' when a centromere element is absent from the construct
notes: ARS1 is strictly a Saccharomyces cerevisiae replication origin and does not function in E. coli or other non-yeast hosts; shuttle vectors require a separate bacterial origin (e.g., pMB1/ColE1). ARS1 alone confers high-copy but highly unstable plasmid maintenance (~10–100 copies/cell, ~50% plasmid loss per generation without selection); pairing with CEN4 (YCp50) or CEN6 (pRS series) gives stable 1–5 copy plasmids. The A-domain ACS is essential; B-domain deletions reduce but do not abolish replication. ARS1 is the canonical model for eukaryotic replication origin studies.

description: The origin of DNA replication from Simian Virus 40 (SV40), a polyomavirus. When present in a plasmid, it enables autonomous episomal replication in mammalian cells that express the SV40 Large T antigen, dramatically amplifying plasmid copy number and boosting transient transgene expression. It is a standard component of high-level mammalian transient expression vectors.
mechanism: The SV40 Large T antigen (TAg) binds specifically to the pentanucleotide repeats within the ori core, unwinds the DNA duplex using its helicase activity, and recruits host replication machinery (RPA, DNA polymerase α-primase, topoisomerases) to initiate bidirectional DNA replication. Replication proceeds autonomously without chromosomal integration in TAg-expressing cells.
known_variants: Minimal SV40 ori (~65 bp core pentanucleotide repeat region only; sufficient for TAg binding but lower efficiency)
Extended SV40 ori including the early promoter region (~500 bp; sometimes co-annotated with the SV40 promoter)
known_misannotations: Frequently confused with the SV40 polyadenylation signal, which is a distinct ~250 bp element located elsewhere in the SV40 genome
The full SV40 early region (promoter + ori) is sometimes annotated as 'SV40 ori' even though the promoter activity is a separate function
Some vectors label it 'SV40 ori/promoter' when the annotation actually spans both elements
notes: Functional only in mammalian cells expressing SV40 Large T antigen; the most common host cell lines are COS-1, COS-7 (African green monkey kidney, constitutively express TAg), and HEK293T (human embryonic kidney, stably transfected with TAg). Absence of TAg (e.g., CHO, HeLa) renders the ori non-functional and the plasmid replicates only at one copy per cell division. The SV40 ori is present in many widely used vectors primarily for use in COS and 293T transient expression systems. It is not functional in E. coli, yeast, or insect cells.

aliases: Epstein-Barr virus origin of plasmid replication
description: EBV oriP is the latent origin of DNA replication from Epstein-Barr virus (Human herpesvirus 4), enabling episomal replication of plasmids in mammalian cells when the viral EBNA-1 protein is supplied in cis or trans. It is widely used in episomal expression vectors to maintain plasmids extrachromosomally through cell division without chromosomal integration, supporting both transient and long-term stable expression. Because it replicates once per cell cycle and segregates with host chromosomes via EBNA-1 tethering, oriP vectors provide low-copy episomal persistence with minimal genotoxic risk.
mechanism: oriP consists of two functional elements: the Family of Repeats (FR, ~20 tandem 30-bp EBNA-1 binding sites) and the Dyad Symmetry element (DS, ~180 bp with four EBNA-1 binding sites that serve as the replication initiation site). EBNA-1 binds both elements — initiating replication at DS and tethering the episome to host chromosomes via FR to ensure mitotic segregation.
known_variants: Minimal oriP (DS element only): supports replication initiation but lacks FR-mediated nuclear retention and segregation, resulting in gradual plasmid loss
Synthetic oriP with reduced FR repeat number (e.g., 6–8 repeats): lower EBNA-1 transcriptional silencing while retaining episomal maintenance
oriP from EBV type 2 strains: functionally equivalent but with minor sequence variation in repeat units
known_misannotations: Annotated as simply 'oriP' without EBV qualifier, causing confusion with other viral or bacterial oriP sequences
FR and DS sub-elements sometimes annotated as separate features rather than as components of a single oriP region
Occasionally mislabeled as SV40 ori when both are present in a vector
Some annotations mark only the DS element (~180 bp) as oriP, omitting the FR region required for stable maintenance
notes: Functional oriP vectors require co-expression of EBNA-1 (either encoded on the same plasmid or in a stable EBNA-1-expressing host cell line such as 293-EBNA or HEK293E). oriP confers only mammalian episomal replication; the vector typically also carries a bacterial ori (e.g., pUC ori) and antibiotic resistance for propagation in E. coli. Maintained at ~5–20 copies per cell under hygromycin or G418 selection. oriP-based vectors are preferred for iPSC reprogramming and CAR-T cell engineering because they are non-integrating.

description: EBNA1 is the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) nuclear antigen required for episomal plasmid replication and maintenance in latently infected mammalian cells. In plasmid engineering, EBNA1 is co-expressed with the EBV origin of plasmid replication (oriP) to create self-replicating, non-integrating episomal vectors that persist through cell division without chromosomal integration. This system is widely used for stable transgene expression in human and primate cells and for generating integration-free iPSCs.
mechanism: EBNA1 is a sequence-specific DNA-binding protein that binds directly to the family of repeats (FR) and dyad symmetry (DS) elements within oriP; the DS element acts as the replication origin while the FR element tethers replicated episomes to host chromosomes during mitosis via interaction with cellular chromatin-associated proteins, ensuring faithful episome segregation.
known_variants: Gly-Ala repeat-deleted EBNA1 (reduces MHC class I antigen presentation, used for immunological studies)
Codon-optimized EBNA1 for enhanced mammalian expression
Type 1 vs. Type 2 EBV natural sequence variants (differ in C-terminal domain)
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'EBNA' without the '1' designation, causing confusion with other EBV nuclear antigens (EBNA2, EBNA3A/3B/3C, EBNA-LP)
The oriP element is occasionally mislabeled as 'EBNA1' in older vector maps
Some vector maps label the entire EBNA1 expression cassette (promoter + CDS + polyA) as 'EBNA1', inflating apparent feature size
notes: EBNA1 functions only in human and other primate cell lines that support EBV latency (e.g., HEK293, HeLa, Vero). The Gly-Ala repeat domain varies in copy number between EBV strains, causing the coding sequence length to range significantly (~1.9–2.1 kb). Episomal vectors relying on EBNA1/oriP replicate at low copy number (~5–20 copies/cell) and are gradually lost in the absence of selection pressure. Biosafety consideration: EBNA1 expression is associated with EBV-driven oncogenesis; episomal vectors typically require BSL-2 handling. Not functional in non-primate mammalian cells or lower eukaryotes.

description: The T7 promoter is a ~17–23 bp cis-regulatory element derived from bacteriophage T7 that is exclusively recognized by T7 RNA polymerase (T7 RNAP), not by host bacterial RNA polymerases. It is the core driver of the widely used T7 expression system (pET vector series), enabling tightly controlled, extremely high-level recombinant protein production in E. coli strains harboring a chromosomal or plasmid-encoded copy of the T7 RNAP gene (e.g., BL21(DE3)). Its orthogonality to the host transcriptional machinery allows near-complete redirection of cellular resources toward target gene expression upon induction.
mechanism: T7 RNAP binds the conserved 23 bp promoter consensus (positions −17 to +6 relative to the transcription start site, core sequence TAATACGACTCACTATA) with high specificity and initiates transcription approximately 5-fold faster and more processively than E. coli RNAP, producing large amounts of transcript from even a single promoter copy.
known_variants: T7 phi10 (class III, strongest; used in most pET vectors)
T7 phi6.5 (class III, slightly weaker than phi10)
T7 A1 promoter (class III, strong; used in some TXTL systems)
T7 phi2.5 (class II, intermediate strength)
T7 phi1.1 (class I, weak; used for balanced expression)
Mutant T7 promoters with single-base substitutions for tunable output
Synthetic consensus T7 promoter (all-consensus positions, slightly stronger than phi10)
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'T7' without specifying 'promoter', causing confusion with T7 terminator or T7 tag features
Confused with T3 promoter (SP6 family; orthogonal specificity, different sequence) — both used in in vitro transcription but not interchangeable
Annotation boundaries vary widely: some include only the −17 to +1 core (17 bp), others extend to +6 or include flanking vector sequence (up to 50 bp)
Sometimes labeled 'T7 promoter 10' conflating the promoter name with the downstream gene 10 coding sequence
Mislabeled as 'T7lac promoter' when the lacO operator is absent — pET vectors with the lac operator insert should be annotated as 'T7lac promoter'
notes: Functional only in host cells or cell-free systems supplying T7 RNAP; completely orthogonal to E. coli σ70 RNAP. The lac operator (lacO) is often placed immediately downstream (creating the 'T7lac' configuration) to reduce basal leaky expression in DE3 strains. In vitro transcription kits (e.g., for mRNA synthesis, TXTL) rely heavily on this promoter. The phi10 variant accounts for the vast majority of pET-series annotations encountered in deposited plasmid records.

description: The T3 promoter is a strong bacteriophage T3-derived promoter recognized exclusively by T3 RNA polymerase (T3 RNAP), a single-subunit phage-encoded enzyme with no host factor requirements. It is widely used in cloning vectors flanking the multiple cloning site to enable directional in vitro transcription of insert sequences into RNA. In many vectors it is paired with T7 or SP6 promoters on the opposite strand, allowing synthesis of sense or antisense transcripts from the same template.
mechanism: T3 RNA polymerase binds directly and specifically to the ~17–20 bp T3 promoter consensus sequence (5′-AATTAACCCTCACTAAAGGG-3′) and initiates highly processive transcription without requiring any host sigma factors or additional transcription machinery. Promoter specificity is absolute — T3 RNAP does not recognize T7 or SP6 promoters and vice versa.
known_variants: Minimal T3 promoter (17 bp core only, positions -17 to +1)
Extended T3 promoter with upstream stabilizing sequence for improved in vitro yield
known_misannotations: Confused with T7 promoter in vectors carrying both promoters flanking the MCS — orientation errors are common
Mislabeled as SP6 promoter in older Stratagene vector annotations
Annotated simply as 'phage promoter' without specifying T3 specificity
Entire T3 RNAP recognition region sometimes annotated as 'T3' without distinguishing core promoter from upstream elements
notes: The T3 promoter is primarily used for in vitro transcription rather than in vivo expression; functional use requires exogenous T3 RNA polymerase (available commercially). In vivo use in E. coli requires co-expression of T3 RNAP from a separate construct (analogous to T7 Express systems). T3 and T7 RNAP share ~80% amino acid identity but have strict, non-overlapping promoter specificities, making T3/T7 promoter pairs useful for independent bidirectional transcription control. The T3 system is less commonly used for in vivo expression than T7 and has fewer commercial host strains optimized for it.

description: The SP6 promoter is the ~18 bp recognition and initiation sequence (consensus: ATTTAGGTGACACTATAG) for the RNA polymerase of Salmonella bacteriophage SP6. It is incorporated into cloning vectors alongside T7 and T3 promoters to enable strand-specific in vitro transcription of cloned inserts for RNA probe synthesis, cRNA generation, ribozyme studies, and mRNA production. Its high specificity for the cognate single-subunit SP6 RNAP makes it orthogonal to host bacterial transcription machinery.
mechanism: SP6 RNA polymerase, a single-subunit enzyme (~96 kDa) encoded by Salmonella phage SP6, binds the ~18 bp promoter with high specificity and initiates transcription at the downstream guanosine residue (+1) without requiring accessory sigma factors or additional initiation proteins, producing high-yield run-off RNA transcripts in a highly processive manner.
known_variants: Extended −30 to +6 sequence used in some vector designs for enhanced polymerase recruitment
Minimal 18 bp core promoter (ATTTAGGTGACACTATAG) as used in most commercial cloning vectors
Mutant SP6 promoters with altered −17 to −10 region used in promoter-strength studies
known_misannotations: Confused with T3 or T7 promoters in bidirectional multi-cloning-site vectors when strand orientation is not specified
Annotated simply as 'SP6' in flat-file GenBank records without specifying promoter type, leading to ambiguity with the phage itself
Reverse-complement strand incorrectly annotated as the active SP6 promoter in some deposited sequences
Mislabeled as a bacterial sigma-70 promoter in automated annotation pipelines due to AT-rich sequence features
notes: SP6 promoter is recognized exclusively by SP6 RNA polymerase and is not a functional promoter for E. coli σ70 holoenzyme or eukaryotic RNA polymerases; it confers no transcriptional activity in cells unless SP6 RNAP is co-expressed. Its primary application is in vitro transcription using commercially purified SP6 RNAP. Most dual-promoter vectors (e.g., pGEM series) position SP6 and T7 promoters on opposite flanks of the multiple cloning site, permitting synthesis of sense or antisense RNA from the same construct. The promoter core is unusually AT-rich (~33% GC), which distinguishes it from T7 (~46% GC) and T3 (~44% GC) promoter sequences.

description: The tac promoter is a synthetic hybrid bacterial promoter created by fusing the -35 region of the E. coli trp promoter with the -10 region and lac operator of the lac promoter, yielding a sequence 3–5× stronger than the native lacUV5 promoter. It is one of the most widely used inducible promoters for high-level recombinant protein expression in E. coli. Transcription is tightly repressed by the LacI repressor and induced by IPTG.
mechanism: RNA polymerase sigma70 recognizes the consensus -35 hexamer (TTGACA, derived from trp) and -10 hexamer (TATAAT, derived from lac) separated by an optimal 17 bp spacer. The downstream lac operator (lacO) retains LacI repressor binding; addition of the gratuitous inducer IPTG dissociates LacI and permits constitutive high-level transcription initiation.
known_variants: tacII — alternative hybrid variant described by Amann et al. 1983 with slightly different spacer
double-lac-operator tac — contains two lacO sites for tighter repression and reduced leaky expression
tac2 — tandem duplicate used in some vectors to further boost transcript levels
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'lac promoter' when the downstream lac operator is annotated separately and the -35 trp origin is overlooked
Confused with trp promoter in older pre-1983 expression constructs that used the native trp P1P2 promoter region
Occasionally mislabeled 'T7 promoter' in mixed expression vectors where both promoter types are present
notes: Designed independently by de Boer et al. and Amann et al. (both 1983). Functional only in bacteria; requires a source of LacI (chromosomal lacI in most E. coli lab strains, or a plasmid-borne lacIq allele) to prevent toxic leaky expression of recombinant proteins. The pGEX GST-fusion and pMAL MBP-fusion systems both rely on the tac promoter. Not active in eukaryotic cells.

description: The trc promoter is a synthetic hybrid promoter combining the consensus -35 element of the E. coli trp promoter (TTGACA) with the -10 element of the lacUV5 promoter (TATAAT), producing one of the strongest IPTG-inducible promoters available for bacterial expression. It is regulated by the lac repressor via a downstream lac operator, enabling tight transcriptional control. It is widely used in high-level recombinant protein production vectors such as pKK223-3 and pTrc99A.
mechanism: RNA polymerase sigma-70 binds efficiently to the near-consensus -35 and -10 elements, driving high-level transcription initiation; the lac repressor binds the operator immediately downstream of the transcription start site to block elongation in the uninduced state. Addition of IPTG causes allosteric dissociation of the repressor from the operator, allowing full induction.
known_variants: tac promoter — closely related hybrid (trp -35 / lacUV5 -10) with slightly different inter-element spacer; often used interchangeably with trc in literature
Double-operator trc — some vectors insert two lac operators (O1 and O3) flanking the promoter for tighter repression and lower leaky expression
known_misannotations: Frequently mislabeled as the tac promoter; trc and tac share the same hybrid origin but differ in spacer sequence and are distinct promoters
Sometimes annotated simply as 'Plac' or 'lacUV5' because both are IPTG-inducible, conflating unrelated promoter architectures
Annotated as 'trp promoter' in older vector maps that highlight only the -35 element origin
notes: Requires co-expression of the lac repressor (lacI or the overexpressing allele lacIq) for inducible control; in lacI-negative strains the promoter drives constitutive high-level transcription that can be toxic to the host. The trc and tac promoters are functionally nearly equivalent and the names are frequently conflated in plasmid annotations and commercial vector documentation. pTrc99A (Pharmacia/Amersham Biosciences) is the canonical trc expression vector, carrying a multiple cloning site and rrnB transcriptional terminator.

description: The lac promoter drives transcription of the E. coli lac operon, which encodes the lactose utilization enzymes LacZ (β-galactosidase), LacY (permease), and LacA (transacetylase). It is one of the most widely used inducible promoters in prokaryotic expression vectors, enabling controlled gene expression through addition of the gratuitous inducer IPTG. Its well-characterized regulation, moderate strength, and tight repression in the uninduced state make it a standard tool for recombinant protein production in E. coli.
mechanism: The lac repressor (LacI), a homotetrameric protein, binds to operator sequences (lacO1, lacO2, lacO3) that flank the promoter, physically blocking RNA polymerase elongation. IPTG binds LacI and induces an allosteric conformational change that releases the repressor from the operator, permitting transcription; full activation also requires binding of the catabolite activator protein (CAP)–cAMP complex to an upstream site, making wild-type Plac subject to catabolite repression.
known_variants: lacUV5 — two base substitutions that eliminate CAP dependency and increase basal strength; used in most pUC vectors
tac promoter — synthetic -35 from trpP, -10 from lacP, stronger than either parent
trc promoter — closely related trp-lac hybrid, slightly different spacer length
lacI^q — promoter-up mutation for lacI overexpression, not Plac itself but commonly co-annotated
known_misannotations: lacUV5 frequently labeled simply as 'lac promoter' even though it differs at two positions and loses CAP dependence
Annotations sometimes span only the -35/-10 core (~50 bp) or extend through the operator into lacZ, creating inconsistent length annotations
'lac regulatory region' or 'lac control region' sometimes collapsed to 'lac promoter' in vector maps
tac or trc promoters occasionally mislabeled as lac promoter in commercial vector annotations
notes: The wild-type lac promoter requires catabolite repression relief (low glucose / high cAMP) for full induction, making it less suitable for cultures in rich media.
