The lacUV5 variant used in pUC and many T7-based vectors (pET series combined with T7lac) is CAP-independent and should be distinguished. In practice, 'lac promoter' in a plasmid context almost always refers to lacUV5 or a hybrid derivative. IPTG induction is dose-dependent; typical working concentrations are 0.1–1 mM.

description: The lac UV5 promoter is a mutant derivative of the E. coli lac promoter carrying two point mutations in the -10 hexamer (converting TATGTTG to the consensus TATAAT), which simultaneously strengthens the promoter and renders it independent of catabolite activator protein (CAP/CRP) activation. It drives robust, IPTG-inducible transcription in E. coli without requiring glucose depletion, making it far more predictable than the wild-type lac promoter in rich media. It is most consequentially used as the regulatory switch controlling chromosomally integrated T7 RNA polymerase in BL21(DE3) strains, thereby underpinning the entire T7/pET expression platform.
mechanism: Sigma-70-holoenzyme RNA polymerase recognizes the -35 (TTGACA) and -10 (TATAAT) hexamers; the UV5 -10 mutation matches the consensus Pribnow box, eliminating the requirement for CAP-cAMP to stabilize the open complex. Transcription is repressed by LacI binding the lac operator; addition of IPTG (a non-metabolizable allolactose analog) induces a conformational change in LacI that abolishes operator binding, allowing constitutive-level transcription.
known_variants: Wild-type lac promoter (lacks UV5 mutations; requires CAP-cAMP for full activity; weaker in rich media)
tac promoter (hybrid: trp -35 box + lacUV5 -10 box; ~5-fold stronger than lacUV5)
trc promoter (similar hybrid to tac; used in pTrc99A)
lacUV5 with synthetic operator arrays (O1/O2/O3 combinations for tighter LacI repression)
known_misannotations: Frequently annotated as simply 'lac promoter', obscuring whether the CAP-independence UV5 mutations are present
Confused with the tac promoter, which differs at the -35 hexamer and is significantly stronger
Operator region (lacO) immediately downstream sometimes included in the annotated promoter span, giving inconsistent length annotations
In pUC vectors the lacUV5-lacZ region is sometimes labeled 'MCS' or 'lacZ-alpha', burying the promoter annotation
notes: The UV5 designation refers to a UV-induced mutant isolated in the 1970s by Gralla and colleagues; the two critical transitions are at positions -12 (A→T) and -7 (G→T) in the non-template strand. lacUV5 is functional only in hosts with a σ70-family RNA polymerase; LacI must also be present for regulation (many expression strains carry a lacI^q allele for overproduction of repressor). The promoter's strength is moderate compared to strong constitutive promoters; its main virtue is tight, inducer-responsive control rather than maximal output.

description: The araBAD promoter (PBAD) drives expression of the araBAD operon in E. coli, encoding L-arabinose catabolic enzymes (L-ribulokinase, L-arabinose isomerase, L-ribulose-5-phosphate-4-epimerase). In plasmid engineering it is extensively used as a tightly regulated, arabinose-inducible expression system with exceptionally low basal activity in the absence of inducer, making it suitable for expressing toxic or growth-inhibitory proteins.
mechanism: Regulated by the bifunctional AraC transcription factor, which represses transcription via DNA looping between O2 and I1 operators in the absence of L-arabinose, and activates transcription upon arabinose binding by repositioning to the adjacent I1 and I2 half-sites. Full activation additionally requires cAMP-CAP (catabolite activator protein) binding at a site upstream of the -35 element, coupling expression to carbon source availability via catabolite repression.
known_variants: Truncated minimal promoter (~150 bp): retains core -10/-35 elements but lacks distal regulatory sequences, reducing AraC-mediated DNA looping
pBAD/araC cassette: includes the araC gene immediately upstream to make the induction system self-contained, independent of host chromosome araC
CAP-independent variant: mutations in the CAP-binding site decouple expression from catabolite repression, enabling arabinose induction in glucose-containing media
known_misannotations: Annotated simply as 'PBAD' without the 'araBAD' qualifier, which can be confused with any BAD-locus element
Confused with the divergent araC promoter (PC), which drives araC expression from the same chromosomal locus in the opposite direction
Labeled 'ara promoter' ambiguously — the ara regulon contains multiple promoters (araBAD, araC, araE, araFGH)
Annotated as covering only the minimal ~60 bp core when the full regulatory region including AraC-binding operators is required for tight repression
notes: In wild-type E. coli strains arabinose transport is self-reinforcing through positive feedback, producing a bistable all-or-none induction response at the single-cell level rather than a graded population response. True analog titration of expression level requires strains with constitutive arabinose uptake (e.g., araE overexpression or araFGH deletions). Compared to lac-based systems, basal (uninduced) leakiness is very low, making PBAD preferred for toxic-gene expression. The araC regulator is typically provided either by the host chromosome or co-encoded on the same vector.

description: The trp promoter is the native regulatory region driving the E. coli tryptophan biosynthesis operon (trpEDCBA). It is a strong bacterial promoter that is repressed by tryptophan and derepressed under tryptophan starvation or by the synthetic analog indoleacrylic acid (IAA), making it useful for tightly regulated recombinant protein expression in E. coli. It was one of the first promoters exploited for industrial-scale recombinant protein production, including early biosynthetic human insulin and growth hormone.
mechanism: The TrpR repressor protein binds tryptophan as a co-repressor; the resulting TrpR–Trp complex occupies the trp operator (trpO), which overlaps the promoter, sterically blocking RNA polymerase binding. Depletion of intracellular tryptophan or addition of IAA causes repressor dissociation, allowing transcription to initiate.
known_variants: tac promoter — hybrid trp −35 / lac UV5 −10 box; stronger and IPTG-inducible
trc promoter — trp −35 / lac −10 hybrid, slightly different spacing from tac
trp promoter with attenuator deleted — removes transcriptional attenuation for higher basal output
known_misannotations: Confused with the tac promoter, which is a chimeric trp/lac construct and not the native trp promoter
Operator region (trpO) sometimes included or excluded inconsistently, yielding different annotated lengths across databases
Occasionally mislabeled as 'trpL promoter' when the leader peptide region is included in the cloned fragment
notes: Induction is typically achieved by tryptophan starvation (tryptophan auxotrophic host on minimal medium) or by adding indoleacrylic acid (IAA, ~25 µg/mL) to rich medium. Leaky expression in tryptophan-rich media limits its use when tight repression is critical. The trp promoter is weaker than the T7 promoter system for high-level expression but provides moderate, controllable output without requiring phage RNA polymerase.

description: The rrnB P1 promoter is the primary (upstream) promoter of the E. coli rrnB ribosomal RNA operon, one of seven rrn operons encoding 16S, 23S, and 5S rRNA. It is among the strongest σ70-dependent promoters in E. coli and is used in plasmid engineering as a strong constitutive-like promoter or as a reference standard for measuring transcriptional activity. During exponential growth it drives very high-level transcription, making it valuable as a benchmark and as a driver of genes whose basal expression must be high.
mechanism: Transcription initiation depends on σ70-RNAP binding to consensus -35 (TTGTCA) and -10 (TATAAT) elements, potently stimulated by an AT-rich UP element upstream of -35 that contacts the α-CTD subunits of RNA polymerase. The Fis nucleoid-associated protein binds three sites in the UP element region and further activates P1; during amino acid starvation the alarmone ppGpp inhibits P1 by destabilizing the open complex, linking rRNA synthesis to nutrient availability via the stringent response.
reference_plasmids: pGEM series derivatives with rrnB promoter–lacZ fusions
known_variants: rrnB P1 ΔUP: deletion of the UP element (−59 to −38), reduces activity ~3–5 fold
rrnB P1 Δfis: point mutations in Fis-binding sites I/II/III, abolishes Fis stimulation
rrnB P1 core (−41 to +1): minimal promoter retaining −35/−10 elements only
rrnB P2: closely related downstream promoter of same operon, lower basal activity, ppGpp-insensitive
known_misannotations: Confused with rrnB P2 when the cloned fragment includes both promoters (P1 starts ~120 bp upstream of P2)
Annotated simply as 'rrn promoter' without specifying P1 vs P2 or which rrn operon (rrnA–rrnH)
Sometimes mislabeled 'rRNA promoter' conflating it with eukaryotic Pol I rRNA promoters
Fragments that include only the −41 to +1 core annotated as full P1, omitting UP element contribution
notes: rrnB P1 activity is tightly coupled to growth rate and ppGpp levels, so expression driven by this promoter will drop dramatically during stationary phase or nutrient stress — unlike synthetic constitutive promoters (e.g., J23 series). For stable high-level constitutive expression in E. coli, synthetic promoters are usually preferred. The rrnB terminators T1 and T2 (located downstream in the same operon) are far more widely deployed in expression vectors than the P1 promoter itself, and the two elements are often confused in annotations.

description: EM7 is a short synthetic constitutive promoter that drives gene expression in E. coli and other gram-negative bacteria. It is most commonly used in dual-purpose expression vectors (e.g., Invitrogen/Thermo Fisher series) to provide constitutive bacterial expression of an antibiotic resistance marker—such as Zeocin (Sh ble) or neomycin (nptII) resistance—enabling selection of transformed bacteria during cloning, while a separate eukaryotic promoter drives transgene expression in mammalian or other cells. Because it is constitutive and sigma-70-dependent, it functions across a broad range of growth conditions without inducers.
mechanism: EM7 contains synthetic consensus -10 (TATAAT) and -35 (TTGACA) hexamer elements recognized by the E. coli housekeeping sigma factor σ70 (RpoD), enabling constitutive transcription initiation by the host RNA polymerase holoenzyme without the need for activator proteins or small-molecule inducers.
known_variants: Truncated core variant (~37 bp) retaining only the -10/-35 elements, used in some minimal-backbone vectors
Extended variant (~100 bp) with upstream AT-rich spacer included in certain Invitrogen destination vectors
known_misannotations: Boundary coordinates vary by ±15–25 bp across vector annotations depending on whether the upstream spacer and transcription start site are included
Occasionally mislabeled as a T7 promoter or generic 'bacterial promoter' in automated annotation pipelines that lack an EM7-specific reference
Sometimes confused with the SV40 promoter when vector maps omit the dual-promoter architecture and only label the eukaryotic element
notes: EM7 is strictly prokaryotic — it does not drive expression in eukaryotes and should never be expected to function in mammalian, yeast, insect, or plant systems. In dual-purpose vectors its primary role is to maintain selectable marker expression in E. coli during propagation, decoupled from the eukaryotic transgene cassette. The promoter is synthetic and its origin is an internal Invitrogen designation; it is not derived from a named natural operon. Because it is constitutive and strong, care should be taken when the downstream resistance gene confers dominant toxicity at high expression levels.

description: The cat promoter is the native bacterial promoter driving transcription of the chloramphenicol acetyltransferase (cat) gene, most commonly derived from transposon Tn9 in Escherichia coli. It is widely used in cloning vectors to drive constitutive expression of the cat selection marker, enabling chloramphenicol resistance as a selectable phenotype. Its moderate strength and constitutive activity in gram-negative bacteria make it a standard component of low- and medium-copy-number cloning vectors.
mechanism: Functions as a sigma-70 (σ70) recognized promoter in E. coli, containing canonical -35 (TTGACA) and -10 (TATAAT) hexamer elements that direct RNA polymerase binding and open-complex formation for constitutive transcription of the downstream cat open reading frame.
known_variants: cat-86 promoter (Bacillus subtilis origin, chloramphenicol-inducible via ribosome stalling attenuation mechanism)
Truncated minimal promoter retaining only -35/-10 elements (~60 bp)
Synthetic cat promoter variants with altered spacing between -35 and -10 elements for tuned expression
known_misannotations: Confused with the cat-86 inducible promoter from B. subtilis, which is regulated by transcriptional attenuation rather than constitutive
Sometimes the entire 5'-UTR and regulatory region including attenuator is annotated as 'cat promoter'
Occasionally mislabeled as 'CmR promoter' or 'Cm promoter', conflating gene product (resistance) with regulatory element
The cm/cat distinction: some databases annotate the same gene as 'cm' (chloramphenicol resistance) vs 'cat' (enzyme name), leading to inconsistent promoter labeling
notes: The most prevalent cat promoter in E. coli cloning vectors derives from Tn9, a class I transposon. pACYC184 (p15A origin) is the canonical reference vector harboring this promoter and is broadly compatible with ColE1-origin plasmids for co-transformation. The cat-86 variant from B. subtilis is mechanistically distinct (transcriptional attenuation by ribosome stalling) and should not be conflated with the constitutive Tn9-derived promoter used in gram-negative vectors.

description: The native promoter driving expression of the tetA gene, which encodes a class A tetracycline/H+ antiporter that confers tetracycline resistance by active efflux. It is negatively regulated by the TetR repressor protein and is exploited in plasmid engineering both as a resistance marker promoter and as a tightly inducible expression element in E. coli. The promoter is derepressed by nanomolar concentrations of tetracycline or doxycycline, enabling precise, low-background inducible expression.
mechanism: TetR homodimers bind one or two tet operator (tetO) sequences that overlap the -10 and -35 promoter elements, directly occluding RNA polymerase binding and blocking transcription initiation. The tetracycline-Mg2+ chelate binds TetR with sub-nanomolar affinity, inducing a conformational change that releases the repressor from the operator and permits transcription.
known_variants: Class A (Tn10-derived, canonical E. coli reference; two overlapping tetO operators)
Class B (pT181-derived, Staphylococcus; different operator spacing)
Classes C, D, E, G, H (minor variants from diverse gram-negative hosts)
Synthetic minimal Ptet with single tetO for tet-on/tet-off mammalian systems (non-native context)
known_misannotations: Annotated simply as 'tet promoter' without class designation, conflating structurally and functionally distinct classes A–H
Confused with the synthetic CMV-based TRE (tet-responsive element) promoter used in mammalian tet-on/tet-off systems
The tet operator (tetO) sequence within the promoter is sometimes annotated as the entire promoter feature
Labeled as 'tetracycline-inducible promoter' which implies activation by tetracycline; it is correctly a derepressible promoter — tetracycline relieves repression rather than activating transcription
Some depositors annotate only the tetA coding sequence and omit the upstream promoter entirely
notes: The class A tetA promoter from Tn10 is the reference for most E. coli plasmid engineering. pASK-IBA vectors (IBA Lifesciences) are purpose-built around this promoter and are noted for very tight basal repression and rapid, uniform induction with anhydrotetracycline (aTc), which induces without inhibiting bacterial ribosomes. The promoter contains two nearly palindromic 19-bp tetO1 and tetO2 operator sequences. Not functional in eukaryotes without reconstruction as a synthetic TRE driving a minimal mammalian promoter.

description: The leftward promoter (pL) of bacteriophage lambda is one of the two major early promoters of phage lambda, driving transcription of the N gene and other early leftward genes. In expression vectors it is paired with the thermolabile CI857 repressor, enabling tight repression at 30°C and strong induction upon temperature shift to 42°C. When fully derepressed it ranks among the strongest promoters characterized in E. coli, making it widely used for high-level recombinant protein production.
mechanism: RNA polymerase engages strong −35 (TTGACA) and −10 (TATGAT) hexamers; the lambda CI repressor binds the adjacent OL operator sites (OL1, OL2, OL3) to block elongation and repress transcription. Use of the thermolabile CI857 allele couples repression to temperature: growth at 30°C maintains repression, while a shift to 42°C rapidly denatures the repressor and allows full transcriptional activation.
known_variants: CI857 temperature-inducible configuration (most common): uses thermolabile repressor for 30°C repression / 42°C induction
Minimal promoter constructs (−60 to +1) lacking full operator array, used where CI-independent constitutive expression is desired
NutL-extended versions that include the N-utilization site upstream for N-mediated antitermination read-through
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'lambda promoter' without specifying pL vs pR, causing ambiguity with the rightward promoter (pR) which has different regulation and context
Entire pL–OL operator region annotated as a single 'promoter' feature, obscuring the distinct operator and promoter sub-elements
Confused with tac or T5 promoters in temperature-shift inducible vectors, since all share the 42°C induction step when CI857 is present
notes: A functional pL expression system requires a source of CI repressor — either chromosomally integrated (e.g., a λ lysogen carrying CI857) or encoded on a compatible plasmid. Without CI repressor, pL is constitutively active; this can be toxic due to very high basal transcription levels before induction. The 42°C induction temperature can stress E. coli cultures and is incompatible with heat-sensitive proteins. pL is not active in eukaryotic hosts. Antitermination by the phage N protein and NutL sites is sometimes exploited to drive read-through of downstream terminators.

description: The rightward promoter (pR) from bacteriophage lambda drives high-level sigma70-dependent transcription of the phage O, P, and Q genes during the lytic cycle. In plasmid engineering it is widely exploited as a tightly regulated, high-strength inducible promoter in E. coli: activity is nearly abolished by the lambda cI repressor and can be switched on by inactivating cI, most commonly by temperature shift using the thermosensitive cI857 allele (37°C repressed → 42°C induced). It is one of the strongest and most tightly controlled inducible promoters available for bacterial protein production.
mechanism: Sigma70-holoenzyme recognizes canonical -35 (TTTACA) and -10 (TATACT) hexamers of pR; the lambda cI repressor binds cooperatively to operator sites OR1 and OR2 immediately downstream of the -10 element, sterically blocking RNA polymerase open-complex formation. Inactivation of cI—by thermal denaturation of the cI857 ts allele at 42°C or by prophage induction—releases repression and allows high-level constitutive transcription.
known_variants: cI857 temperature-inducible system: pR paired with chromosomal or plasmid-borne ts cI857 repressor (induced by 42°C shift)
pR-N antiterminator system: pR combined with lambda N protein and nut sites for read-through of downstream terminators
Synthetic pR derivatives with optimized -35/-10 spacer lengths for altered basal/induced expression levels
pR' (pR-prime): extended variant retaining downstream antiterminator elements
known_misannotations: Confused with lambda pL (leftward promoter): both are lambda phage promoters repressed by cI but drive transcription in opposite directions and have distinct operator sequences (OL vs OR)
Annotated generically as 'lambda promoter' without specifying pR vs pL, causing ambiguity in databases
Operator region (OR1/OR2) annotated as an integral part of the promoter in some records and as a separate 'operator' feature in others, leading to inconsistent length assignments
Occasionally mislabeled as 'T7 promoter' in vectors that co-express T7 RNA polymerase under pR control
notes: pR is almost always deployed with a cI repressor source (cI857 ts gene on the same vector, a compatible plasmid, or integrated in the host chromosome such as DE3-style lysogens). Without cI, pR drives constitutive high-level expression that can be toxic. Functional only in hosts supporting sigma70 transcription (E. coli and close gram-negative relatives). The operator region OR1–OR2 lies within the transcribed leader, so annotated promoter boundaries vary: some records include only the -35 to +1 region (~60 bp) while others extend through OR2 (~200–300 bp).

description: The lacI promoter (PlacI) is the native E. coli promoter driving constitutive, low-level transcription of the lacI gene encoding the lac repressor protein. It maintains basal repressor concentrations sufficient to keep IPTG-inducible systems (Plac, Ptac, PT7lac) tightly repressed in the absence of inducer. In plasmid engineering, PlacI is frequently carried on expression vectors or accessory plasmids to supplement chromosomal lacI and ensure adequate repression on high-copy backbones.
mechanism: PlacI contains two overlapping sigma-70 (σ70) promoter elements, P1 and P2, that produce weak constitutive transcription recognized by the E. coli housekeeping RNA polymerase holoenzyme. The deliberately low activity of PlacI limits lac repressor to substoichiometric levels relative to operator sites, a balance that is disrupted on multicopy plasmids unless a stronger promoter allele (lacIq) or an additional copy of lacI is supplied.
known_variants: lacIq — single G→A transition at the -35 element of P1, increases PlacI activity ~10-fold for higher repressor titers on multicopy plasmids
lacIq1 — alternative promoter-up allele with similar effect to lacIq, used in some Novagen and Invitrogen expression hosts
Ptac-lacI fusions — synthetic constructs that place lacI under a stronger promoter for very tight repression
known_misannotations: Confused with Plac (the lac operon promoter that IS regulated by the lac repressor) — PlacI drives lacI expression, Plac is repressed by it
Annotated simply as 'lacI' conflating the promoter with the lacI coding sequence
lacIq allele sometimes labeled as wild-type PlacI or vice versa, obscuring copy-number compensation
Occasionally mislabeled as 'lac promoter' in vector maps, ambiguously referring to either PlacI or Plac
notes: PlacI is functional only in E. coli and closely related gram-negative hosts that encode the compatible σ70. The wild-type promoter is insufficient to supply repressor from multicopy plasmids (>15 copies); lacIq should be used instead in high-copy contexts. Many commercial expression strains (BL21(DE3), DH5α) carry chromosomal lacI but not lacIq, so tight basal repression on pUC- or ColE1-derived vectors often requires a vector-borne lacIq. The lacI promoter region is typically supplied as a ~300–350 bp fragment upstream of the lacI ATG.

description: The human cytomegalovirus (HCMV) major immediate-early promoter/enhancer drives constitutively high-level transcription in a broad range of mammalian cell types and is one of the strongest known mammalian expression-driving promoters. It is routinely incorporated into transient and stable expression vectors for recombinant protein production, reporter assays, and gene delivery applications. Its broad cell-type activity and high output make it the default promoter choice in mammalian expression systems.
mechanism: The CMV IE promoter contains a powerful enhancer region (~400 bp) harboring multiple binding sites for ubiquitous transcription factors including NF-κB, AP-1, CREB, and SP1, which collectively recruit RNA Pol II and drive constitutive high-level transcription without requiring viral proteins. During lytic HCMV infection, viral IE1/IE2 transactivators further amplify expression, but the enhancer alone is sufficient to achieve strong constitutive activity from episomal or integrated plasmid contexts.
known_variants: CMV minimal promoter (~180-250 bp, TATA box + initiator only, substantially lower activity)
CMV enhancer alone (used upstream of heterologous core promoters, e.g., in CAG/CAGG construct)
CAG promoter (CMV enhancer fused to chicken beta-actin promoter + rabbit beta-globin intron — stronger and more stable)
Truncated CMV variants with selective deletion of enhancer sub-elements (NF-κB sites removed for reduced immunogenicity)
Synthetic/shuffled CMV-like promoters with rearranged transcription factor binding sites
known_misannotations: CMV enhancer annotated separately from the core promoter, producing partial and variably sized annotations for the same element
Confusion between human CMV (HCMV, strain AD169 or Towne) and mouse CMV (MCMV) promoter sequences, which differ significantly
Variable inclusion or exclusion of the 5' UTR intron A region causes length discrepancies (500 bp vs 800 bp) for annotations of nominally the same promoter
Strain not specified (AD169 vs Towne vs Toledo), causing sequence ambiguity between otherwise identically labeled annotations
CMV promoter label sometimes applied to the CAG promoter, which uses only the CMV enhancer fused to a different core promoter
notes: Activity can be silenced by CpG methylation in stable integrants over extended culture, particularly in embryonic stem cells, iPSCs, and certain hematopoietic lineages where the enhancer is epigenetically repressed. For long-term stable expression or stem cell applications, EF1α, PGK, or CAG promoters are often preferable. The CMV promoter is also subject to immune-mediated transcriptional silencing in vivo, limiting its use in gene therapy vectors; lentiviral and AAV vectors frequently substitute EF1α or tissue-specific promoters for in vivo work.

description: The cytomegalovirus (HCMV) immediate-early (IE) enhancer is one of the most potent known cis-acting transcriptional enhancers in mammalian cells, derived from the major IE locus of human cytomegalovirus strain AD169. It contains tandem repeat elements and dense clusters of binding sites for ubiquitous transcription factors, making it active across virtually all mammalian cell types. In plasmid engineering it is used either as part of the full CMV IE promoter or alone (notably in the CAG hybrid promoter) to drive extremely high-level transgene expression.
mechanism: Recruits ubiquitous activators including NF-κB, SP1, AP-1, CREB/ATF, and serum response factor (SRF) through multiple tandem repeat elements in its 'modulator', 'unique', and '21 bp repeat' sub-domains, stimulating RNA Pol II pre-initiation complex assembly at downstream core promoters independent of orientation.
known_variants: Truncated CMV enhancer (~230 bp core, lacks 21 bp repeats) used in some AAV vectors to reduce immunogenicity
CMV enhancer from Towne strain — slightly different repeat copy number vs. AD169
CAG context: CMV enhancer fused to chicken beta-actin (CBA) promoter and rabbit beta-globin intron for sustained expression
Methylation-resistant synthetic variants engineered to resist CpG silencing in stable lines
known_misannotations: Full ~760 bp CMV IE promoter/enhancer region frequently annotated as just 'CMV enhancer' or just 'CMV promoter', conflating enhancer and TATA-proximal promoter elements
The enhancer alone (lacking TATA box and transcription start site) is sometimes called 'CMV promoter', which is incorrect — it cannot independently initiate transcription
In CAG-promoter vectors the CMV-derived portion is occasionally mislabeled 'CMV promoter' rather than 'CMV enhancer'
Mouse CMV (MCMV) enhancer sometimes deposited or described as 'CMV enhancer' without organism qualifier, causing confusion with the more common HCMV version
notes: The CMV enhancer is subject to transcriptional silencing via CpG methylation in stable integrants and pluripotent stem cells; this limits its use in long-term gene therapy contexts. It is inactive or weakly active in undifferentiated ES/iPS cells. The enhancer is highly immunostimulatory due to unmethylated CpG motifs, which is relevant for in vivo delivery. Regulatory jurisdiction in gene therapy products often requires disclosure of CMV-derived elements. The element is orientation-independent but is almost universally used in the sense orientation relative to the downstream promoter.

description: The human elongation factor 1-alpha (EEF1A1) gene promoter drives strong, constitutive expression in virtually all mammalian cell types and tissues. It is widely used in expression vectors—particularly lentiviral and retroviral constructs—as a robust alternative to the CMV promoter because it is substantially more resistant to transcriptional silencing in stable cell lines and during in vivo gene delivery. The promoter can be used in both full-length form (with intron A, ~1.2 kb) or as a truncated short form (EFS, ~212 bp) when transgene cargo size is limiting.
mechanism: Drives RNA Polymerase II-dependent transcription via a canonical TATA box and multiple upstream transcription factor binding sites (SP1, NF-κB, AP-1 elements); the first intron (intron A) contains an intronic enhancer that substantially boosts mRNA accumulation post-transcriptionally by promoting splicing and nuclear export. The locus resides in an open, constitutively active chromatin context due to its role as a ubiquitous housekeeping gene.
known_variants: EFS (EF-1α short) — truncated ~212 bp minimal core; lower expression than full-length but fits large lentiviral payloads
EF-1α + intron A — full ~1.2 kb form including first intron enhancer; highest expression
Mouse EF-1α promoter — orthologous promoter from Mus musculus EEF1A1; similar activity profile
Synthetic/codon-context variants used in some AAV vectors to reduce immunogenicity
known_misannotations: EFS often annotated interchangeably with full-length EF-1α despite significantly lower expression output
Labeled 'EF1-alpha' (digit '1') vs 'EF-1α' inconsistently across vector maps and databases
Some vectors annotate only the intron A enhancer fragment rather than the complete promoter-intron unit
Occasionally confused with the non-human primate or rodent EF-1α ortholog when species of origin is unspecified
notes: EF-1α is the preferred promoter for stable integrations and in vivo applications (AAV, lentivirus) where CMV silencing is a concern. Expression level is broadly comparable to CMV in transient transfection but significantly more durable in stable lines and primary cells. The short form (EFS) is the standard choice for dual-gene lentiviral vectors (e.g., transgene + WPRE + EFS + selection marker) where the 5′ LTR-to-3′ LTR distance must stay under ~8 kb for efficient packaging.

description: The phosphoglycerate kinase 1 (PGK1) promoter is a constitutive, ubiquitous mammalian promoter derived from the housekeeping gene encoding PGK1, an enzyme of the glycolytic pathway. It drives stable, moderate-level expression across a wide range of cell types and tissues without significant silencing over time, making it widely favored in lentiviral vectors, gene targeting cassettes, and gene therapy constructs. The mouse PGK (mPGK) promoter is more commonly used than the human version in research vectors.
mechanism: The PGK1 promoter is a CpG island-containing, TATA-less promoter that recruits RNA Pol II through binding sites for ubiquitous transcription factors including Sp1, AP-2, and NF-1, driving constitutive transcription without tissue-specific regulatory input.
known_variants: Mouse PGK1 promoter (mPGK, ~480 bp core or ~1.1 kb extended) — most commonly used form in research vectors
Human PGK1 promoter (hPGK, ~500 bp) — preferred in some clinical gene therapy contexts
Truncated/minimal PGK promoter (~200–300 bp) — reduced but detectable activity with smaller footprint
known_misannotations: Mouse and human PGK promoters frequently annotated interchangeably without species designation
PGK-driven selection cassettes (PGK-neo, PGK-puro) often annotated as a single compound feature rather than separate promoter and resistance gene elements
Sometimes labeled simply 'PGK' without distinguishing the promoter from the full PGK1 coding sequence in mixed annotations
Occasionally confused with prokaryotic pgk gene regulatory regions in vectors containing both bacterial and mammalian elements
notes: The mPGK promoter is especially prevalent in mouse embryonic stem cell gene targeting vectors where it drives neomycin or puromycin resistance cassettes; its sustained activity in ES cells and early embryos is well established. Expression levels are moderate and generally more stable than CMV, which is prone to silencing in stem cells and long-term culture. The promoter is bidirectionally active at low levels. When annotating, confirm whether the annotated region includes only the upstream promoter fragment or also the 5' UTR of PGK1.

aliases: chicken beta-actin promoter with CMV enhancer
description: The CAG promoter is a strong synthetic hybrid promoter designed for constitutive, ubiquitous transgene expression in mammalian cells. It combines the CMV (cytomegalovirus) immediate-early enhancer, the chicken beta-actin (ACTB) promoter, and typically a rabbit beta-globin intron splice acceptor downstream, yielding among the highest expression levels of any constitutive promoter. It is widely deployed in transient transfection vectors, AAV, lentiviral, and transgenic mouse constructs.
mechanism: The CMV IE enhancer dramatically boosts transcription initiation from the chicken beta-actin TATA-box promoter; the included rabbit beta-globin intron is retained in pre-mRNA and spliced co-transcriptionally, which stabilizes the transcript, promotes nuclear export, and substantially increases steady-state protein output compared to intron-less variants.
known_variants: CAG without intron (CMV enhancer + chicken beta-actin promoter only; shorter ~900 bp, lower expression)
CBA variant (lacks rabbit beta-globin intron; sometimes used interchangeably but distinct)
Truncated CAG (shorter CMV enhancer deletions used in space-constrained AAV payloads)
smCBA (shortened CMV/chicken beta-actin, ~900 bp; used when payload size is limiting in AAV)
CAG with woodchuck hepatitis post-transcriptional regulatory element (WPRE) downstream to further boost expression
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'CMV promoter' when only the CMV enhancer portion is recognized
Annotated as 'chicken beta-actin promoter' or 'ACTB promoter' omitting the CMV enhancer
Confused with 'CBA promoter' (lacks the rabbit globin intron — functionally distinct)
Length underestimated when the rabbit beta-globin intron is excluded from the annotated region
Labeled as 'CAGG' (single G) vs 'CAGGS' (double S) inconsistently across databases
notes: The CAG promoter is paradoxically prone to transcriptional silencing by CpG methylation in pluripotent stem cells, early embryos, and germline — limiting its use in ESC or iPSC applications despite strong somatic expression. In AAV vectors, size constraints frequently drive use of the truncated smCBA or CBh variants. The Niwa et al. 1991 paper (Gene 108:193-199) is the primary reference; the original vector is pCAGGS. The intron in the full CAGGS element is a hybrid intron (IVS from rabbit beta-globin gene), and its inclusion is critical for maximum expression levels.

description: The UBC promoter is derived from the human ubiquitin C (UBC) gene and drives constitutive, ubiquitous expression in mammalian cells. It is widely used in lentiviral and other mammalian expression vectors as an alternative to viral promoters, particularly when stable, long-term transgene expression is needed. Compared to the CMV promoter, UBC tends to show more sustained activity in stably integrated constructs and is less prone to transcriptional silencing over time.
mechanism: The UBC promoter contains a constitutively active CpG island and binds ubiquitous transcription factors including Sp1 and NF-Y, driving RNA Pol II-mediated transcription independent of cell type or external stimuli. Its activity is supported by the housekeeping gene regulatory environment of the endogenous ubiquitin locus.
known_variants: Minimal UBC promoter (~250 bp core fragment lacking distal regulatory elements)
Mouse Ubc promoter (orthologous sequence, ~85% identity to human)
UBC promoter with downstream intron retained for enhanced expression
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'UBB promoter' (ubiquitin B) — UBB and UBC are distinct paralogous genes with different promoters
Labeled generically as 'ubiquitin promoter' without specifying UBC vs UBB vs polyubiquitin loci
Confused with the chicken ubiquitin promoter (used in some older retroviral vectors), which has distinct sequence and regulatory behavior
Truncated versions sometimes annotated with the same name as the full-length promoter despite different activity profiles
notes: The UBC promoter is a popular choice for lentiviral vectors intended for stable integration because it resists CpG methylation-mediated silencing better than the CMV promoter in many cell types, including hematopoietic and stem cells. Expression levels are moderate — generally lower than CMV in transient transfection but more durable in stable lines. The human UBC gene (NCBI Gene ID: 7316) resides on chromosome 12q24.31. When species origin matters (e.g., mouse models), confirm whether the human or mouse ortholog was used, as they are not identical.

description: The SV40 early promoter is a constitutive, broadly active promoter derived from Simian Virus 40 that drives strong transcription in a wide range of mammalian cell types without requiring viral factors. It contains two 72-bp enhancer repeats, a 21-bp repeat region with Sp1-binding GC boxes, and a TATA box. In plasmid engineering it is most widely used to drive selectable markers such as neomycin/G418 resistance (neo) and reporter genes in stable-transfection and transient-expression vectors.
mechanism: Recruits RNA Polymerase II via a TATA box and general transcription factors; Sp1 binds the 21-bp GC-box repeats and synergizes with the 72-bp enhancer elements to activate transcription. The enhancer acts in an orientation- and position-independent manner and does not require SV40 large T antigen for basal promoter activity.
known_variants: SV40 minimal promoter — TATA box and 21-bp repeats only, 72-bp enhancer repeats deleted; weaker activity
SV40 early promoter with single 72-bp enhancer repeat (partial deletion, intermediate strength)
SV40 late promoter — distinct element driving late viral genes, used in a small number of expression vectors; not interchangeable with early promoter
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'SV40 enhancer' when the full promoter+enhancer region is present — the enhancer and promoter are often inseparable in practice but distinct conceptually
SV40 poly(A) signal sometimes included within promoter annotation boundaries, inflating apparent length
Confused with SV40 late promoter, which has different cell-type activity and transcriptional start site
Some annotations label the element 'SV40 promoter/enhancer' (combined) vs. 'SV40 promoter' alone, leading to length and boundary inconsistencies across databases
notes: Generally weaker than the CMV immediate-early promoter for transgene expression but more stable over long-term culture and less prone to silencing in some cell lines. Active in most immortalized mammalian cell lines; lower activity in primary cells. Plasmids containing SV40 sequences may require additional institutional biosafety review in some jurisdictions. The SV40 origin of replication (ori) is a distinct element often present in the same vectors; do not confuse with the promoter.

aliases: Rous sarcoma virus long terminal repeat promoter
description: The RSV promoter is derived from the 5' long terminal repeat (LTR) of Rous Sarcoma Virus, an avian retrovirus, and drives strong constitutive transcription in a broad range of mammalian cell types. It was one of the first viral promoters adopted for mammalian expression vectors and remains in wide use for stable and transient transgene expression. The promoter is active in the absence of any viral trans-acting factors, making it suitable as a standalone transcriptional driver.
mechanism: The RSV LTR U3 region contains TATA box and upstream enhancer elements that recruit general transcription factors and RNA Pol II for constitutive, cap-independent transcriptional initiation; enhancer sequences within U3 confer broad cell-type activity not restricted to avian host cells.
known_variants: Prague C strain LTR (most common in expression vectors)
Schmidt-Ruppin A strain LTR
Truncated RSV LTR (~400 bp U3 only, lacking R and U5)
Self-inactivating (SIN) LTR with U3 deletion (used in lentiviral vectors)
known_misannotations: Confused with Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) — an unrelated human pathogen with a completely different genome; context must confirm Rous Sarcoma Virus
Annotated simply as 'LTR' without specifying RSV, ambiguous when other retroviral LTRs (HIV, MMLV) are present in the same vector
Mislabeled as 'RSV enhancer' when the full U3-R region is present
Region boundaries vary: some annotations include only U3, others include U3-R-U5
notes: The RSV LTR promoter predates the widespread adoption of the CMV immediate-early promoter and is still used where CMV silencing is a concern (e.g., in stem cells or in vivo gene delivery contexts). Unlike CMV, RSV LTR activity is relatively resistant to methylation-induced silencing in some cell types. It is routinely used to drive the Renilla luciferase reporter in the pRL-RSV control vector (Promega). Strength is generally lower than CMV IE in most transformed cell lines.

description: The SFFV promoter is derived from the U3 region of the long terminal repeat (LTR) of Spleen Focus-Forming Virus, a murine retrovirus related to Friend leukemia virus. It drives strong, constitutive transgene expression with particular potency in hematopoietic cells, making it widely used in lentiviral gene therapy vectors targeting HSCs, T cells, and myeloid lineages. Its strong activity in primitive hematopoietic progenitors makes it a preferred alternative to CMV or EF1α for sustained expression after HSC transduction and differentiation.
mechanism: The SFFV U3 promoter contains binding sites for transcription factors enriched in hematopoietic cells, including GATA, Sp1, and NF-E2, which drive high-level RNA polymerase II-mediated transcription. As a gammaretroviral-derived element, it lacks intrinsic splicing signals and functions efficiently as an internal promoter cassette within self-inactivating (SIN) lentiviral vectors.
known_variants: MNDU3 promoter — a chimeric variant combining SFFV U3 with MND (myeloproliferative sarcoma virus) sequences for enhanced expression in HSCs
Truncated SFFV promoter (~200–300 bp) retaining core activity with reduced immunogenicity
CpG-depleted SFFV promoter — modified to reduce epigenetic silencing in pluripotent and early progenitor cells
known_misannotations: Frequently conflated with MSCV (Murine Stem Cell Virus) promoter, which is a related but distinct gammaretroviral element with different activity profiles
Sometimes labeled 'SFFV LTR' when only the U3 region (not the full R-U5) is included — the full LTR is ~600–700 bp
Confused with F-MuLV (Friend Murine Leukemia Virus) promoter, which is the helper virus component of the Friend virus complex
notes: SFFV promoter is one of the strongest hematopoietic-active viral promoters and is a preferred choice for gene therapy applications in blood disorders (e.g., beta-thalassemia, sickle cell, CGD). Its gammaretroviral origin raises historical insertional mutagenesis concerns in early gene therapy trials (although this risk is vector-design-dependent, not promoter-intrinsic). In self-inactivating (SIN) lentiviral vectors, the SFFV element is used as an internal promoter cassette, not as the LTR. Activity is substantially lower in non-hematopoietic mammalian cell types; CMV or EF1α are preferred for ubiquitous expression.

description: The promoter derived from the human elongation factor 1 alpha gene (EEF1A1), one of the most abundant and constitutively expressed proteins in eukaryotic cells. It drives strong, constitutive transgene expression across a broad range of mammalian cell types and is widely used in lentiviral vectors, retroviral vectors, and episomal expression plasmids. Unlike CMV promoter, EF1α is less prone to silencing in stable cell lines and primary cells, making it preferred for long-term expression studies and therapeutic applications.
mechanism: Recruits RNA Polymerase II via a TATA-less core promoter mechanism with multiple SP1 and NF-Y binding sites; the full-length version includes the first intron of EEF1A1, which contains a strong transcriptional enhancer element that substantially boosts expression levels.
known_variants: EFS (short EF1α, ~212 bp truncation of the core promoter lacking the first intron enhancer; used in space-constrained lentiviral vectors)
Full-length with intron 1 (~1.2–1.6 kb; strongest expression, includes intronic enhancer)
Truncated core promoter (~300–500 bp; intermediate strength, no intron)
Mouse EF1α promoter (from Eef1a1; highly similar but distinct sequence, used in some murine vectors)
Codon-context optimized synthetic EF1α variants
known_misannotations: EFS (short form) is sometimes incorrectly labeled 'EF1α promoter' without distinguishing the truncation, causing confusion about expected expression levels
The first intron is inconsistently included or excluded across plasmid annotations; vectors with vs. without intron 1 differ substantially in strength but may both be labeled 'EF1α'
Sometimes listed as 'EF-1' without alpha designation, conflating with EF-1β or other elongation factor promoters
Occasionally mislabeled as 'CMV/EF1α hybrid' when only the EF1α sequence is actually present
notes: EF1α is generally preferred over CMV for stable integrations and primary cell transduction because CMV promoter undergoes methylation-driven silencing. The inclusion of intron 1 is critical — vectors with the full promoter+intron express 5–10× higher than EFS in many cell types. EFS is the standard choice for lentiviral packaging when cargo size is limiting. EF1α activity is largely cell-cycle independent, making it suitable for non-dividing cells including neurons and T cells in adoptive cell therapy contexts.

description: The hPGK promoter is derived from the 5' regulatory region of the human phosphoglycerate kinase 1 (PGK1) gene, a ubiquitously expressed glycolytic enzyme. It drives constitutive, moderate-level transcription in virtually all mammalian cell types and is widely used in lentiviral, retroviral, and AAV vectors to provide stable, long-term expression of selectable markers or transgenes. Its housekeeping-gene origin makes it resistant to silencing in many cell types, including embryonic stem cells and induced pluripotent stem cells.
mechanism: The hPGK promoter contains a CpG island and lacks a canonical TATA box, relying instead on Sp1 and other transcription factor binding sites within a GC-rich initiator region to recruit RNA Pol II for constitutive transcription. As a housekeeping promoter it is active across diverse chromatin environments and is generally refractory to position-effect silencing.
known_variants: Short core fragment (~469 bp immediately upstream of TSS)
Extended version (~1.2 kb including 5' UTR intron 1)
Mouse PGK (mPGK) promoter — orthologous but distinct sequence, commonly used in targeting vectors
known_misannotations: Often labeled simply 'PGK promoter' without specifying human vs. mouse origin; mPGK and hPGK are not identical
Occasionally confused with PGK2 (phosphoglycerate kinase 2), which is testis-specific and functionally unrelated
Variable truncation points across different vector series lead to inconsistent reported lengths under the same name
notes: hPGK is a preferred promoter for driving selection cassettes (e.g., neomycin, puromycin, hygromycin resistance) in stable integration contexts because it is less prone to CpG methylation-mediated silencing than CMV. Expression level is moderate and relatively uniform across cell types, making it suitable for applications requiring predictable dosage rather than maximal output. In lentiviral vectors it is commonly placed in the internal position (3' of the gene of interest) in bicistronic designs.

description: The CBh promoter is a compact hybrid promoter combining the CMV immediate-early enhancer with a shortened chicken beta-actin (CBA) promoter and a truncated chimeric intron, engineered to drive strong, ubiquitous constitutive transgene expression in mammalian cells. It was specifically developed by Gray et al. (2011) for use in adeno-associated virus (AAV) vectors, where its reduced size (~800 bp vs. ~1.7 kb for full CAG/CBA) preserves precious packaging capacity. It supports robust expression across diverse cell types including neurons, hepatocytes, skeletal muscle, and retinal cells.
mechanism: The CMV enhancer element recruits transcriptional activators and coactivators to stimulate RNA polymerase II assembly at the chicken beta-actin core promoter; the downstream truncated hybrid intron (chimera of chicken beta-actin and rabbit beta-globin intron sequences) enhances mRNA processing, nuclear export, and translational efficiency via splicing-coupled mechanisms.
known_variants: scCBh — self-complementary AAV-optimized version with further intron trimming
CBh with WPRE — paired with woodchuck hepatitis virus posttranscriptional regulatory element for enhanced expression
known_misannotations: Frequently mislabeled as 'CAG promoter' or 'CBA promoter' despite being a distinct, shorter construct
Sometimes annotated as 'CMV promoter' when only the enhancer portion is recognized by annotation tools
Occasionally confused with the full-length CB6 promoter or CB7 promoter variants used in liver-directed gene therapy
The 'h' suffix (hybrid intron) is sometimes omitted, causing ambiguity with plain 'CB' promoter references
notes: Developed by Gray SJ et al. (Hum Gene Ther. 2011;22(9):1143-53) to maximize expression within AAV's ~4.7 kb packaging limit. The 'h' denotes the truncated hybrid intron derived from portions of the chicken beta-actin intron 1 and the rabbit beta-globin intron. Preferred over full CAG/CBA in size-constrained AAV constructs, particularly self-complementary AAV (scAAV) which has an even smaller effective payload (~2.4 kb). Expression is ubiquitous and long-lasting in vivo; not suitable for tissue-restricted applications without additional regulatory elements.

description: The TRE (Tetracycline Response Element) promoter is a synthetic inducible promoter consisting of multiple tandem copies of the 19-bp tet operator (tetO) sequence fused to a minimal CMV promoter. It is the central regulatory element of the widely used Tet-On and Tet-Off systems, enabling tightly controlled, reversible transgene expression in mammalian cells. The system allows precise temporal and dose-dependent gene regulation using tetracycline or doxycycline as the chemical inducer.
mechanism: In the Tet-On configuration, the reverse tetracycline transactivator (rtTA) undergoes a conformational change upon doxycycline binding and binds tetO sequences within the TRE, recruiting transcriptional machinery to the downstream minimal CMV promoter. In the Tet-Off configuration, the tTA transactivator binds constitutively and is released upon doxycycline addition, switching expression off.
known_variants: TRE3G — 3rd generation with re-optimized tetO sequences providing higher induction fold and tighter repression in the uninduced state
TRE-Tight — minimal basal (leaky) expression variant; preferred when background expression is problematic
TRE2 — 2nd generation variant with improved signal-to-noise over original TRE
Bidirectional TRE (Pbi-CMV) — drives divergent expression of two transgenes from a single central TRE element
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'CMV promoter' due to the embedded CMV minimal promoter; the full TRE includes tetO repeats and is not equivalent to CMV
TRE and TRE3G used interchangeably despite different tetO compositions and distinct induction characteristics
Labeled as 'tetO' or 'tet operator' without including the minimal promoter, making the element appear non-functional in isolation
Tet-On and Tet-Off TRE elements labeled identically despite being paired with functionally opposite transactivators (rtTA vs tTA)
notes: Functional TRE activity absolutely requires co-expression of the appropriate transactivator (rtTA for Tet-On, tTA for Tet-Off) from a separate constitutive promoter; the TRE alone is silent without its cognate transactivator. Leaky basal expression in the uninduced state is a common experimental concern with earlier versions; TRE3G and TRE-Tight substantially reduce this. Doxycycline is strongly preferred over tetracycline as the inducer due to lower cytotoxicity, better membrane permeability, and higher affinity for rtTA. The original system was described by Gossen & Bujard (1992, PNAS) and Gossen et al. (1995, Science).

description: A synthetic bidirectional promoter that drives simultaneous inducible expression of two transgenes from a central tetracycline response element (TRE) flanked by minimal CMV promoters oriented in opposite directions. It is activated by the reverse tetracycline transactivator (rtTA) in the presence of doxycycline (Tet-On system) or by the tetracycline transactivator (tTA) in the absence of tetracycline (Tet-Off system). It is widely used in mammalian systems to co-regulate two genes under a single inducible cassette, enabling paired reporter/effector or dual-pathway experiments.
mechanism: Multiple tet operator (tetO) sequences within the TRE core recruit rtTA or tTA bound to their small-molecule ligand, which in turn activates transcription from both flanking minimal CMV promoters simultaneously, driving divergent transcription of two downstream coding sequences from a single compact regulatory element.
known_variants: TRE3G bidirectional (3rd-generation TRE with reduced basal leakiness and higher induction ratio)
Original pBI 1st-generation (7× tetO2 core, higher background)
TRE-Tight bidirectional (minimized leaky expression in sensitive cell types)
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'TRE promoter' without indicating bidirectionality, obscuring the dual-expression architecture
Confused with unidirectional TRE or CMV/TRE hybrid promoters when only one transcriptional arm is captured in the annotation
The two flanking minimal CMV promoter elements are sometimes annotated as separate CMV features rather than as integral components of the bidirectional unit
Generation (1st vs 3rd) frequently omitted, leading to ambiguity about expected leakiness and induction fold
notes: Exclusively used in mammalian systems requiring coordinated inducible co-expression of two genes (e.g., paired reporter and gene of interest, or two cooperating pathway components). Expression from each arm can be asymmetric in magnitude. Requires co-expression of rtTA (Tet-On) or tTA (Tet-Off) from a separate constitutive promoter on the same or a companion vector. Third-generation TRE3G variants substantially reduce doxycycline-independent basal transcription, which is a common problem with 1st-generation pBI constructs in sensitive cell types such as neurons or stem cells.

description: The 35S promoter from Cauliflower Mosaic Virus (CaMV) is a strong constitutive promoter that drives high-level transcription in virtually all dicot plant tissues and developmental stages. It is the most widely used promoter in transgenic plant research and agricultural biotechnology. The canonical form spans approximately −343 to +1 relative to the transcription start site.
mechanism: Contains bipartite upstream activating sequences (UAS) organized in two functional domains: domain A (−90 to −46) provides basal promoter activity, while domain B (−343 to −90) harbors an enhancer that amplifies expression broadly across cell types. A TATA box directs transcription initiation via general plant RNA polymerase II machinery.
known_variants: Double 35S promoter (d35S) — enhancer region (domain B) duplicated upstream, yields ~2–5× higher expression
Minimal 35S (−90 bp form) — domain A only; used as a low-activity baseline or negative-enhancer control
35S::Ω — full promoter fused to TMV Omega 5′ UTR leader for enhanced translation efficiency
Synthetic enhanced 35S (e35S) — additional transcription factor binding sites engineered into domain B
known_misannotations: Annotated simply as '35S' without specifying CaMV origin, risking confusion with other viral 35S loci
Double 35S (d35S) labeled as '35S', obscuring the enhancer duplication and inflating apparent baseline activity
Length inconsistencies when the minimal −90 form and the full-length −343 form are used interchangeably under the same label
Promoter region boundaries shifted by inclusion or exclusion of the 5′ UTR / leader sequence in different cloning contexts
notes: The CaMV 35S promoter is largely plant-specific and shows negligible functional activity in bacteria, yeast, or mammalian cells. It is subject to RNA-directed DNA methylation (RdDM) silencing in some transgenic backgrounds, particularly over successive generations. Its sequence is a primary target in regulatory PCR-based GMO detection assays (used as a universal GMO marker by EFSA and FDA methods). In monocots (grasses, cereals), expression is typically weaker than in dicots; maize Ubiquitin-1 (Ubi-1) or rice Actin1 promoters are generally preferred for monocot transformation.

description: The nopaline synthase (nos) promoter is a constitutive plant promoter derived from the T-DNA of the nopaline-type Ti plasmid of Agrobacterium tumefaciens. It drives low-to-moderate levels of gene expression in most plant tissues and is predominantly used to express selectable marker genes (most commonly NPTII/NPT-II for kanamycin resistance) in plant transformation vectors. Its relatively weak constitutive activity makes it well-suited for marker cassettes where high transgene expression is undesirable.
mechanism: The NOS promoter contains TATA box and upstream regulatory elements recognized by plant RNA polymerase II, driving constitutive transcription in plant nuclei. It was natively expressed from Agrobacterium T-DNA after integration into the plant genome and has been functionally dissected to a ~280–350 bp minimal promoter region upstream of the nos ATG.
known_variants: Truncated ~280 bp minimal promoter (commonly used in binary vectors)
Full-length ~400 bp promoter including upstream AT-rich elements
Synthetic nos-like promoters with optimized TATA elements
known_misannotations: Confused with NOS terminator (nos-ter / NOS 3'): the promoter and terminator are distinct elements frequently both present in the same vector and mislabeled interchangeably
Annotated as 'nos' without specifying promoter vs. terminator, causing ambiguity in vector maps
Occasionally labeled 'NOS poly(A)' when the annotator means the terminator, not the promoter
Sometimes listed as driving the NPTII ORF when actually the NPTII cassette uses NOS promoter + NOS terminator flanking the coding sequence
notes: The NOS promoter is almost always paired with the NOS terminator in the same cassette (e.g., NOS-pro::NPTII::NOS-ter). Expression levels are significantly lower than the CaMV 35S promoter, which is intentional for selection markers — high NPTII expression can cause toxicity artifacts. Activity has been confirmed in dicots and monocots but is weaker in monocots. Not active in E. coli, yeast, or mammalian cells. Care should be taken to distinguish the ~300 bp promoter fragment from the ~260 bp terminator fragment, as both are present in most binary vectors and have similar sizes on gels.

description: Root-preferential promoter from tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that drives strong transgene expression in root meristematic and vascular tissues with minimal expression in aerial organs. It is widely used in plant biotechnology to spatially restrict transgene expression to roots, enabling applications such as nematode resistance, rhizosphere-targeted enzyme production, and root-specific metabolic engineering without off-target effects in leaves or seeds.
mechanism: Contains root-specific cis-regulatory elements upstream of the transcription start site that recruit root-expressed transcription factors, particularly those active in meristematic and phloem-associated cells. The promoter activity is developmentally regulated and does not require exogenous chemical induction.
known_variants: Minimal RB7 promoter (truncated ~300–500 bp core retaining root specificity)
RB7 promoter::GUS transcriptional fusions used to map expression domains
RB7 promoter driving Bt toxin or proteinase inhibitor genes for nematode resistance
known_misannotations: Confused with the RB7 MAR (matrix attachment region), a separate insulator/chromatin element from the same Nicotiana tabacum RB7 gene locus
Occasionally mislabeled as a constitutive plant promoter rather than a root-preferential one
Sometimes annotated simply as 'plant promoter' without specifying root specificity
notes: The RB7 gene encodes a water channel-related protein expressed predominantly in tobacco root tips and vascular tissue. The promoter has been validated in both monocot and dicot species via Agrobacterium-mediated transformation, though activity levels can vary across plant families. The RB7 MAR element (a scaffold/matrix attachment region from the same locus) is a distinct functional element often used in the same vectors to reduce positional effects; take care not to conflate the two when annotating transformation vectors.

description: The T7 Te terminator is an intrinsic transcription terminator derived from bacteriophage T7 that halts RNA polymerase-driven transcription downstream of a gene of interest. It is ubiquitous in T7-based expression vectors such as the pET series, where it prevents read-through transcription into the vector backbone and improves transcript stability. Its small size and high efficiency make it the default terminator choice for prokaryotic expression.
mechanism: Functions as a Rho-independent (intrinsic) terminator: the nascent RNA folds into a stable GC-rich hairpin immediately followed by a uridine-rich tract, causing RNA polymerase to pause, destabilize the RNA:DNA hybrid, and dissociate from the template without requiring any accessory factors.
known_variants: T7 Tφ terminator — a structurally distinct T7 phage terminator sometimes used interchangeably but encoded at a different genomic locus
Minimal stem-loop form (~36 bp) versus extended annotated form (~80–90 bp) that includes flanking sequence depending on the vector
known_misannotations: Labeled generically as 'terminator' without T7 attribution, making feature identity ambiguous
T7 Te and T7 Tφ terminators conflated or both annotated as 'T7 terminator' with no distinction
Annotation boundaries vary widely across databases — some cover only the stem-loop hairpin, others extend into upstream or downstream flanking regions
notes: The T7 Te terminator functions with both T7 RNA polymerase and E. coli σ70 RNAP, though efficiency varies. It does not function in eukaryotic expression systems. In many pET vectors it is paired with upstream rrnB T1 and T2 terminators to provide redundant transcription termination, preventing leaky expression. The terminator is located at approximately position 23,562–23,596 in the T7 phage genome (V01146).

description: The rrnB T1 terminator is a Rho-independent (intrinsic) transcriptional terminator derived from the first termination site of the E. coli rrnB ribosomal RNA operon. It is widely used in prokaryotic expression vectors to prevent transcriptional read-through beyond a gene of interest, protecting downstream elements and reducing metabolic burden from antisense transcription. It is frequently deployed in tandem with the downstream rrnB T2 terminator to achieve near-complete transcriptional arrest.
mechanism: Functions as a Rho-independent terminator: the nascent RNA folds into a stable GC-rich stem-loop structure immediately followed by a U-rich sequence, causing RNA polymerase to pause and dissociate from the DNA template without requiring the Rho helicase or other trans-acting cofactors.
known_variants: rrnB T1T2 — T1 used in tandem with the downstream T2 terminator (~250 bp combined) for near-complete termination efficiency
Synthetic/optimized stem-loop variants with increased thermodynamic stability used in some chassis-independent expression systems
known_misannotations: Annotated simply as 'rrnB terminator' without specifying T1 vs T2, making it ambiguous whether T1 alone or the T1T2 tandem is present
The combined T1+T2 region (~230-260 bp) is sometimes collapsed into a single annotation labeled 'rrnB T1 terminator', inflating the apparent length of T1
Occasionally mislabeled as 'rrnB T2 terminator' when downstream of a cloning site, due to confusion about which terminator is proximal to the gene
notes: rrnB T1 alone provides approximately 70-90% termination efficiency in E. coli; combined with T2 efficiency exceeds 99%. Both terminators are Rho-independent and functional across diverse Gram-negative hosts. Not effective in eukaryotic systems, which rely on RNA Pol II cleavage/polyadenylation-based termination signals. The T1 core stem-loop is approximately 46 bp with a 6-8 nt U-rich tail; flanking sequences included in annotations vary by vector, accounting for length variability in databases.

description: The rrnB T2 terminator is the second of two tandem intrinsic terminators located immediately downstream of the 23S rRNA gene in the E. coli rrnB ribosomal RNA operon. It is widely deployed in bacterial expression vectors to provide robust transcriptional termination and block read-through into flanking sequences. When used in tandem with the upstream rrnB T1 terminator, the T1–T2 pair achieves near-complete termination of RNA polymerase in E. coli.
mechanism: Functions as a Rho-independent (intrinsic) terminator through formation of a stable GC-rich hairpin stem-loop in the nascent mRNA, immediately followed by a run of uridine residues. The hairpin stalls RNA polymerase, and the weak rU–dA hybrid within the poly-U tract destabilizes the elongation complex, causing dissociation of polymerase and release of the transcript.
known_variants: rrnB T1–T2 tandem cassette (~300 bp) — T1 and T2 used together for near-complete termination; the most common form in commercial expression vectors
Minimal T2 — trimmed to the stem-loop plus poly-U tract only, used in synthetic circuit design
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'rrnB terminator' without specifying T1 or T2, causing ambiguity when only one of the two is present
Confused with rrnB T1 terminator when the full T1–T2 cassette is cloned and the inter-terminator boundary is not annotated
Occasionally annotated as 'rrnB T1 T2' on features that contain only T2 because T1 was omitted during vector construction
Mislabeled as a Rho-dependent terminator in older literature; it is strictly Rho-independent
notes: T2 alone provides moderate termination efficiency in E. coli; the T1–T2 tandem is strongly preferred in expression vectors where run-through transcription would be problematic. Efficiency is well-validated in E. coli and closely related gram-negative bacteria but is not reliable in gram-positive organisms, yeast, or mammalian cells without species-appropriate terminator replacements. J01695 is the original NCBI deposit of the E. coli rrnB operon and spans both T1 and T2; U00096 is the E. coli K-12 MG1655 complete genome which contains the chromosomal locus in full context.

description: A strong rho-independent transcription terminator derived from bacteriophage lambda, widely incorporated into E. coli expression vectors to prevent transcriptional read-through past the cloning site. It is found in the pET vector series and many other prokaryotic expression systems as a downstream terminator. Its efficiency and compact size make it a preferred element for stabilizing transcripts and insulating transgene expression cassettes.
mechanism: The terminator encodes an RNA hairpin (stem-loop) structure followed by a run of uridine residues in the nascent transcript; the hairpin causes RNA polymerase to pause and the weak rU:dA base pairs within the RNA:DNA hybrid destabilize the elongation complex, triggering intrinsic termination without requiring the Rho factor.
known_variants: Minimal core variant (~52 bp) used in some synthetic constructs retaining only the hairpin and U-run
Extended variant with additional flanking lambda sequence for increased termination efficiency
known_misannotations: Labeled generically as 'lambda terminator' without specifying t0, causing confusion with other lambda terminators (tL1, tL2, tR1, tR2, tR3)
Sometimes annotated as 'T1 terminator' or 'T7 terminator' in poorly curated vector maps
Occasionally confused with the fd phage terminator or rrnB T1/T2 terminators in automated annotation pipelines
notes: The t0 terminator is placed downstream of the multiple cloning site in pET and related vectors to terminate transcription driven by T7 RNA polymerase or E. coli RNA polymerase, thereby stabilizing mRNA and preventing interference with downstream elements. It is highly effective in E. coli and closely related gram-negative organisms but is not typically used in eukaryotic expression systems. The lambda phage complete genome (J02459) contains the reference sequence.

description: The cat terminator is the rho-independent transcriptional terminator from the chloramphenicol acetyltransferase (cat) gene, originally derived from transposon Tn9. It is used downstream of the cat coding sequence in plasmid vectors to terminate transcription and prevent read-through into flanking sequences. Its inclusion ensures efficient mRNA 3′ processing and stable expression of the chloramphenicol resistance marker.
mechanism: Forms a GC-rich stem-loop hairpin in the nascent RNA immediately followed by a uridine-rich tract, causing RNA polymerase to pause and dissociate from the DNA template via the intrinsic (rho-independent) termination pathway. No additional protein cofactors are required.
known_variants: Tn9-derived canonical form (most common in E. coli vectors)
Synthetic codon-optimized cat with retained native terminator
Truncated terminator retaining only the stem-loop core (~47 bp)
known_misannotations: Often annotated as part of the cat CDS 3′ UTR rather than as a discrete terminator feature
Confused with the Tn10-derived tetracycline resistance terminator in dual-marker vectors such as pACYC184
Sometimes labeled 'CmR terminator' inconsistently with 'cat terminator' on the same plasmid map
Occasionally the entire region from stop codon to terminator end is collapsed into the CDS annotation
notes: The cat gene and its associated terminator originate from Tn9, a class I transposon found in gram-negative bacteria. The terminator functions efficiently in E. coli and related gram-negative hosts; activity in gram-positive or eukaryotic systems is unreliable. In low-copy vectors such as pACYC184 the terminator is important for insulating the cat transcription unit from flanking promoters in the backbone.

description: The rrnG terminator is a Rho-independent transcription terminator derived from the rrnG ribosomal RNA operon of Escherichia coli, one of seven chromosomal rRNA operons. Like other rRNA operon terminators, it consists of tandem T1 and T2 terminator elements that together provide robust transcription termination. It is employed in bacterial expression vectors to prevent transcriptional read-through into downstream sequences and to stabilize mRNA 3′ ends.
mechanism: Functions as an intrinsic (Rho-independent) terminator: the nascent RNA folds into a stable GC-rich hairpin stem-loop structure, causing RNA polymerase to pause, followed by a U-rich tract that weakens the RNA:DNA hybrid and triggers polymerase dissociation from the template.
known_variants: rrnG T1 only (first hairpin element used in isolation)
rrnG T2 only (second hairpin element used in isolation)
rrnG T1T2 tandem (both terminators used together for maximal read-through suppression)
known_misannotations: Confused with the more commonly used rrnB T1 or rrnB T2 terminators; annotators sometimes label any E. coli rRNA terminator as 'rrnB terminator'
Annotated generically as 'rRNA terminator' or 'ribosomal terminator' without specifying the operon (rrnA through rrnH)
T1 and T2 elements of the same operon conflated into a single undifferentiated 'rrnG terminator' annotation
notes: E. coli K-12 encodes seven rRNA operons (rrnA, rrnB, rrnC, rrnD, rrnE, rrnG, rrnH), each carrying tandem T1 and T2 intrinsic terminators. The rrnB T1 and T2 terminators are by far the most widely deployed in synthetic biology and are better biochemically characterized; the rrnG terminator is functionally similar but far less commonly encountered in engineered plasmids. When this annotation appears, confirm whether T1, T2, or both elements are present, as efficiency differs between elements.

description: The bovine growth hormone (bGH) polyadenylation signal is a cis-acting 3' RNA processing element derived from the bovine growth hormone gene, containing the canonical AATAAA hexamer and downstream GT-rich elements required for pre-mRNA cleavage and polyadenylation. It is widely used in mammalian expression vectors to ensure proper 3' end processing of transgene mRNA, which is essential for nuclear export, mRNA stability, and translational efficiency. It is generally preferred over the SV40 poly(A) signal in many contexts due to its strong and constitutive activity in a broad range of mammalian cell types.
mechanism: The AATAAA hexamer is recognized by the cleavage and polyadenylation specificity factor (CPSF), while downstream U/GU-rich elements recruit CstF; together these complexes direct endonucleolytic cleavage ~10–30 nt downstream of the hexamer, followed by poly(A) polymerase-mediated addition of a ~200-nt poly(A) tail.
known_variants: Full-length bGH poly(A) (~250 bp, including downstream flanking sequence)
Minimal bGH poly(A) (~120 bp, AATAAA hexamer + minimal downstream elements only)
Synthetic/codon-context-optimized variants in some commercial vector series
known_misannotations: Occasionally mislabeled as 'SV40 poly(A) signal' when both are present in the same vector backbone
Sometimes annotated as just 'poly(A) signal' without the bGH species designation, causing ambiguity with SV40, HSV TK, or rabbit beta-globin poly(A) signals
The annotated region boundaries vary across databases — some annotations include only the hexamer region while others extend 200+ bp to encompass downstream GT/T-rich elements
Occasionally labeled 'BGH pA' or 'bGHpA' with inconsistent capitalization in third-party vector maps
notes: Exclusive to mammalian expression systems; provides no functional activity in prokaryotic or yeast contexts. Often paired with a CMV or EF1α promoter in mammalian expression cassettes. The bGH poly(A) signal is considered stronger and more consistently active than the SV40 late poly(A) in many stable transfection contexts. Present in nearly all pcDNA-series vectors (Invitrogen/Thermo Fisher), making it one of the most commonly encountered poly(A) signals in deposited plasmid sequences.

description: The SV40 late polyadenylation signal is a cis-acting RNA element derived from Simian Virus 40 that directs cleavage and polyadenylation of precursor mRNA transcripts. It is one of the most widely used polyadenylation signals in mammalian expression vectors because it efficiently terminates transcription and stabilizes mRNA by promoting addition of the poly(A) tail. Its inclusion downstream of a transgene is essential for proper mRNA maturation, nuclear export, translational efficiency, and transcript stability in mammalian cells.
mechanism: The canonical AATAAA hexamer and downstream GU/U-rich elements recruit the cleavage and polyadenylation specificity factor (CPSF) and cleavage stimulation factor (CstF) complexes, which cleave the pre-mRNA ~20 nt downstream of the hexamer and direct poly(A) polymerase to add a poly(A) tail of ~200–250 adenosines. This tail protects the mRNA from exonucleolytic degradation and is recognized by poly(A)-binding protein (PABP) to stimulate translation initiation.
known_variants: SV40 early poly(A) signal — derived from the early transcription unit; shorter and less efficient than the late signal
Minimal SV40 poly(A) — truncated to the core AATAAA hexamer plus ~50 bp downstream elements (~100 bp total); reduced efficiency
Synthetic/codon-context-optimized versions present in some commercial vectors with flanking sequence modifications
known_misannotations: Labeled simply as 'poly(A) signal' without specifying SV40 origin, causing confusion with BGH or hGH poly(A) signals
SV40 early poly(A) and SV40 late poly(A) signals are sometimes both annotated identically as 'SV40 poly(A) signal' despite being distinct elements from different transcription units
Occasionally confused with the SV40 promoter/enhancer element, which is a separate upstream regulatory sequence in the same virus
Some annotations omit the signal entirely, labeling only the downstream SV40 transcription terminator region
notes: The SV40 late poly(A) signal (coordinates ~2533–2729 in J02400) is the standard version used in expression vectors. It is not functional in prokaryotes. In vectors co-containing both an SV40 promoter and SV40 poly(A) signal, care must be taken to verify the orientation and position of each element, as their genomic proximity can cause annotation ambiguity. The BGH poly(A) signal (from bovine growth hormone) is a common functional alternative with similar efficiency in mammalian systems.

aliases: Herpes simplex virus thymidine kinase polyadenylation signal
description: The polyadenylation signal derived from the 3' end of the Herpes Simplex Virus 1 (HSV-1) thymidine kinase (TK) gene. It is widely used in mammalian expression vectors and gene targeting constructs to direct proper mRNA 3' end cleavage and polyadenylation. It is particularly prevalent in negative selection cassettes where the HSV TK ORF and its associated poly(A) signal serve as a counter-selectable marker eliminated by ganciclovir or FIAU treatment.
mechanism: Contains the canonical AATAAA hexanucleotide motif recognized by the cleavage and polyadenylation specificity factor (CPSF), which directs endonucleolytic cleavage of the nascent pre-mRNA approximately 10–30 nucleotides downstream, followed by poly(A) polymerase-mediated addition of the poly(A) tail to stabilize and terminate the transcript.
known_variants: Truncated HSV TK poly(A) (~180 bp) used in compact negative selection cassettes
