Extended version retaining additional 3' HSV-1 genomic flanking sequence for more robust termination
known_misannotations: Entire HSV TK expression cassette (promoter + ORF + poly(A)) annotated as a single 'HSV TK' feature, obscuring the poly(A) element
Labeled generically as 'TK poly(A)' without organism designation, causing confusion with human TK1 or TK2 polyadenylation signals
Confused with SV40 late poly(A) signal in annotations of gene targeting vectors that use both elements
notes: Most commonly encountered as part of the HSV TK negative selection cassette in gene knockout and knock-in targeting vectors; ganciclovir (GCV) or FIAU kills cells that retain the cassette. HSV-1 has an unusually high genomic GC content (~68%), making this poly(A) signal notably GC-rich compared to typical mammalian signals. The poly(A) signal is often co-annotated with the TK ORF and is sometimes absent as an independent annotation even when functionally present.

description: The hGH poly(A) signal is a polyadenylation signal derived from the 3' end of the human growth hormone 1 (GH1) gene on chromosome 17. It is used in mammalian expression vectors to direct efficient cleavage and polyadenylation of mRNA transcripts, ensuring proper 3' end formation, mRNA stability, and nuclear export. It is a popular alternative to the BGH (bovine growth hormone) poly(A) signal in gene therapy, DNA vaccine, and recombinant protein expression vectors.
mechanism: Contains the canonical AATAAA hexanucleotide recognized by the cleavage and polyadenylation specificity factor (CPSF), along with downstream GU/U-rich sequence elements that recruit the cleavage stimulation factor (CstF) and cleavage factors CF I and CF II; this complex directs endonucleolytic cleavage approximately 10–30 nt downstream of the AATAAA sequence, after which poly(A) polymerase adds a ~200-adenosine tail that protects the mRNA from degradation and facilitates ribosome recruitment.
known_variants: Minimal hGH poly(A) (~200 bp fragment containing the core AATAAA hexamer and minimal downstream elements only)
Extended hGH poly(A) (~600–650 bp fragment including additional 3' flanking genomic sequence for enhanced efficiency)
Synthetic hGH poly(A) with point mutations optimizing upstream enhancer elements for higher expression
known_misannotations: Frequently confused with BGH (bovine growth hormone) poly(A) signal, which is from a different species but serves the same function and is similarly sized
Often annotated simply as 'poly(A) signal' or 'polyA' without specifying human GH origin, making automated identification ambiguous
Occasionally mislabeled as 'hGH terminator', conflating polyadenylation with transcriptional termination — these are distinct mechanisms
Some vector maps annotate only the AATAAA hexamer rather than the full functional element
notes: The hGH poly(A) signal is widely used in AAV gene therapy vectors and DNA vaccine constructs where a compact and efficient mammalian poly(A) signal is needed. It is functionally interchangeable with the BGH poly(A) signal in most mammalian contexts but may differ in efficiency across specific cell types. The fragment used in most commercial and research vectors spans from shortly after the GH1 stop codon through ~500–650 bp of downstream genomic sequence. Unlike SV40 poly(A), hGH poly(A) has no known patent encumbrances on its use in research vectors.

description: A polyadenylation signal derived from the β-globin gene (rabbit or human origin) that directs 3′-end processing and polyadenylation of mRNA transcripts in eukaryotic cells. Widely used in mammalian expression vectors to ensure proper mRNA termination, stability, and export downstream of transgenes. Its efficiency and compact size make it a preferred alternative to SV40 and BGH poly(A) signals in many viral and non-viral vector systems.
mechanism: The core AATAAA hexamer is recognized by cleavage and polyadenylation specificity factor (CPSF), which together with CstF and associated cleavage factors directs endonucleolytic cleavage of the pre-mRNA approximately 10–30 nt downstream, followed by poly(A) polymerase-mediated addition of a 150–250 nt poly(A) tail that stabilizes the transcript and promotes nuclear export.
known_variants: Rabbit β-globin poly(A) signal (~250 bp, includes extended downstream sequence; used in Promega pCI series)
Human β-globin poly(A) signal (HBB-derived, functionally equivalent; preferred in clinical AAV/lentiviral vectors)
Minimal β-globin poly(A) signal (~58 bp, core AATAAA plus minimal flanking sequence only)
Synthetic/codon-context-optimized versions with enhanced polyadenylation efficiency reported in some AAV constructs
known_misannotations: Frequently confused with BGH (bovine growth hormone) poly(A) signal, which is completely unrelated in sequence
Species origin (rabbit vs. human) not distinguished — both often labeled simply 'β-globin poly(A)'
Annotated generically as 'polyA' or 'poly(A) signal' without specifying β-globin origin, losing species information
Occasionally mislabeled as 'SV40 poly(A)' in older vector maps when the actual element is β-globin-derived
Sometimes annotated as covering only the AATAAA hexamer rather than the full functional processing element
notes: The rabbit β-globin poly(A) signal is the most widely used form in research expression vectors (e.g., Promega pCI/pCI-neo series). The human β-globin variant is functionally equivalent and preferred in clinical-grade AAV and lentiviral vectors due to reduced immunogenicity concerns. Longer versions (≥125 bp) that include the natural downstream sequence element (DSE) are generally more efficient than minimal 58 bp forms. This element provides no promoter or enhancer activity and is purely a 3′-processing signal.

description: The NOS terminator is the 3' polyadenylation and transcriptional termination signal derived from the nopaline synthase (nos) gene of the Agrobacterium tumefaciens Ti plasmid T-DNA. It is one of the most widely used terminators in plant expression vectors, providing efficient mRNA 3' end processing and transcript stabilization in transformed plant cells. It is routinely paired with constitutive (e.g., CaMV 35S) or inducible promoters to drive stable transgene expression in dicot and monocot plants.
mechanism: The NOS terminator contains a polyadenylation signal and downstream sequence elements that direct endonucleolytic cleavage of the nascent pre-mRNA and addition of a poly(A) tail at the 3' end of the transcript. This 3' processing is required for mRNA nuclear export, stability, and efficient translation in eukaryotic plant cells.
known_variants: Minimal NOS terminator (~180 bp core polyadenylation signal only)
Extended NOS terminator with additional flanking genomic sequence (~400 bp)
Synthetic NOS terminator with codon-context-optimized flanking sequence
known_misannotations: Confused with NOS promoter, which drives nptII expression in the opposite orientation within the same T-DNA region
Labeled as 'NOS 3'UTR' conflating the untranslated region alone with the full terminator/polyadenylation element
Annotated as 'nos poly A' or 'NOS polyadenylation signal' omitting upstream cleavage and terminator elements
Incorrectly attributed to plant genome origin rather than A. tumefaciens Ti plasmid
notes: The NOS terminator functions specifically in plant cells and is not effective as a terminator in bacterial or mammalian expression systems. It originates from the T-DNA of the nopaline-type A. tumefaciens Ti plasmid and was among the first plant-active terminators characterized. In the classic binary vector pBI121, it terminates both the GUS reporter and the nptII selectable marker. Compatible with both Agrobacterium-mediated transformation and biolistic delivery. Works in a wide range of plant species including Arabidopsis, tobacco, tomato, maize, and rice.

description: EGFP (Enhanced Green Fluorescent Protein) is a fluorescent reporter derived from the jellyfish Aequorea victoria, engineered with F64L and S65T substitutions to improve brightness, folding efficiency, and photostability relative to wild-type GFP. It is used ubiquitously in cell biology to visualize protein localization, monitor gene expression, and track cellular dynamics by fusing it to proteins of interest or placing it under a promoter of interest. Its excitation maximum is ~488 nm and emission maximum is ~507 nm, making it compatible with standard FITC filter sets and 488 nm laser lines.
mechanism: EGFP undergoes autocatalytic chromophore formation through cyclization and oxidation of the Ser65-Tyr66-Gly67 tripeptide within its beta-barrel structure, producing a fluorophore that absorbs blue light and emits green fluorescence without requiring any exogenous cofactor or substrate.
known_variants: mEGFP — monomeric EGFP with A206K mutation to prevent dimerization
sfGFP — superfolder GFP with additional mutations for improved folding in harsh contexts
Codon-optimized EGFP for S. cerevisiae (yEGFP)
Codon-optimized EGFP for C. elegans
Nuclear-localized EGFP (EGFP-NLS fusion)
Destabilized EGFP (d1EGFP, d2EGFP) — PEST-tag fusions for short half-life reporter assays
EGFP-tagged versions with organelle-targeting sequences (EGFP-Mito, EGFP-ER)
known_misannotations: Labeled simply as 'GFP' without specifying the enhanced variant — obscures which mutations are present
Annotation region includes upstream CMV or EF1a promoter — the EGFP CDS region is annotated too broadly
Confused with EYFP, ECFP, or mVenus in multi-fluorophore constructs labeled generically as 'GFP'
Stop codon sometimes omitted from annotated length in fusion constructs, causing frame-shift confusion
TagGFP2 (Evrogen commercial variant) sometimes annotated as EGFP despite distinct sequence
notes: EGFP is the industry-standard green fluorescent reporter and is present in thousands of deposited plasmids. The canonical Clontech/Takara sequence (pEGFP backbone) contains a Kozak sequence upstream of the ATG. Chromophore maturation requires molecular oxygen and is significantly slower at temperatures above 37°C; expression in bacteria at 37°C can yield dim cells — growth at 30°C improves fluorescence. EGFP dimerizes weakly at high concentrations; use mEGFP (A206K) for single-molecule or FRET applications. The 'enhanced' designation specifically refers to F64L (improves folding at 37°C) and S65T (red-shifts excitation, increases brightness).

description: EYFP is a yellow-shifted variant of EGFP engineered by introducing the T203Y substitution (along with S65G, V68L, Q69K, and S72A) into Aequorea victoria GFP, producing excitation at ~514 nm and emission at ~527 nm. It is widely used as a fluorescent reporter for gene expression, protein localization, and as the donor or acceptor fluorophore in FRET-based biosensors. Its spectral separation from ECFP makes the CFP/YFP pair the classical FRET pair in cell biology.
mechanism: The T203Y mutation stacks a tyrosine residue against the chromophore's phenolic ring via π–π interactions, stabilizing the excited state dipole and red-shifting both excitation and emission relative to EGFP. Chromophore maturation occurs autocatalytically through cyclization and oxidation of the Ser65-Tyr66-Gly67 tripeptide.
known_variants: Venus (F46L addition to EYFP — faster oxidative maturation, brighter at 37°C)
Citrine (Q69M substitution — improved pH stability and chloride insensitivity)
SYFP2 (F46L/V68L/Q69K optimized variant with improved brightness)
Codon-optimized for mammalian expression (Clontech humanized sequence)
Codon-optimized for yeast (S. cerevisiae preferred codons)
Codon-optimized for plant expression
EYFP(F46L) — intermediate toward Venus with faster maturation
known_misannotations: YFP and EYFP used interchangeably, but YFP may refer to the original non-enhanced version with different mutations
Venus frequently mis-labeled as EYFP despite the additional F46L mutation
Citrine sometimes annotated simply as YFP or EYFP
SYFP2 occasionally deposited as EYFP in sequence records
Older records may annotate the full construct (EYFP fusion) under the EYFP feature, conflating the tag with the POI
notes: EYFP is sensitive to acidic pH (pKa ~6.9) and to chloride ions at physiological concentrations, which can quench fluorescence in acidic organelles; Citrine and Venus address these limitations. Dimerization tendency (Kd ~100 µM) can cause artifacts in fusion protein studies — monomeric variants (A206K mutation) should be used for quantitative FRET or single-molecule work. The Clontech pEYFP series uses a humanized, codon-optimized sequence distinct from the original Aequorea cDNA.

description: ECFP (Enhanced Cyan Fluorescent Protein) is a blue-shifted variant of EGFP derived from Aequorea victoria GFP, generated by the Y66W substitution plus folding-enhancing mutations (F46L, N146I, M153T, V163A). It emits cyan fluorescence with peak excitation at ~434 nm and peak emission at ~477 nm, enabling multicolor live-cell imaging and FRET-based assays. It is widely used as a fusion tag to track protein localization and as the donor fluorophore in CFP–YFP FRET pairs.
mechanism: The Y66W mutation replaces the chromophore tyrosine with tryptophan, shifting the conjugated chromophore system and producing cyan emission (~477 nm) rather than the green emission (~509 nm) of EGFP. Autocatalytic oxidative cyclization of the Trp66-Gly67-Ser65 tripeptide upon protein folding forms the mature fluorescent chromophore without requiring cofactors.
known_variants: Cerulean — W66 chromophore + S72A/Y145A for improved brightness and reduced excited-state heterogeneity
CyPet — directed-evolution variant optimized for FRET pairing with YPet
mCerulean — monomeric Cerulean via A206K mutation
mCerulean3 — further brightness-optimized monomeric cyan FP
mTurquoise / mTurquoise2 — highest quantum yield monomeric cyan FPs for modern use
SCFP3A — structurally improved cyan FP with increased quantum yield
Codon-optimized ECFP — Clontech/Takara humanized coding sequence for improved mammalian expression
known_misannotations: Labeled simply 'CFP', ambiguously conflating non-enhanced CFP (lower brightness) with ECFP
Confused with Cerulean, which carries additional brightness-improving mutations beyond ECFP
Annotated as 'GFP' in older records where the specific spectral variant was not tracked
Mislabeled 'mCFP' — no established monomeric CFP exists under that name
Confused with mTurquoise2 in FRET constructs where the donor is described generically as 'ECFP'
notes: ECFP has relatively low brightness (extinction coefficient ~26,000 M⁻¹cm⁻¹, quantum yield ~0.40) and moderate photostability compared to successors such as mTurquoise2 (QY ~0.93); for new constructs mTurquoise2 or mCerulean3 are generally preferred. The label 'ECFP' persists in FRET nomenclature even when the actual fluorophore in use is Cerulean or mTurquoise2. Protein MW is ~27 kDa. Dimerization tendency (inherited from wtGFP A206) can cause artifacts in fusion proteins; use monomeric variants for oligomerization-sensitive applications.

description: mCherry is a monomeric red fluorescent protein engineered from mRFP1 (itself derived from Discosoma sp. DsRed) via directed evolution, exhibiting excitation maximum at 587 nm and emission maximum at 610 nm. It is widely used as a transcriptional reporter, fusion protein tag for localization studies, and partner in multi-color imaging owing to its spectral separation from GFP-family proteins. Its fast maturation, photostability, and monomeric structure make it one of the most broadly deployed fluorescent reporters across model systems.
mechanism: The mCherry chromophore forms autocatalytically from a Gln65-Tyr66-Gly67 tripeptide through cyclization followed by oxidation, creating a planar conjugated system that absorbs and emits in the red spectrum. As an obligate monomer, mCherry avoids the oligomerization-induced artifacts that complicate use of tetrameric DsRed in fusion protein studies.
known_variants: mCherry2 — brighter variant with improved photostability (Shen et al. 2017)
sfmCherry (super-folder mCherry) — enhanced folding in harsh environments or as fusion partner
mCherry-PEST — destabilized version with C-terminal PEST sequence for short half-life reporter assays
Human codon-optimized mCherry — rewritten codons for high mammalian expression
Plant codon-optimized mCherry — optimized for Arabidopsis and other plant hosts
mCherry-NLS — nuclear-targeted fusion for chromatin and transcription studies
mCherry-H2B — histone fusion for nuclear/chromatin imaging
known_misannotations: Generically labeled 'RFP' — mCherry is often annotated simply as 'RFP', conflating it with mRFP1, DsRed-Monomer, or other red FPs
Confused with mRFP1 — the direct engineered precursor to mCherry; distinct protein but sometimes used interchangeably in older annotations
Mislabeled as DsRed or DsRed-Monomer — ancestral tetrameric protein; not equivalent to mCherry
Confused with TagRFP — unrelated red FP from Entacmaea quadricolor used by Evrogen; different sequence and properties
Confused with tdTomato — tandem-dimer red FP with similar emission peak but ~2× the size; separate gene
Labeled mScarlet in some automated annotations — brighter but distinct red FP developed later
notes: mCherry requires molecular oxygen for chromophore maturation and will not fluoresce in strictly anaerobic environments. Chromophore maturation is relatively fast (~15 min at 37°C) compared to other red FPs. The 587/610 nm excitation/emission profile is compatible with standard TRITC or mCherry filter sets. Because it is a true monomer it is the preferred choice for N- and C-terminal fusions where oligomerization would perturb biology. mCherry photobleaches faster than some newer red FPs (e.g., mScarlet-I, mRuby3); for long-term live imaging applications, evaluate mCherry2 or mScarlet as alternatives.

description: mRFP (monomeric Red Fluorescent Protein) is an engineered monomeric derivative of the tetrameric DsRed protein from the coral Discosoma sp., carrying 33 mutations that disrupt the tetramer interface. It emits red fluorescence with excitation maximum at ~584 nm and emission maximum at ~607 nm, enabling its use as a spectrally distinct partner to GFP. It is widely used as a transcriptional reporter, fusion tag for protein localization studies, and dual-reporter assay component in bacteria, yeast, and mammalian systems.
mechanism: The chromophore forms via autocatalytic cyclization and oxidation of the Gln65-Tyr66-Gly67 tripeptide within the beta-barrel scaffold, yielding a conjugated system that absorbs and emits in the red range. The 33 mutations relative to DsRed break obligate tetramerization while preserving chromophore maturation, making it suitable as a monomeric fusion partner without aggregation artifacts.
known_variants: mRFP1 — original published monomer (Campbell et al. 2002), 33 mutations relative to DsRed
Codon-optimized mRFP for enhanced expression in E. coli or mammalian cells
mCherry — brighter, faster-maturing successor derived from mRFP1 by directed evolution (Shaner et al. 2004)
known_misannotations: Annotated generically as 'RFP', conflating it with DsRed, mCherry, mStrawberry, or other red fluorescent proteins
Confused with DsRed: DsRed is an obligate tetramer unsuitable for most fusion applications; mRFP is the monomeric derivative and should be annotated separately
Mislabeled as mCherry (or vice versa) in older plasmid stocks; the two differ significantly in brightness, photostability, pKa, and emission maximum (~607 nm vs ~610 nm)
notes: mRFP1 matures more slowly than mCherry (~60 min vs ~15 min at 37°C), which can complicate live-cell kinetics experiments requiring rapid reporter response. It was largely superseded by mCherry (2004) and subsequently by mScarlet variants for most applications due to superior quantum yield and photostability. Many legacy plasmid backbones in common use still carry mRFP1 rather than newer alternatives. Not recommended as a FRET acceptor without careful spectral characterization due to its broad excitation spectrum overlapping with common donors.

description: TagRFP-T is a monomeric, highly photostable red fluorescent protein derived from the sea anemone Entactinya quadricolor, engineered from the parental TagRFP by introducing the S158T point substitution. It is widely used as a genetically encoded reporter for protein fusion tagging, organelle labeling, and long-term live-cell imaging where photobleaching is a concern. Its monomeric nature prevents artifactual protein clustering that occurs with dimeric or tetrameric RFPs.
mechanism: TagRFP-T forms a red-shifted fluorophore through autocatalytic cyclization and oxidation of residues within its beta-barrel core, yielding excitation and emission maxima of approximately 555 nm and 584 nm, respectively. The S158T substitution stabilizes the chromophore micro-environment, dramatically reducing the photobleaching rate relative to the parental TagRFP without substantially altering brightness or spectral properties.
known_variants: TagRFP (parental protein, S158 wild-type, less photostable)
Codon-optimized TagRFP-T for mammalian expression (Evrogen)
N-terminal fusion variant (pTagRFP-T-N1 series)
C-terminal fusion variant (pTagRFP-T-C1 series)
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'TagRFP' without the -T suffix, losing photostability variant information
Generically labeled 'RFP', discarding all variant-specific metadata
Confused with TurboRFP, a related but distinct Evrogen red FP
Confused with other Tag-series FPs (TagBFP, TagCFP, TagGFP2, TagYFP) which share the same protein scaffold family
notes: TagRFP-T was described by Shaner et al. (2008, Nature Methods) as one of the most photostable monomeric red FPs available at the time of publication, outperforming mCherry in photostability under many imaging conditions. The '-T' suffix specifically denotes the S158T mutation. Evrogen (Moscow) commercializes TagRFP-T and distributes the standard expression vectors. When used as a fusion tag, linker length should be optimized as the beta-barrel structure can sterically affect some fusion partners. Not suitable for use in systems requiring far-red or near-infrared reporters.

description: mTurquoise2 is a monomeric cyan fluorescent protein (CFP) engineered by Goedhart et al. (2012) from the mTurquoise scaffold, achieving the highest quantum yield (~0.93) of any CFP variant. It is widely used as a fluorescent reporter for subcellular localization studies, fusion protein tagging, and as a FRET donor paired with yellow fluorescent proteins such as Venus or mVenus.
mechanism: mTurquoise2 adopts the canonical GFP beta-barrel fold with an autocatalytically formed chromophore (excitation ~434 nm, emission ~474 nm); engineered mutations stabilize the chromophore environment, yielding a near-unity quantum yield and monoexponential fluorescence lifetime (~3.8 ns) that is particularly suited for FLIM-FRET measurements.
known_variants: mTurquoise (predecessor; lower quantum yield ~0.84, biexponential lifetime)
mCerulean3 (parallel CFP engineering lineage with comparable brightness)
codon-optimized for human/mammalian expression
codon-optimized for yeast (S. cerevisiae)
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'mTurquoise' without the '2' suffix — a distinct protein with meaningfully lower quantum yield and biexponential lifetime
Labeled as 'CFP' or 'ECFP' — much older and dimmer variants with different spectral properties
Confused with 'Cerulean' or 'mCerulean' — related CFP lineage but distinct mutations and performance characteristics
notes: mTurquoise2 is the recommended CFP for quantitative FRET experiments, especially FLIM-based approaches, due to its monoexponential decay and very high quantum yield. The protein is monomeric and does not dimerize at physiological concentrations, making it safe for fusion-protein studies. Codon-optimized sequences are strongly recommended for mammalian and plant expression. The high quantum yield means it is also exceptionally bright for simple imaging applications.

description: mVenus is a monomeric yellow fluorescent protein derived from Venus, an enhanced EYFP variant engineered from Aequorea victoria GFP. It produces bright yellow-green fluorescence (excitation max ~515 nm, emission max ~528 nm) and is widely used as a transcriptional reporter, fusion tag for live-cell imaging, and FRET acceptor paired with cyan fluorescent proteins. The A206K substitution eliminates the weak homodimerization tendency of the parent Venus protein, making mVenus suitable for fusion protein studies where artifactual clustering would confound interpretation.
mechanism: The chromophore forms spontaneously via cyclization and oxidation of a Thr-Tyr-Gly tripeptide within the 11-stranded beta-barrel scaffold, requiring only molecular oxygen as a cofactor. Venus carries mutations F46L, M153T, V163A, and S175G relative to EYFP that together accelerate oxidative maturation, improve brightness, and shift the pKa to approximately 6.0.
known_variants: Venus — parent non-monomeric form, prone to weak dimerization at high concentrations
mVenus (A206K) — monomerized by A206K substitution, preferred for fusion protein studies
mVenus-PEST — C-terminal PEST degron tag added for short-half-life reporter assays
Citrine (Q69M) — related bright YFP variant with improved acid and chloride resistance
mVenus-NLS / mVenus-NES — trafficking-tagged versions for subcellular localization studies
known_misannotations: Generically labeled 'YFP' without specifying Venus or mVenus, conflating multiple distinct variants
Confused with Citrine, which has nearly identical spectral properties but different mutations and pH behavior
Dimeric Venus and monomeric mVenus used interchangeably in annotations despite different oligomerization states
Annotated as 'EYFP' in older plasmid records, obscuring the Venus-specific maturation mutations
notes: mVenus fluorescence is pH-sensitive (pKa ~6.0) and dims noticeably in acidic compartments such as lysosomes and late endosomes; use pH-stable variants (e.g., mNeonGreen) for trafficking studies through the endolysosomal pathway. For FRET experiments, mCerulean3 or mTurquoise2 are the standard cyan donor partners. The F46L mutation significantly speeds chromophore maturation relative to EYFP, making mVenus practical in rapidly dividing bacteria. Codon-optimized versions exist for mammalian, yeast, and plant expression and may differ in GC content and restriction site composition.

description: mNeonGreen is a bright monomeric green fluorescent protein derived by engineering the tetrameric yellow-green fluorescent protein LanYFP from the lancelet Branchiostoma lanceolatum. It exhibits excitation and emission maxima at ~506 nm and ~517 nm, respectively, and is among the brightest and most photostable monomeric green fluorescent proteins available. It is widely used as a fusion tag and transcriptional reporter across prokaryotic and eukaryotic systems.
mechanism: The mNeonGreen barrel folds around a chromophore formed by autocatalytic cyclization and oxidation of residues Thr-Tyr-Gly (positions 63–65), producing a fluorophore that absorbs blue light and emits green fluorescence without requiring exogenous cofactors.
known_variants: mNeonGreen2 — second-generation variant with improved brightness and photostability (Allele Biotechnology)
mNeonGreen-Tandem (2×, 3×) — head-to-tail repeats for enhanced fluorescence intensity
Human codon-optimized mNeonGreen for improved mammalian expression
E. coli codon-optimized mNeonGreen for prokaryotic expression
known_misannotations: Annotated generically as 'GFP' or 'EGFP' due to similar spectral properties and green color
Abbreviated as 'mNG' which is occasionally mis-expanded as 'mNeoGreen' (missing 'n')
Confused with mGreen or other engineered GFP variants in database submissions that lack proper provenance
Tetrameric ancestor LanYFP sometimes conflated with the monomeric mNeonGreen in older records
notes: Developed by Shaner et al. (2013, Nature Methods) at Allele Biotechnology. Notably derived from a non-Aequorea cephalochordate source, making it phylogenetically distinct from GFP-family proteins. Its monomeric form is critical for fusion-protein applications where GFP oligomerization would cause artifacts. pKa ~5.7 makes it suitable for slightly acidic compartments. Allele Biotechnology offers commercial licensing; check terms before distribution in products.

description: iRFP (infrared fluorescent protein) is a near-infrared fluorescent protein engineered from the bacteriophytochrome RpBphP2 of Rhodopseudomonas palustris, with excitation/emission maxima of ~690/713 nm. It uses biliverdin IXα as a covalently bound chromophore, which is an endogenous metabolite in mammalian cells, eliminating the need for exogenous cofactor supplementation in animal imaging applications. iRFP is widely used for non-invasive in vivo imaging because near-infrared light penetrates tissue more deeply than visible wavelengths and avoids autofluorescence.
mechanism: iRFP autocatalytically incorporates biliverdin IXα (a heme catabolite produced by heme oxygenase) into its PAS-GAF chromophore-binding domain via a thioether bond to a conserved cysteine residue, producing stable near-infrared fluorescence without requiring molecular oxygen for chromophore maturation.
known_variants: iRFP670 — blue-shifted variant (ex/em 643/670 nm), dimeric
iRFP682 — dimeric, intermediate wavelength (ex/em 663/682 nm)
iRFP702 — dimeric, red-shifted (ex/em 673/702 nm)
iRFP720 — red-shifted dimeric variant (ex/em 702/720 nm)
miRFP670 — monomeric version for fusion tagging, engineered from BrBphP
miRFP703 — monomeric, brighter variant for mammalian cell imaging
miRFP713 — monomeric iRFP713-equivalent with improved brightness
codon-optimized iRFP — human-codon-optimized for enhanced mammalian expression
known_misannotations: Conflation of dimeric iRFP with monomeric miRFP variants — iRFP is an obligate dimer and unsuitable for protein fusion tagging without linker engineering
Labeling as 'iRFP' without specifying spectral variant (iRFP670/682/702/713/720) leads to ambiguity in multi-color experiments
Annotation as standard GFP-like fluorescent protein, missing the requirement for biliverdin chromophore — maturation is oxygen-independent but biliverdin-dependent
Confusion with iFluo or other near-IR dyes; iRFP is a genetically encoded protein, not a chemical dye
notes: iRFP requires biliverdin IXα as chromophore; mammalian and animal cells supply this endogenously via heme oxygenase-1/2. In E. coli and yeast, biliverdin must be supplemented exogenously or co-expressed with a heme oxygenase (e.g., HO1). Unlike GFP-family proteins, iRFP maturation does not consume oxygen, making it useful in hypoxic environments. iRFP is an obligate homodimer and should not be used as a direct fusion tag without monomeric variants (miRFP series). The full iRFP family spans 670–720 nm emission and can be multiplexed with visible fluorescent proteins in spectral imaging experiments.

description: Blue Fluorescent Protein (BFP) is a variant of Aequorea victoria GFP bearing a Y66H chromophore mutation that shifts emission to ~440–460 nm blue light. It is used as a fluorescent reporter for gene expression, protein localization, and multi-color imaging alongside green, yellow, and red fluorescent proteins. Improved variants such as EBFP2 and TagBFP offer substantially increased brightness and photostability over the original BFP.
mechanism: The Y66H mutation replaces the tyrosine in GFP's chromophore tripeptide (Ser65-Tyr66-Gly67) with histidine, forming an imidazole-based chromophore that absorbs at ~380–400 nm and emits blue fluorescence (~440–460 nm) upon UV or violet light excitation.
known_variants: EBFP (Enhanced BFP): F64L/Y66H double mutant with improved folding and brightness over original BFP
EBFP2: additional mutations (A22G, K26R, L221V, and others) yield ~3× brighter signal than EBFP with markedly improved photostability
Azurite: Y66H-based variant engineered for improved brightness and reduced pH sensitivity
TagBFP: structurally unrelated monomeric blue FP from Entacmaea quadricolor sea anemone, far brighter than EBFP2
mTagBFP2: improved monomeric version of TagBFP with superior photostability
Codon-optimized versions for mammalian, yeast, insect, or bacterial expression
known_misannotations: BFP is frequently confused with CFP (Cyan Fluorescent Protein, Y66W mutation), which has distinct spectral properties (~475 nm emission) and different imaging filter requirements
EBFP or EBFP2 plasmids often annotated simply as 'BFP', obscuring which generation variant is present
TagBFP (derived from sea anemone) sometimes labeled as BFP despite being a structurally unrelated protein from a different organism
Y66H GFP mutant occasionally appears in databases annotated as 'GFP mutant' without indicating the blue spectral shift
ECFP sometimes labeled BFP in older literature predating standardized fluorescent protein nomenclature
notes: The original BFP (Y66H GFP) is largely obsolete due to low brightness (QY ~0.24) and poor photostability; modern experiments favor EBFP2 or TagBFP. All BFP variants require UV or violet excitation (~350–405 nm), which can cause phototoxicity and autofluorescence in live-cell imaging — a significant drawback compared to longer-wavelength reporters. TagBFP, despite sharing the 'BFP' label, is encoded by a completely different gene from a different organism and should be distinguished in annotations. BFP is spectrally well-separated from GFP/EGFP, making it useful for two-color experiments in bacteria and yeast where CFP/GFP bleed-through is problematic.

description: Citrine is a yellow fluorescent protein (YFP) variant derived from enhanced YFP (EYFP) via a single Q69M substitution, engineered by Griesbeck et al. (2001). It exhibits improved pH stability (pKa ~5.7 vs ~6.9 for EYFP) and markedly reduced chloride sensitivity, making it more reliable for imaging in acidic compartments. It is widely used as a fluorescent reporter, fusion tag, and FRET acceptor paired with CFP donors.
mechanism: Citrine undergoes autocatalytic chromophore maturation, forming a p-hydroxybenzylidene-imidazolinone fluorophore from the Thr65-Tyr66-Gly67 tripeptide within its beta-barrel scaffold; the Q69M mutation repositions a water molecule near the chromophore, shielding it from protonation and anion binding. Excitation peak is ~516 nm and emission peak is ~529 nm.
known_variants: Codon-optimized for human expression (humanized Citrine)
Codon-optimized for plant expression
Citrine-tagged fusion constructs with nuclear localization signal (NLS-Citrine)
Dimeric Citrine (dCitrine) for specific localization studies
Citrine-CAAX for membrane targeting
known_misannotations: Frequently labeled generically as 'YFP' or 'EYFP', obscuring the Q69M identity and improved properties
Confused with Venus (another pH-improved YFP) — Venus carries different mutations (F46L, M153T, V163A, S175G) and is a distinct protein
Sometimes annotated as 'GFP' when only excitation/emission filters are documented without sequence verification
FRET constructs using Citrine as acceptor may be annotated with the donor name only (e.g., 'CFP-FRET construct'), hiding Citrine identity
notes: Citrine was described in Griesbeck et al. (2001) J Biol Chem 276:29188–29194. Its pKa of ~5.7 makes it suitable for imaging in mildly acidic organelles (endosomes, secretory vesicles) where EYFP is unreliable. It is a preferred FRET acceptor with CFP or CyPet. Monomeric variants should be confirmed by sequence when oligomeric state matters, as wild-type GFP-family proteins weakly dimerize at high concentrations. For FRET applications, distinguish carefully from Venus, which has faster maturation but different spectral properties.

description: mTagBFP2 is an engineered monomeric blue fluorescent protein derived from the sea anemone Actinia equina, with excitation maximum at ~399 nm and emission maximum at ~454 nm. It is an improved variant of mTagBFP with enhanced brightness, photostability, and reduced cellular cytotoxicity compared to earlier BFP variants. It is widely used as a fusion tag, transcriptional reporter, and subcellular marker in multicolor fluorescence imaging experiments.
mechanism: mTagBFP2 adopts a beta-barrel fold enclosing an autocatalytic chromophore formed by cyclization and oxidation of a His-Tyr-Gly tripeptide (positions 63–65), yielding a chromophore with an unusually short absorption maximum in the violet range (~399 nm) that produces blue fluorescence upon excitation.
known_variants: mTagBFP (direct precursor, slightly lower brightness and photostability)
TagBFP (non-monomeric parental protein from Actinia equina)
Mammalian codon-optimized mTagBFP2
E. coli codon-optimized mTagBFP2
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'mTagBFP' (the older, dimmer precursor variant — distinct sequence)
Generically annotated as 'BFP', losing variant identity and conflating with EBFP
Confused with EBFP2, which is GFP-derived and spectrally similar but structurally unrelated
Labeled as 'TagBFP' omitting the monomeric designation, incorrectly implying oligomerization tendency
notes: mTagBFP2 is derived from Actinia equina rather than Aequorea victoria GFP, making it fully orthogonal in sequence to GFP-family reporters and ideal for multicolor imaging alongside GFP, YFP, and mCherry. Its ~454 nm emission is well-separated from GFP (~509 nm), enabling clean spectral unmixing. It matures efficiently at 37°C and is among the brightest and most photostable blue FPs available. The 'Tag' naming reflects its origin from the TagRFP lineage; the 'm' prefix confirms engineered monomericity, which is critical for faithful fusion-protein localization studies.

description: mEmerald is a monomeric green fluorescent protein (GFP) variant engineered from EGFP with enhanced photostability and reduced tendency to dimerize. It is widely used as a fluorescent fusion tag to track protein localization, dynamics, and expression in live cells. The A206K substitution relative to EGFP abolishes the weak dimerization seen with standard EGFP, making it preferable for fusions where oligomerization would perturb target protein behavior.
mechanism: mEmerald autocatalytically forms a chromophore from an internal Ser-Tyr-Gly tripeptide within a beta-barrel scaffold, requiring only molecular oxygen. Upon excitation at ~487 nm it emits green fluorescence with a peak at ~509 nm (quantum yield ~0.68, extinction coefficient ~57,500 M⁻¹cm⁻¹).
known_variants: Codon-optimized for human/mammalian expression
Codon-optimized for E. coli expression
C-terminal fusion version (linker variants)
N-terminal fusion version (linker variants)
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'EGFP' or generic 'GFP' — mEmerald has distinct mutations (e.g., S72A, N149K, M153T, I167T, A206K) beyond standard EGFP F64L/S65T
Confused with non-monomeric 'Emerald' (which lacks the A206K monomerizing substitution)
Annotated as 'eGFP' despite having different spectral and oligomerization properties
notes: mEmerald was characterized by Shaner et al. (2005, Nature Methods) as part of a systematic survey of fluorescent protein properties. Its improved photostability over EGFP makes it a better choice for long time-lapse imaging. The monomeric character (A206K) is critical when fusing to proteins that must not artificially dimerize. It folds efficiently at 37°C, making it suitable for mammalian cell imaging without temperature-shift tricks.

description: tdTomato is a tandem dimer red fluorescent protein (excitation max ~554 nm, emission max ~581 nm) engineered by linking two copies of dTomato in tandem, making it one of the brightest red fluorescent proteins available. It is widely used as a cell lineage tracer, fusion tag reporter, and counterpart to GFP in dual-reporter experiments. Its obligate dimeric structure (as a single polypeptide tandem dimer) avoids intermolecular aggregation while retaining the brightness advantage of dimeric fluorophores.
mechanism: tdTomato folds into a beta-barrel structure enclosing a chromophore formed by autocatalytic cyclization and oxidation of the Gln-Tyr-Gly tripeptide at positions 66–68 of each dTomato monomer unit; the tandem arrangement keeps both chromophore-bearing barrels on a single chain, yielding very high effective brightness (extinction coefficient ~138,000 M⁻¹cm⁻¹, quantum yield ~0.69).
known_variants: Human codon-optimized tdTomato (most common form in mammalian expression vectors)
mTdTomato — engineered monomeric variant with reduced brightness but improved for tight fusion tagging
Nuclear-targeted tdTomato (H2B-tdTomato fusion)
Membrane-targeted tdTomato (CAAX-box or GAP-43 signal fusion)
known_misannotations: Annotated simply as 'dTomato' (the monomeric precursor, ~711 bp) when the actual insert is the full tandem dimer (~1449 bp)
Labeled 'DsRed' or 'DsRed2' in older annotations due to shared DsRed evolutionary origin
Called 'Tomato' without indicating tandem-dimer vs. monomer status, creating confusion with mTomato
Occasionally mis-annotated as 'RFP' generically, obscuring the specific spectral properties
notes: tdTomato was developed by Shaner et al. (2004, Nat Biotechnol 22:1567–72) in the Tsien lab at UCSD. Because it is an obligate tandem dimer on a single polypeptide, it cannot be used as a tight N- or C-terminal fusion tag for proteins where dimerization would disrupt function — use mCherry or mScarlet for that purpose. tdTomato is however excellent as a free cytoplasmic or nuclear reporter. The Ai9/Ai14 Cre-reporter mouse lines use Rosa26-CAG-tdTomato and are a widely distributed reference for in vivo lineage tracing.

description: mKate2 is a monomeric far-red fluorescent protein (excitation 588 nm, emission 633 nm) derived from Entacmaea quadricolor eqFP578 via iterative directed evolution through Katushka and mKate. It provides ~3-fold improved brightness and superior photostability over its predecessor mKate, with faster chromophore maturation. It is widely used as a far-red channel reporter in multicolor imaging, flow cytometry, and in vivo studies due to its spectral separation from GFP, YFP, and common RFPs.
mechanism: mKate2 autocatalytically forms its chromophore through cyclization and oxidation of a Leu-Tyr-Gly tripeptide (residues 63–65 in the beta-barrel fold), requiring only molecular oxygen as a cofactor. The extended pi-conjugation of the chromophore shifts emission to the far-red range (~633 nm).
known_variants: mKate (predecessor — dimmer, slower maturation, less photostable)
mKate2 codon-optimized for human/mammalian expression
H2B-mKate2 (nuclear-targeted histone fusion)
mKate2-CAAX (plasma membrane-targeted via CAAX motif)
mKate2-Mito (mitochondria-targeted via COX8 signal peptide)
mKate2-Endo (endosome-targeted)
known_misannotations: Labeled 'mKate' when sequence is actually the improved mKate2 variant
Confused with Katushka or TurboFP635 (dimeric far-red FPs from the same parent lineage)
Confused with mPlum, iRFP, or iRFP713 (unrelated near-infrared fluorescent proteins)
Annotated as TagFP635 (a related but distinct Evrogen product designation)
Confusion with dTomato or mCherry in poorly labeled far-red clone stocks
notes: mKate2 requires molecular oxygen for chromophore maturation and will not fluoresce under strict anaerobic conditions. Its pKa of ~5.4 makes it relatively stable at neutral pH but signal is reduced in acidic compartments (e.g., lysosomes). Monomeric at physiological concentrations, making it suitable for fusion tags without artifactual dimerization. The far-red emission (~633 nm) is compatible with standard 633/647 nm laser lines in confocal and flow cytometry instruments. Published by Shcherbo et al. (2009) Nature Methods 6:321–323.

description: Clover is a monomeric, bright green fluorescent protein engineered from the Aequorea victoria GFP lineage, developed by Lam et al. (2012, Nature Methods) for improved FRET dynamic range. It was specifically designed as a high-brightness FRET donor paired with the red fluorescent protein mRuby2. Clover is widely used as a genetically encoded reporter for protein localization, live-cell imaging, and FRET-based biosensors in mammalian systems.
mechanism: Clover undergoes autocatalytic chromophore formation from a Thr-Tyr-Gly tripeptide (analogous to the Ser-Tyr-Gly chromophore of EGFP), producing an excitation peak near 505 nm and emission near 515 nm. Its monomeric nature and improved quantum yield (~0.76) relative to EGFP make it effective as an energy donor in FRET pairs.
known_variants: mClover3 — further engineered monomeric variant with improved brightness and photostability (Bajar et al. 2016)
Clover-NLS — nuclear localization signal fusion for nuclear reporting
Clover-H2B — histone H2B fusion for chromatin labeling
known_misannotations: Sometimes annotated as 'EGFP' in vector maps because it shares the GFP scaffold; Clover has distinct spectral and brightness properties
Confused with 'mClover3' — mClover3 is a later, distinct improved variant, not the same as the original Clover
Occasionally labeled 'GFP' generically in plasmid databases, obscuring its FRET-optimized properties
notes: Clover was codon-optimized for mammalian expression and performs best in mammalian and insect cell systems. Its primary value is as a bright, monomeric FRET donor; for simple green reporting without FRET, EGFP or mEGFP may be preferred due to wider availability of validated constructs. mClover3 (Bajar et al. 2016, Scientific Reports) supersedes Clover for most applications, offering higher brightness and photostability. Clover should not be confused with the four-leaf clover motif in protein structure literature.

description: mRuby2 is a second-generation monomeric red fluorescent protein derived from the sea anemone eqFP611 (Entacmaea quadricolor), engineered for improved brightness and photostability over the original mRuby. It emits in the red spectrum (excitation ~559 nm, emission ~600 nm) and is widely used as a transcriptional or translational reporter and for protein-fusion tagging in multicolor imaging experiments. Its strict monomeric character minimizes aggregation artifacts relative to dimeric or tetrameric RFPs.
mechanism: Like other GFP-family proteins, mRuby2 undergoes spontaneous autocatalytic cyclization and oxidation of an internal tripeptide to form a conjugated chromophore within its 11-stranded beta-barrel scaffold, producing red-shifted fluorescence without requiring any exogenous cofactors or substrates.
known_variants: mRuby (original predecessor, ~3-fold lower cellular brightness)
mRuby3 (subsequent variant with improved folding efficiency and brightness)
Human codon-optimized mRuby2 (for mammalian expression)
E. coli codon-optimized mRuby2 (for prokaryotic expression)
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'mRuby' (the original, dimmer predecessor) when the improved mRuby2 is present
Generically annotated as 'RFP', losing spectral and performance distinction from mCherry, mScarlet, or tdTomato
Confused with mRuby3 in vectors that carry the further-optimized successor
Sometimes listed as eqFP611 derivative without specifying the mRuby2 generation
notes: mRuby2 offers roughly 3-fold higher cellular brightness than the original mRuby and is fully monomeric, making it well-suited for tagging proteins where dimerization would perturb function. For new designs, mRuby3 (a further-improved variant) should be considered as it provides even higher brightness. The ~600 nm emission is spectrally separable from GFP/YFP channels, enabling dual- or multicolor reporter experiments. Chromophore maturation requires molecular oxygen and may be slower at low temperatures or in anaerobic conditions.

description: Firefly luciferase (luc+) is a codon-optimized variant of the bioluminescent reporter enzyme from Photinus pyralis, engineered by Promega for enhanced expression in mammalian cells. It catalyzes ATP-dependent oxidation of D-luciferin to produce light (~550–570 nm), enabling quantitative, highly sensitive measurement of promoter activity, gene regulation, and signal transduction. It is among the most widely used reporters in molecular biology due to its large dynamic range, low background, and compatibility with dual-luciferase assays when paired with Renilla luciferase.
mechanism: Firefly luciferase catalyzes a two-step reaction: first adenylating D-luciferin with ATP to form luciferyl-AMP, then oxidizing it in the presence of O2 and Mg2+ to generate oxyluciferin and a photon at ~560 nm. The reaction is strictly ATP-dependent, making luminescence output proportional to cellular energy status and transcriptional activity.
known_variants: luc (original wild-type Photinus pyralis coding sequence, not codon-optimized)
luc+ (codon-optimized for mammalian expression; reduced CpG and ATTTA motifs; used in pGL3 vectors)
luc2 (further optimized vs luc+; eliminated peroxisomal targeting signal, used in pGL4 vectors)
luc2P (destabilized with PEST domain for dynamic expression studies)
luc2CP (destabilized with both CL1 and PEST sequences for rapid turnover)
Infra-Red luciferase (CBRluc, click beetle red luciferase — related but distinct enzyme at 615 nm)
known_misannotations: luc vs luc+: these are distinct sequences; luc is the wild-type P. pyralis gene while luc+ is codon-optimized — often used interchangeably in annotations
luc2 vs luc+: pGL4 vectors use luc2, not luc+; the two are frequently conflated in plasmid annotations
Renilla luciferase (hRluc/rLuc) confused with firefly luciferase — mechanistically unrelated enzymes from different organisms, different substrates (coelenterazine vs D-luciferin)
NanoLuc confused with luc+: NanoLuc is an engineered ~19 kDa enzyme with different substrate (furimazine) and far higher specific activity
Peroxisomal targeting: the original luc protein contains a C-terminal SKL peroxisomal targeting signal absent in luc2; annotations do not always distinguish
notes: luc+ was specifically engineered to remove sequences that reduce expression in mammalian cells (e.g., cryptic splice sites, CpG dinucleotides, ATTTA instability motifs) relative to the wild-type Photinus pyralis luc gene. Light output requires exogenous D-luciferin substrate. Firefly luciferase is temperature-sensitive and its activity is suppressed at >37°C, relevant for fever-model mammalian assays. Dual-luciferase assays commonly co-express firefly and Renilla luciferases as experimental and normalization reporters, respectively (e.g., Promega Dual-Luciferase Reporter Assay System).

description: Renilla luciferase is a bioluminescent reporter enzyme derived from the sea pansy Renilla reniformis that oxidizes coelenterazine to produce blue light (~480 nm emission). It is widely used as a normalization control in dual-luciferase reporter assays paired with firefly luciferase (Fluc), enabling ratiometric correction for transfection efficiency and cell viability. Unlike firefly luciferase, Rluc does not require ATP, making it suitable for measuring activity in diverse cellular compartments.
mechanism: Rluc catalyzes the ATP-independent oxidative decarboxylation of coelenterazine to coelenteramide and CO2, releasing photons at ~480 nm. The reaction proceeds via a dioxetanone intermediate and does not require luciferin, ATP, or Mg2+.
known_variants: hRluc — humanized/codon-optimized for mammalian expression, increased GC content (~50%)
RLuc8 — engineered variant with 8 mutations for enhanced thermostability and ~4-fold increased brightness
RLuc8.6 — red-shifted RLuc8 derivative emitting at ~535 nm for reduced tissue autofluorescence
Rluc-PEST — destabilized by PEST degradation sequence for short-lived promoter activity measurements
NanoLuc — distantly related engineered enzyme; often confused with Rluc but is a distinct protein
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'luciferase' without Renilla/firefly distinction, creating ambiguity with Fluc
Confused with NanoLuc (Nluc), which is a distinct engineered enzyme not derived from Renilla
hRluc often annotated as 'Rluc' despite significant codon differences from the native gene
Annotated as 'luc' — the gene symbol typically reserved for firefly luciferase
notes: Renilla luciferase requires exogenous coelenterazine substrate addition for activity; substrate must be supplied to cells immediately before reading. Coelenterazine is cell-permeable but unstable and light-sensitive. Rluc signal decays rapidly compared to firefly luciferase, necessitating sequential measurement in dual-luciferase assays. NanoLuc uses the same coelenterazine pathway but is ~100-fold brighter and should not be conflated with Rluc. RLuc8 and RLuc8.6 are the preferred variants for in vivo imaging applications due to improved stability.

description: NanoLuc is a small (19.1 kDa, 171 aa), monomeric luciferase engineered by Promega via directed evolution from the deep-sea shrimp Oplophorus gracilirostris bioluminescent complex. It produces bright, stable, glow-type blue luminescence (~460 nm) using the synthetic substrate furimazine, yielding ~150-fold greater specific activity than firefly or Renilla luciferases at equivalent molar concentrations. Its small coding sequence (~513 bp) and high brightness make it widely used in reporter assays, real-time cellular imaging, and fusion protein studies.
mechanism: Catalyzes the oxidative decarboxylation of furimazine (a cell-permeable coelenterazine analog) to produce coelenteramide and CO2, releasing a photon of blue light (~460 nm) via an ATP-independent bioluminescent reaction with exceptionally high catalytic efficiency.
known_variants: NlucP — C-terminal PEST degron fusion for short protein half-life and dynamic transcriptional reporter assays
NanoBiT LgBiT (18 kDa) — large fragment of split NanoLuc for detecting protein-protein interactions by complementation
NanoBiT SmBiT (11 aa) — small fragment of split NanoLuc; low intrinsic affinity ensures complementation is interaction-dependent
NanoTrip — three-fragment split variant for orthogonal complementation assays
Codon-optimized mammalian variant (Promega pNL vectors, standard for most labs)
Codon-optimized bacterial/E. coli variant
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'luciferase' without specifying NanoLuc, causing confusion with firefly luciferase (FLuc, ~550 nm, luciferin substrate) or Renilla luciferase (RLuc, coelenterazine substrate)
NanoBiT half-fragments (LgBiT or SmBiT) occasionally annotated as full-length NanoLuc
Substrate confused with coelenterazine (used by Renilla) — furimazine is not interchangeable with native coelenterazine analogs
Sometimes conflated with the native Oplophorus luciferase heterotetramer (~105 kDa), which is structurally unrelated to the engineered monomer
notes: NanoLuc was derived by Promega from the 19 kDa subunit of the native Oplophorus gracilirostris luciferase complex and optimized by 8 rounds of directed evolution. Furimazine is commercially available as the Nano-Glo substrate (Promega) and is not interchangeable with firefly luciferin or standard coelenterazine. Background luminescence is negligible in most eukaryotic and prokaryotic hosts. Patents cover NanoLuc sequences and the NanoBiT split system (US8557970 and related). The codon-optimized mammalian sequence is the de facto standard in most plasmid repositories.

description: β-galactosidase is an enzyme encoded by the E. coli lacZ gene that cleaves lactose (and chromogenic/fluorescent substrates such as X-gal, ONPG, and CPRG) into galactose and glucose. In plasmid engineering it serves as a colorimetric or chemiluminescent reporter for promoter activity, recombinant insert detection (blue-white screening via lacZα complementation), and gene expression quantification. The lacZα fragment, encoding only the N-terminal alpha peptide, is the form most commonly embedded in cloning vectors and functions through intragenic complementation with ΔM15 host strains.
mechanism: The full-length LacZ enzyme (1,023 aa) forms a homotetrameric glycoside hydrolase that cleaves β-d-galactosides; in blue-white screening, X-gal (5-bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl-β-d-galactopyranoside) is hydrolyzed to a blue indigo precipitate. The lacZα complementation system exploits intragenic alpha complementation: the plasmid-encoded α-peptide (residues 1–59 or 1–146) associates in trans with a chromosomally expressed ΔM15 fragment to reconstitute active enzyme.
known_variants: lacZα fragment (~447 bp, encodes only the N-terminal α-peptide for blue-white complementation)
lacZΔM15 (chromosomal deletion variant in DH5α/JM109 hosts; complements lacZα on plasmid)
Codon-optimized lacZ for mammalian expression (human codon bias, no CpG depletion)
lacZ-NLS (nuclear localization signal fusion for nuclear reporter assays)
Secreted β-galactosidase (SEAP-like, truncated signal peptide fusions)
Thermostable variant from Thermus thermophilus (used in high-temp assays)
known_misannotations: lacZα fragment annotated as full-length lacZ — lacZα is ~447 bp; full lacZ is ~3,075 bp
lacZ and lacI confused — lacI encodes the lac repressor, not β-galactosidase
lacZ annotated as a selection marker rather than a reporter (it confers no antibiotic resistance)
Blue-white screening misattributed to lacZ alone — requires ΔM15 host for α-complementation
pCH110 lacZ (SV40-driven) annotated as prokaryotic lacZ due to identical coding sequence
notes: Two distinct use cases share the lacZ label and must not be conflated: (1) the full 3,075 bp lacZ ORF used as a quantitative reporter (ONPG or chemiluminescent assay, e.g., in pCH110 or lentiviral reporter constructs); and (2) the short lacZα fragment (~447 bp) used solely for blue-white colony screening in standard cloning vectors (pUC, pBluescript). The lacZα system only produces blue colonies when the host strain carries the complementing ΔM15 deletion (e.g., DH5α, JM109, XL1-Blue). For mammalian reporters, lacZ is largely superseded by GFP/luciferase due to endogenous mammalian β-galactosidase background activity, particularly in senescent cells.

description: β-glucuronidase (GUS), encoded by the E. coli uidA gene, is a widely used reporter that cleaves glucuronide substrates to produce colorimetric or fluorescent products detectable by histochemical or biochemical assay. Introduced by Jefferson et al. (1987), it became the dominant reporter for stable plant transformation because most plant tissues lack endogenous glucuronidase activity, yielding very low background. It is used to monitor promoter activity, transformation efficiency, and tissue-specific expression patterns.
mechanism: Catalyzes hydrolysis of β-D-glucuronides; in reporter assays it cleaves X-Gluc (5-bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl-β-D-glucuronide) to yield an insoluble blue indigo precipitate for histochemical localization, or cleaves MUG (4-methylumbelliferyl-β-D-glucuronide) to the fluorescent product 4-methylumbelliferone for quantitative fluorometric measurement.
known_variants: GUSPlus (GUS+): intron-disrupted version that prevents expression in Agrobacterium, eliminating false-positive blue staining in plant transformation assays
Codon-optimized GUS: plant-codon-optimized uidA for higher expression in monocots or dicots
GUS-GFP fusion: dual reporter combining GUS histochemistry with GFP fluorescence imaging
Thermostable GUS variants: engineered for higher-temperature stability in industrial enzyme applications
known_misannotations: Confused with lacZ (β-galactosidase): both are E. coli-derived hydrolase reporters; databases occasionally mislabel one as the other in older vector annotations
GUS vs GUS+: intron-containing GUS+ and native bacterial GUS are often annotated identically despite functional differences critical for plant assays
uidA labeled as 'GUS gene' without distinguishing CDS from full operon context, leading to length discrepancies in feature databases
Endogenous mammalian GUS activity (GUSB locus) sometimes conflated with the E. coli reporter gene in cross-species annotation contexts
notes: Endogenous β-glucuronidase activity is present in mammals (lysosomal GUSB), many fungi, and some bacteria, making GUS unsuitable as a reporter in those systems without careful background controls. Activity is inhibited by saccharolactone, which can be used to distinguish endogenous from reporter activity. The GUS assay requires tissue fixation that is incompatible with live imaging; GFP-based reporters have largely supplanted GUS for live-cell applications, but GUS remains a gold standard for permanent histochemical documentation of plant transformation. Optimum enzyme pH is ~6.8; activity is abolished by glutaraldehyde fixation above ~0.1%.

description: BSD encodes blasticidin S deaminase, an enzyme derived from Aspergillus terreus that confers resistance to blasticidin S, a nucleoside antibiotic that inhibits peptide bond formation during translation. It is widely used as a selectable marker in mammalian, yeast, and insect cell systems, particularly in stable transfection and lentiviral integration workflows where multiple selection markers are needed simultaneously.
mechanism: Blasticidin S deaminase catalyzes the hydrolytic deamination of blasticidin S at its cytosine moiety, converting it to the inactive metabolite deaminohydroxyblasticidin, which no longer inhibits ribosomal peptidyl transferase activity.
known_variants: human codon-optimized BSD (hBSD) for enhanced mammalian expression
BSD fusion constructs (e.g., BSD-GFP, BSD-2A-reporter) for dual-selection and reporter applications
known_misannotations: Confused with bsr (blasticidin S resistance gene from Bacillus cereus), which encodes a ribosomal RNA methyltransferase — a distinct gene with a completely different resistance mechanism
Generically annotated as 'blasticidin resistance' without distinguishing BSD (deaminase) from bsr (methyltransferase)
Occasionally mislabeled 'bsdA' or 'blsD', conflating it with biosynthetic genes in the blasticidin S producer Streptomyces griseochromogenes
notes: BSD is the preferred blasticidin resistance marker for eukaryotic cell lines because the deaminase mechanism is effective across a broad range of eukaryotic ribosomes. The bsr gene is more commonly used for bacterial selection. Blasticidin S is effective against both prokaryotes and eukaryotes, making BSD useful in mixed-selection schemes. Typical selection concentrations are 2–10 µg/mL in mammalian cells and 100–300 µg/mL in yeast. BSD is often paired with neomycin (G418) or hygromycin in multi-marker systems. The compact size (~396 bp CDS) makes it well-suited for size-constrained vectors such as AAV.

description: The vegetative origin of replication (oriV) from the broad-host-range IncP-1α plasmid RK2 (also deposited and described as RP4, RP1, R18, and R68). It confers autonomous replication across a remarkably wide range of gram-negative bacteria, making it one of the most versatile origins used in plasmid engineering for organisms lacking species-specific cloning tools. It is routinely incorporated into broad-host-range shuttle vectors and expression systems for environmental, agricultural, and industrial organisms.
mechanism: Replication is initiated by the plasmid-encoded TrfA protein (TrfA-44 or the shorter TrfA-33 isoform), which binds to iteron repeat sequences within oriV and recruits the host DnaB helicase and replicative machinery. Copy number (~5–7 per chromosome equivalent in E. coli) is regulated by iteron-mediated handcuffing, in which TrfA-bound iterons pair and sequester free TrfA, creating an autorepression loop.
known_variants: Minimal oriV (~1.3 kb) retaining only the iterons and AT-rich unwinding element, lacking accessory replication enhancers
trfA-33 isoform vectors (shorter TrfA from internal start codon) with altered host range relative to full-length trfA-44 constructs
oriV paired with a trimmed trfA gene in cis for self-sufficient replication without helper plasmid
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'RK2 ori' without distinguishing oriV (vegetative replication) from oriT (conjugal transfer origin), which are separate elements on RK2
Confused with the IncP-1β oriV from plasmid R751, which is structurally and phylogenetically distinct despite sharing the incompatibility group family designation
Annotated as 'IncP ori' without specifying the α or β subgroup, conflating incompatible subtypes
The plasmid aliases RP4, RP1, R18, and R68 sometimes lead to duplicate or inconsistent annotations for what is the same replicon
notes: RK2, RP4, R18, R68, and RP1 are all names for the same ~60 kb plasmid; any vector annotated with these names carries the same oriV. The functional host range encompasses most gram-negative Proteobacteria but does not extend to gram-positive bacteria or eukaryotes without additional heterologous elements. If the trfA gene is removed from the backbone (as in some mobilizable suicide vectors), TrfA must be supplied in trans from a helper strain. oriT is often co-retained for conjugal mobilization but is not required for replication.

description: The R6K gamma (γ) origin of replication is a conditional origin derived from the IncX plasmid R6K that requires the plasmid-encoded Pi (π) protein for activity. Plasmids carrying this origin replicate only in pir+ bacterial strains, making them invaluable as suicide vectors, transposon-delivery vectors, and allelic exchange systems in which replication is intentionally blocked in target strains. It is also the origin used in Invitrogen/Thermo Fisher Gateway DONR entry vectors.
mechanism: The Pi protein (product of the pir gene) binds seven 22-bp iteron sequences within the γ origin and recruits host DnaB helicase and the replication machinery for initiation. In pir⁻ strains no Pi is available, the origin is non-functional, and the plasmid is lost — forcing integration or counterselection. The pir116 gain-of-function allele relaxes iteron handcuffing and raises copy number ~10–15-fold.
known_variants: R6K α (alpha) origin — ~1.2 kb, Pi-dependent, less commonly used in synthetic vectors
R6K β (beta) origin — intermediate size, Pi-dependent, occasionally retained alongside γ in full R6K backbone
pir116 allele context — when Pi is supplied by the pir116 relaxed-control allele, copy number rises from ~15 to ~250 copies/cell
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'R6K ori' without specifying the γ, α, or β sub-origin, making it ambiguous which of the three R6K origins is present
Confused with oriV of other IncX-family plasmids (e.g., R6, R6-5) that share sequence similarity
Occasionally mislabeled 'ColE1 ori' in legacy vector maps when the actual backbone is pir-dependent
Greek letter γ encoded inconsistently (γ, g, gamma) causing search and annotation mismatches
notes: Requires pir+ host strains for propagation: SM10λpir, BW20767, EC100D pir+, DH5αλpir, or PIR1/PIR2 (Thermo Fisher). Standard laboratory strains (DH5α, TOP10, BL21) are pir⁻ and cannot replicate R6K-based vectors. The Gateway cloning system exploits this origin in DONR vectors so that non-recombined plasmids are lost in pir⁻ strains after BP recombination, providing built-in negative selection. Not functional in gram-positive organisms or eukaryotes.

description: The CMV immediate-early (MIE) promoter is derived from the major immediate-early gene region of human cytomegalovirus (HCMV) strain AD169 and is one of the strongest known constitutive promoters. It drives high-level transgene expression across a broad range of mammalian cell types and is ubiquitous in mammalian expression vectors, viral vectors (lentiviral, adenoviral, AAV), and reporter constructs. Its strength and broad activity made it the default choice for heterologous protein production and gene delivery applications.
mechanism: The CMV MIE promoter contains a powerful upstream enhancer region (~400 bp) packed with binding sites for transcription factors including NF-κB, SP1, AP-1, CREB, and YY1, which together drive robust, constitutive transcription from the downstream TATA-containing core promoter. Activity can be silenced by CpG methylation at the promoter over time, particularly in pluripotent stem cells, neurons, and during long-term stable genomic integration.
known_variants: CpG-depleted CMV promoter — engineered to reduce methylation-induced silencing for stable and in vivo use
Minimal/core CMV promoter — TATA box region only (~200 bp), substantially lower activity than full-length
CMV enhancer fused to chicken beta-actin promoter (CAG/CAGG/CBA promoter) — common hybrid for sustained in vivo expression
mCMV promoter — derived from mouse cytomegalovirus, distinct sequence but similar function
CMV promoter + chimeric intron — synthetic intron inserted in 5' UTR to boost mRNA stability and expression
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'CMV promoter' when only the enhancer element is present, without the core promoter
Confusion between HCMV (human) and mCMV (mouse) CMV promoter sequences, which have different binding site compositions
Annotation extended into the 5' UTR or intron A region, inflating the annotated feature length
Labeled 'CMV early promoter' instead of 'CMV immediate-early promoter' — these are distinct temporal gene classes in CMV biology
CAG/CAGG promoter (CMV enhancer fused to chicken beta-actin core) mislabeled simply as 'CMV promoter'
Truncated CMV promoter variants used in some commercial vectors annotated with the same name as the full-length version
notes: The CMV promoter is the most widely used constitutive mammalian promoter but is subject to transcriptional silencing by CpG methylation during stable chromosomal integration, especially in embryonic stem cells, iPSCs, and neuronal lineages. Expression levels vary by cell type (very high in HEK293, HeLa, CHO; lower in primary cells and neurons). For long-term in vivo expression or stem cell applications, CpG-depleted CMV or CAG promoter variants are preferred. The canonical sequence is derived from HCMV strain AD169; minor sequence differences exist between strains but functional activity is conserved.

description: The human ubiquitin C (UBC) promoter is a strong constitutive promoter derived from the 5' regulatory region of the human ubiquitin C gene (NCBI Gene ID 7316) located on chromosome 12q24.31. It drives robust, ubiquitous transgene expression across virtually all mammalian cell types and tissues. It is widely used in lentiviral and integrating expression vectors as an alternative to CMV when long-term transcriptional stability is required, since it is significantly more resistant to epigenetic silencing.
mechanism: The UBC promoter contains a CpG island and multiple constitutively active transcription factor binding sites (SP1, NF-Y, and others) that maintain open chromatin and support persistent RNA Pol II recruitment. Its CpG-rich architecture makes it less susceptible to de novo DNA methylation and subsequent transcriptional silencing compared to viral promoters such as CMV, enabling durable expression in stable integrants and in vivo.
reference_plasmids: pWPI (EF1α variant; UBC sometimes swapped in)
known_variants: Truncated UBC core promoter (~500 bp minimal fragment retaining constitutive activity)
Mouse Ubc promoter (orthologous, slightly different sequence, used in some murine vectors)
known_misannotations: Confused with UBB (ubiquitin B) promoter — a paralogous human ubiquitin gene with a distinct but related promoter
Labeled generically as 'ubiquitin promoter' without species designation, creating ambiguity with plant ubiquitin promoters (e.g., maize Ubi-1) used in plant vectors
Occasionally mislabeled as 'EF1α' when a similar constitutive mammalian promoter is intended but the source sequence is actually UBC
Some older vectors annotate it simply as 'hUb' conflating UBB and UBC gene origins
notes: UBC is the preferred constitutive promoter for stable lentiviral integrations and in vivo gene delivery studies where CMV silencing is a known problem (e.g., pluripotent stem cells, primary T cells, in vivo liver transduction). Expression levels are typically lower than CMV IE in transient transfection but substantially more stable over time. The commonly cloned fragment spans approximately 1.2 kb immediately upstream of the UBC transcription start site. Do not confuse with the maize Ubiquitin-1 (Ubi-1) promoter used for plant transformation — these are entirely unrelated.

description: The promoter derived from the human elongation factor 1-alpha (EEF1A1) gene, one of the most abundantly and constitutively expressed housekeeping genes in mammalian cells. It drives strong transgene expression across a wide range of mammalian cell types including primary cells, stem cells, and T cells, and is a preferred alternative to CMV when silencing is a concern. Widely used in lentiviral, AAV, and plasmid expression vectors for stable and transient expression.
mechanism: Drives RNA Polymerase II-dependent transcription through a constitutively active CpG island-containing promoter; the native EEF1A1 locus is among the most transcriptionally active in the genome due to continuous demand for translation elongation factor. The first intron of EEF1A1, when included downstream of the promoter, substantially enhances expression via splicing-coupled mechanisms.
known_variants: EFS (short form, ~212 bp) — intron-deleted truncated version commonly used in lentiviral vectors to reduce insert size
Mouse EF1α promoter — nearly identical sequence and activity to the human version, used interchangeably in many vectors
EF1α-HTLV hybrid — EF1α fused with HTLV-1 R-U5 elements for enhanced retroviral expression
Synthetic EF1α — fully synthesized version to eliminate cryptic splice sites or restriction sites
known_misannotations: EFS (short, intron-deleted form) frequently mislabeled as full-length EF1α promoter in deposited plasmids
Mouse and human EF1α promoters used interchangeably but labeled only as 'EF1α' without species designation
CMV-EF1α hybrid promoters sometimes annotated as plain EF1α
Vectors retaining the EEF1A1 first intron downstream may annotate the intron separately or merge it into the promoter annotation
notes: The full-length human EF1α promoter includes the first intron (~900 bp), which significantly boosts expression and should be retained where vector size permits. The short form (EFS, ~212 bp) lacks the intron and is the standard choice for lentiviral vectors operating near packaging capacity. Unlike CMV, EF1α is highly resistant to CpG methylation-induced silencing, making it the preferred constitutive promoter for iPSC, hematopoietic stem cell, and in vivo gene therapy applications. Activity is robust across species (human, mouse, primate) and generally maintained in quiescent and differentiated cells.

description: A synthetic bidirectional promoter consisting of two tetracycline response element (TRE) minimal promoters arranged in opposing orientations flanking a central array of tet operator (tetO) sequences, enabling simultaneous doxycycline-inducible transcription of two separate transgenes in opposite directions. It is widely used in mammalian cells to tightly co-regulate expression of two genes (e.g., a gene of interest paired with a reporter) from a single compact regulatory cassette. This architecture ensures stoichiometric co-expression and reduces the footprint compared to using two separate inducible promoters.
mechanism: Upon addition of doxycycline, the reverse tetracycline transactivator (rtTA; Tet-On system) binds the central tetO array and recruits transcriptional machinery to the flanking minimal CMV promoters, driving RNA polymerase II elongation in both directions simultaneously. In the Tet-Off variant, the tTA transactivator is displaced by doxycycline, silencing both promoters coordinately.
known_variants: TRE-Tight-BI: uses the tighter TRE-Tight sequence for reduced basal (leaky) expression
TRE3G-bi: third-generation tetO array with improved signal-to-noise and stronger maximal induction
asymmetric biTRE: two flanking promoters of different strengths to produce unequal but co-regulated output levels
minimal promoter swap: HSV TK minimal promoter substituted for CMV minimal promoter in one or both directions
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'TRE promoter' without indicating bidirectionality, losing the orientation information for the second transgene
Each half (left-facing and right-facing minimal promoter) annotated as two separate 'CMV minimal promoter' features, obscuring the shared tetO array
Labeled 'CMV promoter' because of the CMV minimal promoter component, incorrectly implying constitutive CMV activity
Confused with standard unidirectional TRE when only one output gene is cloned, making the bidirectional architecture invisible in the annotation
notes: Used almost exclusively in mammalian Tet-On/Tet-Off systems (rtTA or tTA). Requires co-expression of a compatible transactivator (rtTA2s-M2, rtTA3, or Tet-On 3G for Tet-On; tTA or tTA-Advanced for Tet-Off). The two promoter outputs are not always equal in strength; empirical testing of cloning orientation is recommended. Leaky (uninduced) expression is higher for TRE than TRE-Tight variants. Not functional as a standalone promoter — requires transactivator. Doxycycline concentration must be optimized per cell type (typically 0.1–1 µg/mL). The tetO array is sensitive to CpG methylation in long-term stable lines, which can silence both arms.

description: Blue Fluorescent Protein (BFP) is a blue-shifted variant of Aequorea victoria GFP generated by the Y66H substitution, which shifts excitation to ~383 nm and emission to ~448 nm. It is used as a fluorescent reporter in multicolor live-cell imaging and as a FRET donor paired with GFP or YFP. Enhanced variants (EBFP2, mTagBFP2) address the original BFP's poor brightness and photostability for practical applications.
mechanism: The Y66H mutation replaces tyrosine with histidine in the autocatalytic chromophore-forming tripeptide (residues 65–67), creating a histidine-based chromophore that absorbs near-UV light (~383 nm) and emits blue fluorescence (~448 nm) through an excited-state proton transfer mechanism requiring no exogenous cofactors.
known_variants: EBFP — Enhanced BFP with additional stabilizing mutations for improved folding and brightness over original Y66H
EBFP2 — significantly brighter and more photostable version; current standard for blue-channel reporters
mTagBFP — monomeric blue FP engineered from Actinia equina FP; ~3-fold brighter than EBFP
mTagBFP2 — further improved monomeric variant with enhanced photostability
Azurite — blue FP with improved pH stability derived from EBFP mutagenesis
Codon-optimized for human/mammalian expression (distinct GC content from original A. victoria sequence)
known_misannotations: Confused with CFP (Cyan Fluorescent Protein) — BFP peaks at ~448 nm vs CFP at ~476 nm; distinct mutations and spectral profiles
mTagBFP or TagBFP2 labeled generically as 'BFP' despite being derived from a different parental FP (sea anemone vs jellyfish)
EBFP2 annotated as EBFP — they are distinct variants with substantially different brightness and photostability
BFP used loosely as a catch-all for any blue-emitting fluorescent protein regardless of spectral properties
notes: The original BFP (Y66H only) is largely obsolete due to low quantum yield and rapid photobleaching; EBFP2 or mTagBFP2 are the recommended choices for modern blue-channel imaging. Near-UV excitation (~383 nm) required by classical BFP can cause phototoxicity in live cells, limiting its use in sensitive biological systems. All GFP-derived BFP variants require molecular oxygen for chromophore maturation and are thus not suitable for strict anaerobic applications.
