known_variants: GSG-P2A: N-terminal GSG linker added to improve cleavage efficiency in fusion contexts
human codon-optimized P2A for enhanced expression in mammalian cells
truncated P2A (17 aa core) omitting upstream linker residues
T2A/P2A hybrid sequences used in some bicistronic reporter constructs
known_misannotations: Labeled generically as '2A peptide' without specifying P2A vs T2A/E2A/F2A, causing confusion in databases
Annotated as 'IRES' in older constructs despite fundamentally different mechanism and size
Cleavage site G/P sometimes attributed to proteolytic cleavage rather than ribosomal skipping
GSG linker residues sometimes excluded from the annotated feature, leading to variable reported lengths
Occasionally mislabeled as T2A (from Thosea asigna virus) due to similar function
notes: P2A was first systematically characterized alongside T2A, E2A, and F2A by Kim et al. (2011, PLoS ONE). Among the 2A family, P2A generally shows the highest cleavage efficiency in mammalian cells. A small stub of 17–19 amino acids remains on the C-terminus of the upstream protein and can affect function or immunogenicity; this should be considered when designing constructs. The downstream protein retains a proline at its N-terminus. For fluorescent reporter co-expression, CAR-T cell engineering, and CRISPR delivery of multiple components, P2A has become the de facto standard linker.

description: E2A is a short self-cleaving peptide derived from Equine Rhinitis A Virus (ERAV) that enables co-expression of multiple proteins from a single transcript. It is used in polycistronic constructs to produce near-equimolar amounts of two or more proteins without an IRES, which can suppress downstream translation.
mechanism: E2A mediates ribosomal skipping: the ribosome fails to form a peptide bond between the penultimate glycine and the C-terminal proline of the 2A sequence, causing cotranslational cleavage that releases the upstream protein while translation continues into the downstream ORF.
known_variants: GSG-E2A: N-terminal Gly-Ser-Gly linker added upstream to improve ribosomal skipping efficiency
codon-optimized E2A for human/mammalian expression
furin-E2A: furin cleavage site (RAKR) inserted immediately upstream to remove the residual 2A tag from the upstream protein C-terminus
known_misannotations: Labeled simply as '2A' without specifying ERAV origin, causing confusion with T2A (Thosea asigna virus), P2A (porcine teschovirus-1), or F2A (FMDV)
Confused with the human E2A transcription factor gene (TCF3/ITF1), a completely unrelated bHLH transcription factor involved in B-cell development
Annotated as 'IRES' in older constructs where a 2A element was used for bicistronic expression
Cleavage site sometimes annotated as a protease site rather than a cotranslational ribosomal skipping event
notes: E2A has lower cleavage efficiency in mammalian cells compared to P2A and T2A, which are generally preferred for high-efficiency applications. A residual ~20 aa peptide remains on the C-terminus of the upstream protein after skipping; this can affect protein function and should be considered in fusion protein designs. Adding a furin site upstream of E2A eliminates this tag for secreted or cell-surface proteins routed through the secretory pathway. E2A retains reasonable activity across a wide range of expression systems including plants and yeast, making it more broadly applicable than some other 2A peptides.

description: F2A is a 2A self-cleaving peptide derived from Foot-and-mouth disease virus (FMDV) used to link multiple open reading frames within a single polycistronic transcript, enabling co-expression of two or more proteins from one promoter. It is widely employed in plasmid engineering as an alternative to IRES elements, producing near-equimolar protein ratios from upstream and downstream ORFs. F2A is commonly used in lentiviral vectors, CAR-T constructs, and reporter co-expression systems.
mechanism: F2A mediates co-translational 'cleavage' via ribosomal skipping: the ribosome fails to form a peptide bond between the conserved glycine and proline residues at the C-terminus of the 2A peptide (consensus ...DVEENPGP↓), releasing the upstream protein with a short C-terminal F2A tag while translation of the downstream ORF continues, with the downstream protein retaining an N-terminal proline.
known_variants: GSG-F2A: N-terminal GSG linker added to improve ribosomal skipping efficiency
codon-optimized F2A for human/mammalian expression
codon-optimized F2A for plant expression
furin-F2A: RAKR furin cleavage site inserted upstream to remove residual 2A tag from upstream protein
minimal F2A (18 aa core, without upstream linker)
known_misannotations: Labeled simply as '2A' without specifying FMDV origin, conflating F2A with T2A, P2A, or E2A
Annotated as a protease cleavage site rather than a ribosomal skipping sequence
Confused with IRES elements, which use a fundamentally different mechanism
GSG linker sometimes attributed to the upstream ORF CDS rather than the 2A element
Furin site (RAKR) upstream of F2A sometimes annotated as part of the preceding protein rather than the 2A cassette
notes: F2A generally shows lower cleavage efficiency than P2A and T2A in mammalian cells; T2A and P2A are typically preferred when maximal cleavage efficiency is required. All 2A-cleaved upstream proteins retain a variable-length C-terminal 2A peptide stub. Adding a furin recognition sequence (RAKR) immediately upstream of F2A allows post-translational removal of this stub via endogenous furin. Cleavage efficiency is context-dependent and can be affected by flanking protein sequences and cell type.

description: A short peptide sequence (LEVLFQGP) recognized and cleaved between the glutamine and glycine residues by PreScission protease (HRV 3C protease, Human Rhinovirus 14 3C protease). It is inserted between an affinity or solubility tag (e.g., GST, His6, MBP) and the protein of interest to allow tag removal after purification. Cleavage leaves only a minimal GP dipeptide scar on the N-terminus of the target protein, making it one of the least intrusive tag-removal systems available.
mechanism: HRV 3C is a cysteine protease that recognizes the consensus motif LEVLFQ/GP and catalyzes peptide bond hydrolysis between the glutamine (Q) and glycine (G) residues via a Cys-His-Asp catalytic triad. The reaction proceeds efficiently at 4°C, reducing proteolysis of temperature-sensitive targets during tag removal.
known_variants: Extended linker form with flanking Gly-Ser spacers (e.g., GSLEVLFQGPGS) for increased protease accessibility
Codon-optimized for E. coli expression (e.g., CTG GAA GTG CTG TTT CAG GGT CCC)
Codon-optimized for human/mammalian expression
Dual-tag constructs combining PreScission site with a second cleavage site (e.g., TEV + PreScission)
known_misannotations: Labeled generically as 'protease cleavage site' without identifying the enzyme, causing confusion with TEV, thrombin, Factor Xa, and enterokinase sites
Confused with TEV protease site (ENLYFQS/G), which shares the Q/G scissile bond motif
Sometimes annotated only as 'linker' when embedded within a flexible linker region
HRV 3C and PreScission are often treated as distinct features in databases despite being the same enzyme recognition sequence
Cleavage scar residues (GP) sometimes mistakenly included in the annotation of the target protein CDS rather than the cleavage site feature
notes: PreScission protease is commercially sold as a GST fusion protein (e.g., by Cytiva/GE Healthcare), which enables one-step removal of both the GST tag on the target and the protease itself via glutathione resin pulldown. Cleavage efficiency can be reduced when the site is sterically occluded; adding flexible Gly-Ser spacers flanking the LEVLFQGP core sequence typically restores activity. Unlike thrombin or Factor Xa, PreScission has very low non-specific cleavage activity and works optimally at 4°C, making it preferred for unstable proteins.

description: The TEV protease site is a seven-amino-acid recognition sequence (ENLYFQ/S) cleaved by the Tobacco Etch Virus nuclear inclusion protease (NIa). It is ubiquitously used in recombinant protein engineering to enable precise, highly specific removal of affinity tags (His6, GST, MBP, SUMO, etc.) following protein purification. Its extremely high sequence specificity—orders of magnitude greater than thrombin or factor Xa—results in negligible off-target cleavage, making it the preferred tag-removal protease in structural and biochemical studies.
mechanism: TEV protease is a cysteine protease that recognizes the heptapeptide ENLYFQ↓S and cleaves between the Gln (P1) and Ser (P1') residues; the P1' residue (typically Ser or Gly) remains on the N-terminus of the released protein. The enzyme requires reducing conditions (DTT or TCEP) for activity because of its active-site Cys residue.
known_variants: ENLYFQ/G — P1' Gly variant; leaves Gly on the protein N-terminus after cleavage, useful when downstream Met-aminopeptidase processing is desired
ENLYFQ/M — P1' Met variant; allows Met-aminopeptidase to generate a native N-terminus
Codon-optimized for E. coli (e.g., GAGAACCTGTACTTCCAGTCT) — higher expression of the recognition site in bacterial constructs
Codon-optimized for human/mammalian expression — altered codon usage for higher protein yields in CHO/HEK293 systems
Extended linker variants (e.g., GGSENLYFQS) — flexible glycine-serine spacer added upstream for improved protease accessibility
known_misannotations: Annotated as only six residues ENLYFQ, omitting the P1' Ser/Gly that is required for correct cleavage chemistry
Confused with HRV 3C (PreScission) protease site (LEVLFQ/GP), which has a similar purpose but different sequence and enzyme
Mislabeled as 'thrombin cleavage site' or 'factor Xa site' in older vector maps where the tag-removal protease was not specified
Sometimes labeled 'TEV sequence' or 'TEV linker' without indicating it is a protease recognition sequence, causing confusion with TEV regulatory elements
Occasional reversal of cleavage orientation in annotation: the site is annotated on the wrong strand or with start/end coordinates that exclude one residue
notes: TEV protease is active over a broad temperature range (4–30 °C) and at near-neutral pH (pH 7–8), allowing tag removal under gentle conditions that preserve protein stability. Cleavage efficiency depends critically on accessibility: the site should be positioned in a flexible linker region, not buried in a folded domain. A small residue (Ser or Gly) invariably remains on the protein N-terminus post-cleavage; this must be considered when a native N-terminus is essential. His-tagged TEV protease variants (e.g., from pRK793) can be removed from the digest by a second Ni-NTA step. TEV protease is inhibited by EDTA at high concentrations (chelates structural zinc) and by its own reaction product (product inhibition at high conversion).

description: A short peptide sequence (LVPRGS) recognized and cleaved by the serine protease thrombin between the arginine and glycine residues. It is widely used in recombinant protein production to remove affinity tags (such as His-tag, GST, or MBP) after purification, leaving minimal extra residues on the target protein. The site is typically inserted between the affinity tag and the protein of interest during vector construction.
mechanism: Thrombin is a serine protease that recognizes the consensus sequence LVPR↓GS and cleaves the peptide bond between arginine (R) and glycine (G) via a Ser-His-Asp catalytic triad mechanism. Specificity arises from interactions of the P1 arginine side chain with the S1 specificity pocket of thrombin, making cleavage highly selective under optimal conditions.
known_variants: LVPR↓GS (canonical 6 aa, 18 bp — leaves Gly-Ser on target N-terminus)
LVPR↓GH (alternative C-terminal residue in some commercial vectors)
Extended ALVPRGS with N-terminal Ala for improved protease accessibility
Codon-optimized LVPRGS for E. coli high-expression (elevated GC content)
Double thrombin site (LVPRGS-LVPRGS) for improved cleavage efficiency on buried sites
known_misannotations: Confused with Factor Xa cleavage site (IEGR↓) — both remove N-terminal affinity tags but use distinct enzymes
Confused with PreScission (HRV 3C) protease site (LEVLFQ↓GP) — used in pGEX-6P series, different enzyme and specificity
Confused with TEV protease site (ENLYFQ↓S) — TEV is now more common, leading to mislabeling of older constructs
Cleavage position annotated between LVP and RGS rather than at the correct LVPR↓GS junction
Annotated generically as 'protease site' or 'linker' without specifying thrombin
Entire LVPRGS sequence annotated as part of the affinity tag rather than as a distinct cleavage site feature
notes: After thrombin cleavage, two residues (Gly-Ser) typically remain on the target protein N-terminus, which must be considered when exact N-terminal sequence is required. Thrombin has low but measurable off-target activity at similar sequences (e.g., XPRGX) within some target proteins. TEV protease has largely replaced thrombin in modern lab practice due to greater specificity, lower cost, and ease of removal by His-tag. Thrombin cleavage is performed at room temperature or 4°C, typically in 20 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0 with 150 mM NaCl and 2.5 mM CaCl2; CaCl2 is required for thrombin activity.

description: A short peptide sequence (DDDDK) recognized and cleaved by enterokinase (enteropeptidase, EC 3.4.21.9), a serine protease. It is engineered into recombinant expression vectors between an affinity tag and the protein of interest, enabling tag removal after purification. Cleavage occurs C-terminal to the lysine, leaving no extra residues on the target protein's N-terminus when the site is positioned immediately upstream of the gene of interest.
mechanism: Enterokinase recognizes the highly specific tetracidic sequence (Asp)4-Lys and hydrolyzes the peptide bond on the C-terminal side of the lysine residue via a catalytic serine triad mechanism, releasing the downstream protein with an authentic N-terminus.
known_variants: Extended recognition sequence with additional upstream basic residues (e.g., DDDDK with flanking linker) to improve solvent accessibility
E. coli codon-optimized DDDDK (e.g., GACGACGACGACAAG vs. GATGATGATGATAAA)
Tandem EK site (DDDDKDDDDK) used in rare constructs for more complete cleavage
known_misannotations: Confused with Factor Xa cleavage site (which recognizes IEGR/IEGF, not DDDDK)
Confused with thrombin cleavage site (LVPR↓GS) — both are common tag-removal proteases
Site occasionally annotated as including flanking linker residues beyond the minimal DDDDK core
Enzyme name inconsistency: 'enterokinase' and 'enteropeptidase' refer to the same enzyme but are treated as distinct features in some annotation tools
notes: Enterokinase is highly specific due to the unusual poly-Asp requirement but is significantly slower and more expensive than TEV or PreScission (HRV 3C) protease, limiting its use in high-throughput applications. Cleavage efficiency is reduced when the site is buried within a folded domain or when the immediately downstream residue is proline. Bovine and recombinant human enterokinase light chain (catalytic domain) are both commercially available; recombinant forms are preferred for GMP applications. Internal DDDDK sequences in the target protein can lead to non-specific cleavage.

description: Factor Xa is a serine protease that recognizes the tetrapeptide sequence IEGR and cleaves on the C-terminal side of the arginine residue, leaving no extra residues on the protein of interest. It is widely used in fusion protein expression systems to remove affinity tags (His-tag, MBP, GST) after purification, enabling recovery of a native or near-native N-terminus on the target protein.
mechanism: Factor Xa is a serine endoprotease (EC 3.4.21.6) of the blood coagulation cascade that recognizes the consensus sequence (Ile/Val/Leu)-Glu-Gly-Arg and cleaves the peptide bond on the C-terminal side of Arg, generating a clean N-terminus on the downstream protein with no extraneous amino acids.
known_variants: Extended consensus (Ile/Val/Leu)-Glu-Gly-Arg — P4 position tolerates branched aliphatic residues beyond Ile
Codon-optimized IEGR for E. coli (e.g., ATC-GAG-GGC-CGT)
Codon-optimized IEGR for mammalian expression (e.g., ATC-GAG-GGG-AGG)
known_misannotations: Labeled generically as 'cleavage site' or 'protease site' without specifying Factor Xa
Confused with thrombin cleavage site (LVPRGS), which also leaves a two-residue scar (Gly-Ser)
Cleavage position sometimes annotated within the IEGR sequence rather than immediately C-terminal to Arg
Occasionally mislabeled as 'Factor X site' (omitting the 'a' activation state designator)
Sometimes confused with enterokinase (DDDDK) or PreScission/HRV 3C sites in vector maps
notes: Factor Xa cleavage generates a scar-free N-terminus on the protein of interest, unlike thrombin (leaves Gly-Ser). However, Factor Xa can exhibit secondary cleavage at non-canonical sites, particularly internal Arg residues, which can reduce yield. Cleavage efficiency depends on local protein folding and accessibility. The reaction is typically performed at room temperature in physiological buffer; divalent cations (Ca2+) are required for full protease activity. Factor Xa has been largely superseded by TEV protease and HRV 3C (PreScission) in modern workflows due to their higher sequence specificity and recombinant availability, but remains standard in legacy MBP and His-tag fusion vectors.

description: loxP is a 34 bp site-specific recombination sequence from bacteriophage P1 that is recognized exclusively by Cre recombinase. Two loxP sites in direct orientation flanking a DNA segment ('floxed') directs Cre-mediated excision of the intervening sequence as a circular episome; two sites in inverted orientation cause inversion of the intervening DNA. It is the most widely deployed conditional gene-control tool in mouse genetics, neuroscience, and synthetic biology, enabling precise spatial and temporal control of gene expression or deletion.
mechanism: Cre recombinase (38 kDa tyrosine recombinase from phage P1) binds as a homodimer to each 13 bp inverted repeat flanking the 8 bp asymmetric spacer, forming a synaptic tetrameric complex. Sequential single-strand cleavage via a 3′-phosphotyrosine intermediate generates a Holliday junction intermediate, which is resolved by a second pair of cuts to yield recombinant products with a single residual loxP scar.
known_variants: lox66 — mutant right inverted repeat; paired with lox71 for near-unidirectional integration (double-mutant lox product is recombination-dead)
lox71 — mutant left inverted repeat; paired with lox66 for unidirectional cassette integration
lox2272 — incompatible spacer variant (GGATACTT spacer); recombines only with itself, enabling orthogonal dual-recombinase experiments
lox5171 — incompatible spacer variant for additional orthogonal recombination channel
loxN — incompatible spacer variant (TCGATCGA spacer) for further orthogonal use
loxP mutant m2 / loxP-AS — various spacer-substituted incompatible variants described for multiplexed engineering
known_misannotations: Annotated as simply 'lox' without specifying the variant, obscuring which spacer is present and preventing reliable identification of orthogonality
Confused with incompatible variants (lox2272, lox5171, loxN) that share the inverted repeats but differ in spacer and do not recombine with wild-type loxP
Annotated in reverse complement orientation without strand notation, introducing directional ambiguity for excision vs. inversion prediction
Residual post-excision loxP scar left by Cre-mediated deletion sometimes unannotated, causing downstream confusion about insert boundaries
FRT site (Flp recombinase target) occasionally mislabeled as loxP due to similar function but completely different sequence and enzyme
notes: The 8 bp asymmetric spacer (canonical: GCATACAT) encodes directionality and variant identity — only matching spacers recombine efficiently. Recombination leaves a single 34 bp loxP scar after excision that is itself a functional Cre substrate, making re-insertion possible but disfavored for large inserts. Cre activity is near-complete with strong promoters, but mosaic excision occurs with leaky Cre expression or low-dose Tamoxifen in CreERT2 systems. loxP sites are inert without Cre recombinase and do not interfere with normal transcription or replication.

description: lox2272 is a 34 bp mutant loxP recombination site engineered with a distinct 8 bp spacer (GGATACTT) that renders it orthogonal to wild-type loxP and other lox variants such as lox5171. It is used in plasmid engineering for multiplexed Cre-mediated recombination including recombinase-mediated cassette exchange (RMCE) and combinatorial gene expression systems such as Brainbow, where independent and simultaneous recombination events must occur at distinct sites within the same cell.
mechanism: Cre recombinase binds the two 13 bp inverted repeat arms of lox2272 and catalyzes strand exchange at the 8 bp asymmetric spacer. The altered spacer sequence (GGATACTT vs. loxP's ATGTATGC) prevents heterologous strand exchange between lox2272 and other lox variants, conferring strict site-specificity so that only lox2272 × lox2272 recombination proceeds.
known_variants: lox2272 with extended flanking sequences for improved recombination efficiency in chromatin contexts
lox2272 paired in inverted orientation for excision/inversion configurations in dual-recombinase systems
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'loxP' when the sequence is the mutant lox2272 variant — visually similar in maps but functionally incompatible
Confused with lox5171 or loxN in Brainbow-derived constructs where all three variants co-occur
Annotated without the '2272' suffix (simply 'lox site'), making variant identity ambiguous in flat-file records
notes: lox2272 orthogonality to loxP is conferred by spacer sequence incompatibility, not by differences in the inverted repeat arms; Cre recombinase binds all lox variants equally but can only complete strand exchange when spacers are identical. This makes it broadly compatible with any Cre-expressing system. Originally characterized by Langer et al. (2002, Nucleic Acids Res) as part of a genetic screen for non-compatible loxP sites; widely adopted in Brainbow multicolor lineage-tracing (Livet et al., 2007, Nature) and in RMCE strategies requiring two orthogonal recombination events.

description: loxN is a 34 bp engineered variant of the loxP recombination site in which the 8 bp asymmetric spacer region is mutated, rendering it incompatible with wild-type loxP and other spacer-mutant lox variants. It is recognized and acted upon by Cre recombinase, enabling directional excision or integration between matched loxN×loxN pairs without cross-reacting with loxP, lox2272, lox5171, or lox511 sites. This orthogonality makes loxN valuable in multi-cassette expression systems, intersectional genetic strategies, and sequential recombination schemes where independent control of multiple recombination events is required.
mechanism: Cre recombinase monomers bind the 13 bp inverted repeat arms of two loxN sites and assemble a synaptic tetramer; strand exchange occurs across the 8 bp spacer via a Holiday junction intermediate. The spacer mutations in loxN prevent productive synapsis with non-matching lox sites because the spacer sequences must be complementary for rejoining after the first strand exchange.
reference_plasmids: pCAG-Cre (used alongside loxN-flanked alleles in intersectional mouse genetics)
known_variants: loxN with additional inverted-repeat mutations (e.g., combined spacer + arm mutations for further orthogonality)
lox71/lox66 hybrid contexts where loxN is used as a third orthogonal site
known_misannotations: Mislabeled as loxP when the spacer sequence is not explicitly verified by sequencing
Confused with lox2272, lox5171, or lox511 because all are functionally described as 'incompatible lox variants'; name used inconsistently across literature
Occasionally annotated generically as 'lox site' without specifying the N variant, causing ambiguity in multi-site constructs
notes: Compatibility between lox sites is determined entirely by the 8 bp spacer; loxN recombines exclusively with another loxN site and not with loxP or other spacer variants. Verify the spacer sequence (not just the flanking inverted repeats) when confirming loxN identity. loxN is frequently deployed in mouse conditional genetics alongside loxP to achieve intersectional labeling or two-step recombination. Cre recombinase supply is the same regardless of lox variant; no engineered recombinase is needed.

description: The FRT (Flp Recombination Target) site is a 34 bp sequence recognized by the Flp site-specific recombinase derived from the Saccharomyces cerevisiae 2-micron plasmid. In plasmid and genome engineering, pairs of FRT sites in direct orientation allow Flp recombinase to excise the intervening DNA—most commonly used to remove selectable markers after targeted integration—while FRT sites in inverted orientation cause inversion of the flanked segment. The Flp/FRT system functions across bacteria, yeast, insect, and mammalian cells and is a standard tool in conditional knockout mouse construction.
mechanism: Flp recombinase binds cooperatively to the two 13 bp inverted repeat arms of the FRT site and catalyzes strand exchange via a topoisomerase IB-like mechanism, forming a covalent 3′-phosphotyrosine intermediate at staggered cuts within the 8 bp asymmetric spacer; recombination between two directly oriented FRT sites proceeds through a Holliday junction intermediate to excise the intervening sequence as a relaxed circular molecule, leaving a single 34 bp FRT scar at the junction.
known_variants: FRT3 (F3) — single point mutation in the 8 bp asymmetric spacer; orthogonal to wild-type FRT and does not cross-react; used in multi-cassette RMCE
F5 — second orthogonal spacer variant enabling simultaneous two-cassette RMCE alongside F3
Extended FRT (48 bp) — includes additional flanking sequences used in some early cloning protocols but not required for recombination
Mutant FRT with altered spacer used in parallel recombination systems to prevent unwanted inter-site recombination
known_misannotations: Labeled 'FLP site' instead of 'FRT site' — FLP/Flp is the recombinase enzyme, FRT is the DNA recognition sequence
Only the 8 bp spacer annotated as FRT rather than the full 34 bp functional unit including inverted repeats
Confused with loxP in vector maps when both Cre/lox and Flp/FRT systems are present in the same construct
Orientation omitted despite the asymmetric spacer conferring directionality critical for excision vs. inversion outcomes
Annotated as 'FRT sequence' without specifying wild-type vs. orthogonal variant (F3/F5), causing ambiguity in RMCE vectors
notes: Direct-orientation FRT sites yield excision of the flanked cassette, leaving a single 34 bp FRT scar that is retained in the genome or plasmid permanently. Inverted FRT sites cause reversible inversion. The wild-type Flp recombinase is thermolabile at 37 °C in mammalian cells; improved variants FLPe (thermostabilized) and FLPo (human codon-optimized) substantially increase efficiency but recognize the same FRT sequence. FRT-flanked NeoR cassettes are a standard design in conditional knockout mouse targeting vectors, allowing marker removal by transient Flp expression after germline transmission.

description: F3 is a mutant Flp Recombination Target (FRT) site carrying an altered 8-bp asymmetric spacer sequence that renders it incompatible with wild-type FRT sites and other FRT mutants such as F14. It is used in recombinase-mediated cassette exchange (RMCE) systems to achieve directional, position-specific integration of expression cassettes at pre-defined genomic docking sites. Flanking a genomic locus with two distinct incompatible FRT variants (e.g., F3 at one boundary, F14 at the other) prevents re-excision and enforces unidirectional cassette exchange.
mechanism: Flp recombinase catalyzes strand exchange exclusively between FRT sites sharing identical spacer sequences; the mutated 8-bp spacer in F3 physically prevents recombination with wild-type FRT or F14. When an F3-flanked donor cassette is co-delivered with Flp recombinase into a cell carrying an F3/F14 docking locus, a single productive exchange event integrates the cassette in a defined orientation without producing an excisable product.
known_misannotations: Annotated as wild-type 'FRT' without noting the mutant spacer, leading to incorrect prediction of Flp-mediated excision between F3 and standard FRT sites
Confused with F14 or other incompatible FRT variants when plasmid annotations are transferred between labs without specifying spacer identity
Occasionally labeled 'FRT3' conflating it with unrelated lox variant naming conventions
notes: F3 sites require Flp recombinase supplied in trans (commonly FLPe or FLPo, codon-optimized for higher activity in mammalian cells). RMCE efficiency depends on pairing two distinct incompatible FRT variants (typically F3 and F14) at opposite junctions of both the genomic docking cassette and the donor vector. The 34-bp size is identical to wild-type FRT, making F3 indistinguishable by gel electrophoresis alone; sequencing across the spacer region is required to confirm identity.

description: attB1 is the left (5') attachment site used in Invitrogen Gateway cloning, derived from bacteriophage lambda attB with a modified 7-bp core O region that enforces orthogonality with attB2 and prevents illegitimate recombination. It is appended to the 5' end of forward PCR primers to generate attB1-flanked amplicons that recombine directionally with the attP1 site on a Gateway donor vector (e.g., pDONR221) via the BP Clonase reaction. After recombination, attB1 is consumed and converted to attL1 on the resulting entry clone.
mechanism: Lambda Integrase (Int) and Integration Host Factor (IHF) catalyze site-specific recombination between attB1 and attP1 through staggered strand exchange within the 7-bp overlap region; the modified core sequence in attB1 relative to attB2 ensures strict directionality and site orthogonality.
known_variants: attB1 with embedded Kozak sequence (enables direct N-terminal tagged expression without extra sequence manipulation)
attB1 with in-frame stop codon (for untagged C-terminal expression in LR destination vectors)
attB1r (reverse-complement orientation, used in MultiSite Gateway for promoter-fusion constructs)
attB4 / attB4r (orthogonal MultiSite Gateway sites used for multi-fragment assembly; not cross-reactive with attB1)
known_misannotations: Mislabeled as attL1 — attL1 is the hybrid site generated after BP recombination and is not the input substrate
Confused with attP1 — attP1 is the partner site present on the donor vector, not the PCR-amplified insert
Annotated generically as 'att site' or 'Gateway site' without specifying type (B/P/L/R) or site number (1/2)
Annotated as attB without the site number, conflating attB1 and attB2 which have different core sequences and are not interchangeable
notes: attB1 and attB2 flank the gene of interest in Gateway BP PCR products; the two sites differ at the 7-bp O core, enforcing insert directionality. The minimal functional attB1 sequence is ~25 bp, but primer designs typically include 2–4 extra 5' bases to improve recombination efficiency. In MultiSite Gateway cloning (e.g., three-fragment assembly), additional orthogonal att sites (attB4, attB4r, attB3) are combined with attB1/attB2 to assemble multiple fragments in a defined order. attB1 is a synthetic recombination site — its sequence is proprietary to Thermo Fisher (Invitrogen) and is covered by patents on the Gateway system, though the underlying lambda Int recombination machinery is well-characterized in the literature.

description: attB2 is the second bacterial attachment site in Invitrogen's Gateway cloning system, derived from bacteriophage lambda site-specific recombination machinery. It is incorporated at the 3' end of a gene of interest via the reverse PCR primer and recombines specifically with attP2 during the BP Clonase reaction to directionally clone inserts into donor vectors. Together with attB1 at the 5' end, attB2 flanks the insert to define orientation and reading frame in downstream Gateway-compatible destination vectors.
mechanism: The lambda integrase (Int) protein, assisted by integration host factor (IHF), catalyzes strand exchange between attB and attP sites through a Holliday junction intermediate; the commercial BP Clonase enzyme mix recapitulates this reaction in vitro. Sequence divergence in the overlap region between site-pair variants (attB1/attP1 versus attB2/attP2) enforces directional specificity, preventing recombination between mismatched partners and ensuring the insert cannot be cloned in reverse orientation.
known_variants: attB2r — reading-frame-shifted variant enabling C-terminal tag fusions without additional stop codons
attB2 with in-frame stop codon — used when no downstream peptide tag is desired
Multisite Gateway attB2 — used in four- or five-fragment multisite recombination assemblies
Topo-adapted attB2 — modified for directional TOPO/Gateway hybrid cloning vectors
known_misannotations: Confused with attP2 (found in donor vectors) or attL2/attR2 (found in entry and destination clones respectively); the correct att site type depends on cloning history
Annotated generically as 'att site' or 'recombination site' without specifying the B/P/L/R class or the 1/2 index
Labeled attB2 on the donor vector itself (pDONR), when the donor vector actually carries attP2 before the BP reaction
Sequence span reported inconsistently — some annotations include only the 7 bp overlap core, others include the full ~25–30 bp minimal functional site
notes: attB2 is specific to the Invitrogen/Thermo Fisher Gateway recombination platform and is not interchangeable with Cre/lox, Flp/FRT, or other site-specific recombination systems. After LR recombination into a destination vector, the expression clone retains attB1 and attB2 flanking the insert; these sites encode short amino acid sequences that become part of the expressed protein if no stop codon precedes attB2. Researchers must account for the attB2-encoded peptide tail (~6–8 aa) when designing C-terminal fusion proteins or epitope-tag constructs.

description: attP1 is a modified bacteriophage lambda phage attachment site used in the Gateway recombination cloning system. It resides in Gateway destination vectors flanking the negative selection cassette (ccdB/CmR), and undergoes site-specific recombination to transfer an expression insert from an entry clone. The numeric suffix '1' distinguishes it from the orthogonal attP2 site, enforcing insert directionality.
mechanism: attP1 undergoes integrase (Int) and integration host factor (IHF)-mediated site-specific recombination with attB1 in the BP reaction, or with attL1 in the LR reaction catalyzed by Gateway Clonase enzyme mix (Int/IHF/Xis). Recombination at the 15 bp overlap core generates hybrid attL1 or attR1 sites in the respective products.
known_variants: attP2 — orthogonal partner site ensuring unidirectional, in-frame cloning; recombines only with attB2/attL2/attR2
Native lambda attP (~243 bp) — the wild-type phage attachment site from which Gateway attP1 was derived and modified for orthogonality
known_misannotations: Confused with attL1 or attR1, which are hybrid recombination products generated after a BP or LR reaction respectively
Annotated generically as 'att site' or 'lambda att' without specifying the Gateway variant number or directionality
Occasionally mislabeled as attP (unnumbered), conflating the native lambda attP with the engineered Gateway attP1
notes: attP1 is an Invitrogen/Thermo Fisher-engineered derivative of the lambda phage attP site; the full lambda attP is ~240 bp with arm sequences required for Int/IHF binding. Destination vectors bearing attP1/attP2 flanking ccdB rely on ccdB toxicity to counter-select non-recombinant plasmid in standard E. coli strains (DB3.1 is ccdB-resistant and used for destination vector propagation). The orthogonality of att1 vs att2 sites prevents inverted or tandem insertions.

description: attP2 is the second phage-side attachment site used in Invitrogen's Gateway recombination cloning system, derived from bacteriophage lambda site-specific recombination. It resides in Gateway donor vectors (e.g., pDONR series) and undergoes a BP reaction with attB2 on a PCR product to generate an attL2–insert–attL1 entry clone. The numeric suffix distinguishes it from attP1, enforcing directional, orientation-specific insertion of the gene of interest.
mechanism: The BP Clonase enzyme mix (lambda Integrase + Integration Host Factor) catalyzes recombination between attB2 on the incoming DNA and attP2 in the donor vector at a 7-bp overlap (core) sequence, producing attL2 and attR2 flanking sites; the reaction is thermodynamically reversible via LR Clonase (Integrase + IHF + Excisionase).
known_variants: attP2 (MultiSite Gateway) — used in three- and four-fragment MultiSite Gateway assemblies alongside attP3, attP4, attP5
Minimal attP2 core — truncated versions retaining only the 25-bp overlap region used in some synthetic constructs
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'attP' without the '2' suffix, losing directionality information and causing confusion with attP1
Confused with attB2, attL2, or attR2 — all related but functionally distinct sites in the Gateway pathway
Sometimes annotated as a generic 'recombination site' without specifying Gateway system context
Numeric suffix dropped in older annotations (pre-MultiSite era), making orientation ambiguous
notes: attP2 is functionally inseparable from attP1 in the Gateway system; both must be present in the donor vector for directional cloning. The 1/2 numbering encodes directionality: attP1 recombines exclusively with attB1, attP2 exclusively with attB2, preventing inversions. In MultiSite Gateway, additional att site pairs (attP3–attP5) allow simultaneous assembly of three or four fragments. The ccdB negative-selection cassette flanked by attP1–attP2 in pDONR vectors kills non-recombinant colonies. attP sites are substantially longer than attB sites (~240 bp vs ~25 bp) because they include the arm sequences required for cooperative Int binding.

description: attL1 is the left hybrid recombination site generated by Lambda Int-mediated recombination between attB1 and attP1 during the BP reaction in the Invitrogen Gateway cloning system. It flanks the 5′ end of the insert in Gateway Entry clones and serves as the substrate for LR Clonase during directional transfer of inserts into destination vectors. Because attL1 and attL2 carry distinct arm sequences, they recombine exclusively with their cognate partners (attR1 and attR2, respectively), ensuring unidirectional, sequence-specific cloning.
mechanism: Lambda integrase (Int) and integration host factor (IHF) catalyze strand exchange at the shared 7-bp crossover core (O sequence: CAACTTTT); the flanking P and B arm sequences unique to attL1 determine partner specificity, restricting recombination to the cognate attR1 site during the LR reaction.
known_variants: attL2 — partner site with distinct arm sequences, flanks the 3′ end of the insert in Entry clones
attL1 in multi-site Gateway vectors (e.g., pDONR-P1P4) using alternate numbered att sites
Mutant att sites (att-Δarm) with altered Int-binding affinity used in some academic systems
known_misannotations: Annotated simply as 'att' or 'lox' without specifying hybrid type or variant number
Confused with attB1 (the pre-BP-reaction donor site on the PCR product)
Mistakenly labeled as attR1 (the complementary right-side hybrid site in the destination vector)
Labeled 'Gateway site' without distinguishing attL from attR or specifying the 1 vs. 2 variant
notes: attL1 is a proprietary element of the Gateway recombination cloning system (Thermo Fisher Scientific / Invitrogen). It is always paired with attL2 to flank the gene of interest in an Entry clone. NC_001416 (Lambda phage genome) contains the ancestral attL site from which Gateway att variants were derived, but the Gateway-numbered attL1 sequence carries additional engineered specificity mutations not present in wild-type Lambda attL. The 7-bp O core is identical across all att variants and is the precise strand-exchange locus.

description: attR1 is an engineered bacteriophage λ site-specific recombination sequence used in the Gateway cloning system. It is present in Gateway Destination vectors (DEST vectors), flanking the negative-selection ccdB cassette on the 5′ side, and participates in the LR Clonase reaction to exchange the ccdB insert for a gene of interest. The LR reaction between attL1/attL2 (Entry clone) and attR1/attR2 (Destination vector) produces an expression clone bearing attB1 and attB2 sites.
mechanism: The LR Clonase enzyme mix (bacteriophage λ integrase Int, integration host factor IHF, and excisionase Xis) catalyzes directional, strand-exchange recombination between attL and attR half-sites at a shared 7 bp overlap (core O region). attR1 recombines exclusively with attL1 (not attL2), because the variant core sequences confer specificity and prevent cross-reactivity.
known_variants: Multisite Gateway attR1: used in multi-fragment assemblies (attR1/attR2/attR3/attR4/attR5 series) with distinct core sequences preventing cross-reactivity
Truncated attR1: minimal 25 bp core-only form sometimes annotated in simplified vector maps
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'attR' without the '1' suffix, creating ambiguity with attR2 or wild-type lambda attR
Confused with the native bacteriophage λ attR site, which differs in arm sequence composition and flanking context
Occasionally mislabeled as 'attB1' or 'attL1' when vector orientation is misread
Annotation length varies widely (25–150 bp) depending on whether arm sequences are included, causing feature boundary inconsistencies across databases
notes: attR1 is functional in all host organisms because the LR Clonase enzymes are supplied exogenously in vitro; no host factor is required. The '1' designation distinguishes it from attR2 by a 4 bp difference within the 7 bp overlap core, making the two sites non-cross-reactive. Destination vectors contain a ccdB negative-selection marker between attR1 and attR2; bacteria lacking the F′ episome (ΔccdB strains) must be used to propagate DEST vectors. After successful LR recombination, the ccdB cassette is replaced by the insert and selection switches to the vector's standard antibiotic marker.

description: The bacterial attachment site (attB) recognized by the PhiC31 serine integrase from Streptomyces phage ΦC31. It recombines site-specifically with the phage attachment site (attP) to drive unidirectional, irreversible integration of an attB-bearing donor plasmid into a genome locus carrying attP (a 'landing pad'). Widely used for stable single-copy genomic integration in Drosophila, mammalian cells, zebrafish, and plants.
mechanism: The PhiC31 integrase catalyzes recombination between attB (~34 bp) and attP (~39 bp) via a serine-mediated double-strand break mechanism, generating hybrid attL and attR product sites; the reaction is unidirectional in the absence of an excisionase cofactor, ensuring stable integration without spontaneous excision.
known_variants: Minimal attB — 34 bp crossover core only, no flanking arm sequences
Extended attB — ~50 bp including short arm sequences for improved recombination efficiency
Pseudo-attB sites — endogenous sequences in human, mouse, and Drosophila genomes recognized at low frequency by PhiC31 integrase
known_misannotations: Confused with Gateway attB1/attB2 sites, which are Lambda phage-derived and use an entirely different integrase; the shared 'attB' name causes frequent mislabeling
attP (phage/landing-pad site) mislabeled as attB, or vice versa — attP is larger (~39 bp) and goes into the genome, attB goes on the donor vector
Hybrid attL or attR product sites generated post-integration mislabeled as attB
Annotated generically as 'att site' or 'recombination site' without specifying the PhiC31 system
notes: PhiC31 attB is placed on the donor/transfer plasmid; the complementary attP landing pad must be pre-integrated in the target genome. The system is orthogonal to Cre/lox, FLP/FRT, and Gateway (Lambda) recombination. Off-target integration into pseudo-attB sites in mammalian genomes is a known concern, particularly in therapeutic contexts. Do not confuse with the nomenclature used in Gateway cloning, where attB1 and attB2 designate Lambda-derived sites and the 'B' refers to a completely different biochemical context.

description: PhiC31 attP is the phage attachment site from Streptomyces bacteriophage ΦC31, recognized by PhiC31 integrase to mediate site-specific recombination with the cognate bacterial attachment site attB. It is widely deployed in Drosophila transgenesis, mammalian gene therapy, and plant transformation to achieve stable, single-copy genomic integration at defined 'landing pad' loci. Because the reaction produces hybrid attL and attR sites that are not substrates for re-excision, integrations are essentially permanent without a dedicated excisionase.
mechanism: PhiC31 integrase, a large serine recombinase, binds attP and attB, synapses the two sites, and catalyzes a double-strand break-and-rejoining reaction via a covalent serine-phosphodiester intermediate, requiring no ATP, metal cofactors, or DNA supercoiling. The 39 bp crossover region within attP is the minimal sequence required for recombination; flanking arm sequences increase efficiency but are not essential.
known_variants: Minimal attP (39 bp core crossover region only; reduced efficiency vs full-length site)
Extended attP (~400 bp; includes full arm sequences for maximal recombination efficiency)
Pseudo-attP sites (naturally occurring near-cognate sequences in mammalian genomes that support low-efficiency integration)
known_misannotations: Confused with attB site — both are short sequences from the same system but attP is phage-derived and attB is host-derived; they are not interchangeable
attL or attR hybrid sites (products of integration) mislabeled as attP in downstream cloning vectors
Confused with lambda phage attP — lambda and PhiC31 use entirely different recombinase families (tyrosine vs serine) and att sites are not cross-compatible
Full attP arm region omitted in constructs and labeled as 'attP' when only the minimal 39 bp core is present
notes: PhiC31 is particularly valuable because its integrase functions without accessory host factors, making it active across a wide range of organisms. The high GC content reflects the Streptomyces origin (~72% genomic GC). Pseudo-attP sites in mammalian genomes (e.g., human chromosome 8p22) can serve as unintended off-target integration sites; genome-wide mapping of these has been performed in human, mouse, and Drosophila. The integrase gene itself must be supplied in trans (typically on a co-transfected plasmid or mRNA) and is not part of the attP site. For landing-pad strategies, attP is inserted genomically first, and the donor vector carries attB.

description: SpCas9 is an RNA-guided DNA endonuclease from Streptococcus pyogenes and the most widely used CRISPR effector for genome editing. In plasmid engineering it is delivered as an expression cassette to provide the Cas9 protein that, together with a single guide RNA, generates a site-specific double-strand break in target DNA.
mechanism: SpCas9 forms a ribonucleoprotein complex with an sgRNA and scans dsDNA for a 5'-NGG-3' protospacer-adjacent motif (PAM); upon PAM recognition the HNH and RuvC nuclease domains each cleave one strand, generating a blunt-ended double-strand break 3 bp upstream of the PAM.
known_variants: Human codon-optimized SpCas9 (hSpCas9) — optimized for mammalian expression, ~55–65% GC
eSpCas9(1.1) — enhanced specificity via REC3 domain charge neutralization
SpCas9-HF1 — high-fidelity variant (N497A/R661A/Q695A/Q926A)
HypaCas9 — hyper-accurate via N692A/M694A/Q695A/H698A mutations
SpCas9-D10A nickase — RuvC inactivated, generates single-strand nicks only
SpCas9-H840A nickase — HNH inactivated, generates single-strand nicks only
dSpCas9 — catalytically dead (D10A + H840A), used for CRISPRi/CRISPRa/base editing scaffolds
xCas9 — evolved for expanded PAM compatibility (NG, GAA, GAT)
SpRY — near-PAMless variant accepting NRN and NYN PAMs
SpCas9-VQR/VRQR — engineered PAM variants recognizing NGA/NGAG
Cas9-BE3 / ABE — base editor fusions retaining nickase activity
known_misannotations: Labeled simply 'Cas9' without species prefix, causing confusion with SaCas9 (~3.2 kb, NNGRRT PAM) and other orthologs
NLS sequences (SV40 NLS, nucleoplasmin bipartite NLS) inconsistently included or excluded from annotated feature boundaries
2A self-cleaving peptide and downstream fluorescent protein or selection marker fusions sometimes folded into the Cas9 annotation
dSpCas9 (nuclease-dead) mislabeled as SpCas9, masking absence of cleavage activity
Nickase variants (D10A or H840A) annotated as 'Cas9' without indicating single-strand-nick-only activity
SaCas9 confused with SpCas9 due to shared 'Cas9' name despite being a different species ortholog
notes: SpCas9 encodes 1368 amino acids (~4.1 kb CDS). Native S. pyogenes sequence is ~35% GC; human-codon-optimized versions reach 55–65% GC, causing substantial length-normalized GC variation across constructs. Nearly all mammalian expression vectors include one or two nuclear localization signals fused N- and/or C-terminally. Patent landscape is complex (Broad Institute vs. UC Berkeley/Doudna); commercial use may require licensing. SaCas9 from Staphylococcus aureus (~3.2 kb) is a common alternative chosen for its smaller size and AAV-compatibility.

description: SaCas9 is the CRISPR-associated RNA-guided endonuclease from Staphylococcus aureus, used as a compact alternative to SpCas9 for genome editing. Its smaller coding sequence (~3.2 kb vs ~4.2 kb for SpCas9) enables single-vector AAV packaging alongside sgRNA expression cassettes and regulatory elements. It is widely deployed in in vivo therapeutic genome editing where AAV payload capacity is the primary constraint.
mechanism: SaCas9 assembles with a single guide RNA (sgRNA) into a surveillance complex that scans double-stranded DNA for a 5'-NNGRRT-3' PAM sequence (R = A or G), then unwinds and interrogates the adjacent protospacer; upon 20-nt complementarity, it cleaves both strands via its RuvC and HNH domains to produce blunt-ended double-strand breaks repaired by NHEJ or HDR.
known_variants: Human codon-optimized SaCas9 (most common form in mammalian plasmids; same length, higher GC than native)
SaCas9-KKH (E782K/N968K/R1015H; relaxed PAM NNNRRT, expands targetable sites)
dSaCas9 (D10A + H840A catalytic dead; used as CRISPRi/CRISPRa scaffold)
SaCas9n (D10A nickase; single-strand nick for paired-guide HDR strategies)
SaCas9-ABE / SaCas9-CBE base editors (fused to deaminase domains for A-to-G or C-to-T edits)
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'Cas9' without species qualifier, conflating with SpCas9 and different PAM requirements
PAM sequence listed as NGG (SpCas9 PAM) instead of the correct NNGRRT
Native S. aureus sequence and human codon-optimized version used interchangeably in plasmid records despite differing GC content
NLS-SaCas9-NLS fusion sometimes annotated as a single 'SaCas9' feature, obscuring NLS boundaries
notes: SaCas9 is ~1,053 aa (3,159 bp CDS), making it the most widely used small Cas9 ortholog for AAV delivery. Its NNGRRT PAM is more restrictive than SpCas9's NGG, reducing targetable sites by roughly 3-fold but potentially lowering off-target activity. Nuclear localization signals (NLS) are invariably fused N- and/or C-terminally for mammalian use and are often included within the annotated feature boundaries. SaCas9-KKH is the dominant PAM-expanded variant when targeting flexibility is needed. The wide GC range in expected_gc_fraction reflects the difference between the low-GC native S. aureus coding sequence (~33% GC genome) and human codon-optimized constructs (~50–55% GC).

description: AsCas12a is a Class 2 Type V-A CRISPR-Cas RNA-guided endonuclease from Acidaminococcus sp. BV3L6 that recognizes 5'-TTTV-3' PAM sequences and generates staggered double-strand breaks. It is used for genome editing, transcriptional regulation (dCas12a), and nucleic acid diagnostics (DETECTR platform), and is valued for its smaller crRNA, ability to self-process multiplex crRNA arrays without a tracrRNA, and generation of 5' overhangs compatible with directional cloning.
mechanism: AsCas12a uses a single RuvC nuclease domain to cleave both strands of dsDNA with a staggered cut that produces ~5-nt 5' overhangs, guided by a single ~42-nt crRNA with no tracrRNA required. After target cleavage, activated AsCas12a exhibits non-specific trans-cleavage of single-stranded DNA, a property exploited in diagnostic applications.
known_variants: Human codon-optimized AsCas12a (hAsCas12a) — standard for mammalian cell editing, same length as native
enAsCas12a (enhanced) — engineered substitutions in the PAM-interacting domain improve activity at non-canonical TTTV PAMs
AsCas12a-RR — altered PAM specificity toward TYCV PAMs (Kleinstiver et al. 2019)
AsCas12a-RVR — altered PAM specificity toward TATV PAMs (Kleinstiver et al. 2019)
dAsCas12a — D908A catalytic dead mutant used for CRISPRi/CRISPRa without cutting
AsCas12a Ultra — further engineered for robust activity in diverse cell types
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'Cpf1' without organism prefix, creating ambiguity with LbCas12a (Lachnospiraceae bacterium ND2006), which has a distinct PAM and efficiency profile
Confused with LbCas12a in vector annotations; the two share ~35% sequence identity and differ significantly in on-target activity in human cells
Older annotations use the deprecated name 'AsCpf1' without cross-referencing Cas12a, leading to failed synonym lookups
enAsCas12a variants annotated as wild-type AsCas12a, obscuring the presence of PAM-altering mutations
notes: AsCas12a was originally named AsCpf1 (CRISPR from Prevotella and Francisella 1) and renamed under the unified Cas12a nomenclature. Its T-rich PAM (5'-TTTV-3' on non-target strand, 5' of protospacer) makes it orthogonal and complementary to SpCas9 (NGG PAM), enabling targeting of AT-rich genomic regions inaccessible to SpCas9. The native CDS encodes a 1,307-amino acid protein (~147 kDa). AsCas12a generally exhibits lower on-target activity in mammalian cells compared to LbCas12a but is more extensively characterized structurally and has more available engineered variants.

description: CjCas9 is the RNA-guided endonuclease from Campylobacter jejuni and one of the smallest naturally occurring Cas9 orthologs (~984 aa, ~2.95 kb CDS). Its compact size allows co-packaging with its sgRNA into a single adeno-associated virus (AAV) vector, making it uniquely suited for in vivo gene therapy applications where cargo limits are critical. It is used in plasmid engineering to deliver targeted double-strand breaks at genomic loci defined by a single guide RNA.
mechanism: CjCas9 assembles with a single guide RNA (sgRNA) into a ribonucleoprotein complex that scans dsDNA for the PAM sequence NNNNRYAC (R = purine, Y = pyrimidine). Upon target recognition, conformational activation of the RuvC and HNH nuclease domains generates a blunt-ended double-strand break three bases upstream of the PAM.
known_variants: Human codon-optimized CjCas9 (hCjCas9) — standard for mammalian expression
Catalytically dead dCjCas9 (D8A/H559A) — for CRISPRi and CRISPRa applications
CjCas9 nickase — single catalytic domain inactivated for reduced off-targets
CjCas9 base editor fusions — linked to cytidine or adenine deaminase domains
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'Cas9' without organism qualifier, causing confusion with SpCas9
Confused with SaCas9 (Staphylococcus aureus Cas9), another small Cas9 ortholog
PAM incorrectly cited as NNGRRT (which is the SaCas9 PAM, not CjCas9)
Occasionally mislabeled as 'CjeCas9' with inconsistent abbreviation conventions
notes: CjCas9's primary advantage over SpCas9 is its ~2.95 kb CDS, which together with an sgRNA expression cassette (~0.5 kb) fits within the ~4.7 kb single-stranded cargo limit of AAV vectors. The 8-nt PAM (NNNNRYAC) is more restrictive than SpCas9 NGG, reducing targetable sites genome-wide. GC content varies substantially between native C. jejuni sequence (~30% GC, reflecting the organism's AT-rich genome) and human codon-optimized versions (~50–55%). First characterized for in vivo editing by Kim et al. (2017, Nat Commun). The low native GC can cause instability in E. coli expression contexts; codon-optimized versions are preferred for most plasmid applications.

description: nSpCas9 (D10A) is a nickase variant of Streptococcus pyogenes Cas9 carrying a D10A point mutation in the RuvC nuclease domain, which generates single-strand DNA nicks rather than double-strand breaks. It is widely used in paired-nickase genome editing strategies (two offset gRNAs directed to opposite strands) that dramatically reduce off-target indels relative to wild-type SpCas9. It also serves as the core scaffold in cytosine and adenine base editors (e.g., BE3, ABEmax) and in CRISPRi/a systems requiring low genotoxicity.
mechanism: The D10A substitution inactivates the RuvC catalytic domain of SpCas9 while leaving the HNH domain functional; guide RNA-directed binding results in a nick on only the strand complementary to the guide RNA (the target strand), leaving the non-target strand intact.
known_variants: Human codon-optimized (hSpCas9-D10A) — standard for mammalian expression
BE3 — nSpCas9-D10A fused to rAPOBEC1 + UGI for cytosine base editing (C→T)
ABE7.10 / ABEmax — nSpCas9-D10A fused to evolved TadA adenosine deaminase for A→G base editing
eSpCas9-D10A — enhanced specificity via K848A/K1003A/R1060A charge-neutralization mutations plus D10A
HiFi Cas9-D10A — R691A + D10A combination for reduced off-target activity
nSaCas9-D10A — Staphylococcus aureus Cas9 D10A ortholog (smaller CDS ~3,156 bp; PAM: NNGRRT)
With SV40 or nucleoplasmin NLS — N- or C-terminal nuclear localization signal fusions for nuclear targeting
known_misannotations: Labeled 'dCas9' or 'dead Cas9' — dCas9 requires both D10A and H840A; D10A alone retains HNH activity and is a nickase, not catalytically dead
Confused with H840A nickase — H840A inactivates HNH and nicks the non-target strand via RuvC; D10A nicks the opposite (target) strand, a critical distinction for paired-nickase polarity
Annotated as 'Cas9' without noting the D10A mutation, causing the feature to appear as a wild-type nuclease
Labeled 'nCas9' without specifying D10A vs H840A, creating ambiguity in paired-nickase offset design
notes: D10A is always an engineered/synthetic construct; no wild-type organism carries this mutation. The most common deposit is a human codon-optimized CDS (~4,104 bp). In paired-nickase designs, gRNA offsets of 0–100 bp on opposite strands efficiently generate DSBs suitable for HDR; offsets >200 bp yield predominantly independent nicks and are unsuitable for DSB-dependent editing. The D10A scaffold is preferred over H840A for base editors because HNH nicking of the target strand is the appropriate substrate for BER-pathway base conversion. Always verify which domain is mutated when interpreting nCas9-containing constructs.

description: dSpCas9 is a catalytically inactive derivative of Streptococcus pyogenes Cas9 carrying D10A and H840A point mutations that ablate both RuvC and HNH nuclease activities while preserving sgRNA-guided DNA binding. It is used as a programmable DNA-targeting scaffold for CRISPRi (gene repression), CRISPRa (gene activation), base editing, epigenome editing, and live-cell genomic imaging when fused to appropriate effector domains.
mechanism: The D10A substitution inactivates the RuvC-like nuclease domain and H840A inactivates the HNH domain; the double mutant binds target dsDNA with full guide-RNA specificity (NGG PAM required) but cannot cleave either strand. Effector proteins fused to dSpCas9 (e.g., KRAB repressor, VP64 activator, DNMT3A, TET1, p300) are thereby recruited to user-defined genomic loci.
known_variants: Human codon-optimized dSpCas9 (most common in mammalian vectors)
E. coli codon-optimized dSpCas9 (bacterial CRISPRi)
dSpCas9-KRAB fusion (transcriptional repression)
dSpCas9-VP64 fusion (transcriptional activation)
dSpCas9-VPR fusion (VP64-p65-Rta; enhanced activation)
dSpCas9-p300 core fusion (histone H3K27 acetylation)
dSpCas9-TET1 fusion (targeted DNA demethylation)
dSpCas9-DNMT3A fusion (targeted DNA methylation)
SunTag-dSpCas9 (amplified effector recruitment via scFv-fused activators)
dSpCas9 with bipartite NLS (optimized nuclear import in mammalian cells)
known_misannotations: Annotated simply as 'Cas9' without indicating catalytic inactivity, causing confusion with nuclease-active SpCas9
Labeled 'dCas9' without organism qualifier, conflated with dead variants of other Cas9 orthologs (e.g., dSaCas9, dAsCas12a)
Fused effector domain (KRAB, VP64, etc.) included within the annotated dCas9 feature span rather than annotated separately
Referred to as 'CRISPRi' or 'CRISPRa' in the feature name, obscuring the underlying dCas9 coding sequence
notes: dSpCas9 retains all PAM recognition (NGG) and guide RNA requirements of wild-type SpCas9; only cleavage is abolished. The coding sequence is identical in length (~4,107 bp, 1,368 aa) to SpCas9. GC content varies widely depending on codon optimization: native S. pyogenes sequence is ~30–35% GC; human-codon-optimized versions are ~55–65% GC. Nuclear localization signals (mono- or bipartite NLS) are typically appended for mammalian nuclear targeting. When annotating, the dCas9 ORF and any fused effector domain should be annotated as distinct features to avoid length-based misidentification.

description: Cas13a (formerly C2c2) is a Type VI-A CRISPR RNA-guided RNA endonuclease that targets and cleaves single-stranded RNA rather than DNA. It is encoded in plasmid vectors for programmable RNA knockdown, antiviral applications, RNA diagnostics (SHERLOCK), and as a scaffold for RNA base editing when catalytically inactivated (dCas13a). Unlike Cas9, Cas13a acts solely on RNA targets without altering genomic DNA sequences.
mechanism: Cas13a uses a CRISPR RNA (crRNA) spacer to guide the protein to complementary single-stranded RNA; target binding activates two HEPN (Higher Eukaryotes and Prokaryotes Nucleotide-binding) domains that cleave the target transcript. Once activated, Cas13a also exhibits non-specific collateral RNA degradation, a property exploited in nucleic acid diagnostics but a consideration for in vivo applications.
known_variants: LwaCas13a — Leptotrichia wadei ortholog, most widely used in mammalian systems with well-characterized crRNA design rules
LshCas13a — Leptotrichia shahii ortholog, first experimentally characterized Cas13a; requires non-G protospacer flanking site (PFS) in bacteria
LbaCas13a — Leptotrichia buccalis ortholog, active in mammalian cells
dCas13a — catalytically dead variant carrying HEPN domain point mutations (e.g., R474A/H479A in LwaCas13a); used for RNA tracking and scaffolding
Cas13a-ADAR2DD fusion — dCas13a fused to adenosine deaminase acting on RNA 2 (ADAR2) for programmable A-to-I RNA base editing (REPAIR system)
Codon-optimized Cas13a — human codon-optimized ORFs for improved mammalian expression, commonly with NLS or NES tags
known_misannotations: Labeled 'C2c2' in older plasmid deposits — the previous name before systematic Type VI CRISPR nomenclature was adopted in 2017
Confused with Cas13b, Cas13c, or Cas13d (CasRx) — all are Type VI CRISPR effectors but are distinct proteins encoded by different genes with different PFS requirements and sizes
Occasionally mis-annotated as a DNA nuclease or Cas9 variant; Cas13a is strictly an RNA endonuclease with no reported DNA-cutting activity
dCas13a fusions sometimes annotated only by the effector domain name (e.g., 'ADAR2DD') without indicating the Cas13a scaffold
notes: Collateral cleavage (non-specific bystander RNA degradation upon target engagement) is a key property exploited in SHERLOCK diagnostics but may cause cytotoxicity in mammalian cells at high expression levels. LwaCas13a is the preferred ortholog for mammalian knockdown experiments due to relaxed PFS requirements and high on-target activity. The crRNA architecture (direct repeat + spacer) differs from Cas9 sgRNAs and must be matched to the specific Cas13a ortholog used.

description: The sgRNA scaffold is the invariant structural RNA backbone of the single guide RNA used to direct Streptococcus pyogenes Cas9 (SpCas9) to a target genomic locus. It is derived from the tracrRNA of S. pyogenes and mimics the crRNA:tracrRNA duplex that naturally recruits Cas9, serving as the constant portion cloned downstream of a variable 20 nt spacer sequence. In plasmid engineering, the scaffold is expressed under an RNA Pol III promoter (typically U6 or H1) and is sufficient to form a functional Cas9 ribonucleoprotein complex when paired with any spacer.
mechanism: The scaffold folds into a series of RNA secondary structures — including a repeat:anti-repeat duplex, a nexus hairpin, and stem loops 1–3 — that are specifically recognized and bound by the SpCas9 REC and HNH/RuvC lobes. Upon binding, the resulting Cas9–sgRNA ribonucleoprotein scans DNA for an NGG PAM sequence, unwinds the adjacent duplex, and cleaves both strands through the HNH and RuvC nuclease domains to generate a blunt-ended double-strand break.
known_variants: Extended scaffold (Chen et al. 2013) — ~20 nt 3′ extension of stem loop 3 improves editing efficiency in mammalian cells
MS2 aptamer scaffold (SAM system, Konermann et al. 2015) — stem loop 2 replaced with MS2 hairpin for CRISPRa transcriptional activation
Chemically modified scaffold — 2′-O-methyl and phosphorothioate modifications at 5′ and 3′ termini for improved nuclease stability in RNP delivery
Optimized scaffold (Dang et al. 2015) — point mutations (A→U at position 4 of nexus) improve sgRNA folding and Cas9 binding affinity
PP7 aptamer scaffold — stem loop 2 replaced with PP7 hairpin for orthogonal CRISPRi recruitment
Truncated scaffold (17–18 nt spacer pairing) — reduces off-target cleavage at cost of mild on-target efficiency
known_misannotations: Entire sgRNA expression cassette (U6 promoter + spacer + scaffold + poly-T terminator) annotated as 'sgRNA scaffold'
Scaffold mislabeled as 'tracrRNA' despite being a chimeric fusion sequence not naturally occurring in S. pyogenes
Scaffold region confused with or merged into the 20 nt spacer (target-specific) sequence annotation
Full sgRNA (spacer + scaffold) annotated as just 'scaffold' collapsing the functional distinction
U6 promoter or poly-T terminator bases included within scaffold annotation boundaries
notes: The canonical scaffold sequence (~80 nt) used in most plasmids originates from the Jinek et al. 2012 (Science) chimeric guide design, with the extended form popularized by the Zhang lab (pX330 series). The scaffold is SpCas9-specific and incompatible with other Cas9 orthologs (SaCas9, NmCas9) or Cas12a/Cpf1, each of which requires its own cognate scaffold. Only the 20 nt spacer upstream of the scaffold determines genomic targeting; the scaffold sequence is invariant. For dCas9-based applications (CRISPRa, CRISPRi, base editing, prime editing), the same scaffold is used as in nuclease-active SpCas9 constructs.

description: The sgRNA scaffold for SaCas9 (Staphylococcus aureus Cas9) is the fixed structural RNA component that, when fused 3′ to a 20–24 nt spacer sequence, forms the complete single guide RNA directing SaCas9 to its genomic target via an NNGRRT PAM. SaCas9 is widely used for AAV-based in vivo genome editing due to its compact coding sequence (~3.2 kb) relative to SpCas9. The scaffold is derived from the S. aureus CRISPR2 locus tracrRNA and is structurally and functionally distinct from the SpCas9 scaffold.
mechanism: The scaffold folds into a series of stem-loop structures—including a repeat:anti-repeat duplex, nexus hairpin, and two terminal hairpins—that are recognized by and recruit the SaCas9 protein; the 5′ spacer sequence then base-pairs with a complementary DNA strand adjacent to an NNGRRT PAM, positioning SaCas9 to introduce a blunt-ended double-strand break via its RuvC and HNH nuclease domains.
known_variants: Extended scaffold with optimized repeat:anti-repeat duplex stem for improved expression and activity
MS2 aptamer-tagged scaffold for CRISPRa/CRISPRi recruitment of transcriptional effectors via MCP fusion proteins
SaKKH-Cas9 compatible scaffold (identical scaffold sequence; altered PAM specificity resides in the protein, not the RNA)
known_misannotations: Confused with SpCas9 sgRNA scaffold — the two scaffolds are structurally non-interchangeable and swapping them abolishes editing activity
Labeled as 'tracrRNA' rather than 'sgRNA scaffold', obscuring that the expressed unit is the full spacer-scaffold chimera
Annotated as generic 'sgRNA' or 'guide RNA' without specifying the Cas9 ortholog, causing mismatched scaffold-protein pairings in cloning
notes: The SaCas9 sgRNA scaffold is not interchangeable with the SpCas9 scaffold; using the wrong scaffold with the wrong Cas9 ortholog abolishes editing activity. Length reported here excludes the 20–24 nt spacer. In mammalian expression plasmids, the spacer is typically cloned upstream of the scaffold using BbsI or BsmBI Golden Gate sites. The SaCas9 system requires NNGRRT PAM sequences, limiting targetable sites compared to SpCas9 (NGG), though this is a protein-level constraint, not a scaffold-level one.

description: The U6 promoter drives transcription of U6 small nuclear RNA by RNA Polymerase III, producing short, non-polyadenylated transcripts with defined 3′ ends. It is the dominant promoter used to express guide RNAs (gRNAs) in CRISPR/Cas9 systems and short hairpin RNAs (shRNAs) for RNAi, owing to its constitutive high activity and precise transcription initiation. Its Pol III dependence ensures compact, unmodified transcripts—critical for functional small RNAs.
mechanism: RNA Polymerase III is recruited via a proximal sequence element (PSE) centered at approximately −55 and a TATA-box-like element at approximately −25 to −30 relative to the +1 transcription start site; transcription terminates at a run of four or more consecutive thymidines in the non-template strand, yielding transcripts without 5′ caps or 3′ poly-A tails.
known_variants: Human U6 (hU6) — derived from the RNU6B locus on human chromosome 12; most widely used form
Mouse U6 (mU6) — derived from the murine Rnu6 locus; ~90% identity to hU6, sometimes preferred in all-mouse constructs
Minimal U6 promoter (~120 bp) — truncated version retaining core PSE and TATA elements with reduced but functional activity
U6+27 extended variant — includes nucleotides +1 to +27 of the native transcript to improve guide RNA loading into RISC or RNP complexes
known_misannotations: Confused with the H1 promoter — another Pol III promoter (from RN7SK/H1 RNA gene) used for similar applications but distinct in sequence and regulatory context
Human and mouse U6 sequences labeled interchangeably despite ~10% sequence divergence and measurable differences in activity in heterologous cell types
Annotated generically as 'RNA Pol III promoter' without specifying U6 identity, obscuring comparisons across constructs
Occasionally mislabeled as U1 or U2 snRNA promoter, which are Pol II-driven and produce capped, polyadenylated transcripts
notes: Guide RNA sequences expressed from U6 must begin with a guanosine at the +1 position for efficient Pol III initiation; a non-G first nucleotide in the target spacer requires addition of a synthetic +1 G, which may slightly reduce on-target efficiency. The U6 promoter is constitutively active and not readily inducible. While optimized for mammalian cells, functional Pol III activity from hU6 has been reported in zebrafish embryos; plant and insect systems typically require species-matched Pol III promoters for reliable expression.

description: The H1 promoter is a human RNA Polymerase III promoter derived from the RPPH1 gene, which encodes the RNA component of the nuclear RNase P complex. It is widely used in plasmid and viral vectors to drive constitutive expression of short hairpin RNAs (shRNAs) for RNA interference (RNAi) applications. Unlike the U6 promoter, H1 does not restrict the +1 transcription start nucleotide, allowing expression of shRNAs beginning with any nucleotide.
mechanism: A type-3 external RNA Pol III promoter with all cis-regulatory elements located upstream of the transcription start site, enabling Pol III to initiate transcription and produce short, non-polyadenylated RNA transcripts terminated by a run of four or more thymidines. The lack of +1 nucleotide constraint (U6 requires a G) gives H1 greater flexibility for shRNA target selection.
known_variants: Minimal H1 promoter (~230 bp truncation retaining full activity)
H1 promoter with downstream Pol III terminator pre-cloned for direct shRNA insertion
known_misannotations: Confused with the H1 histone gene promoter, which is an RNA Pol II promoter and entirely unrelated in sequence and function
Labeled generically as 'Pol III promoter' without distinguishing from U6 or 7SK promoters
Occasionally annotated as 'U6 promoter' when both are used in the same shRNA vector context
notes: H1 is the preferred Pol III promoter when the siRNA target sequence begins with a nucleotide other than G, since U6 mandates a G at the +1 position. H1-driven transcripts are not capped or polyadenylated and are restricted to mammalian (vertebrate) cell applications; it shows little to no activity in yeast, insect, or plant systems. First characterized and popularized in the pSUPER shRNA vector system (Brummelkamp et al., Science 2002).

description: The 7SK promoter is a type 3 external RNA Polymerase III promoter that drives constitutive transcription of the endogenous 7SK snRNA, a ~331 nt regulatory small nuclear RNA that sequesters the P-TEFb (CDK9/cyclin T1) kinase complex to globally suppress transcription elongation. In plasmid engineering, it is repurposed as an alternative Pol III promoter to drive nuclear expression of shRNAs, sgRNAs, and other short non-coding RNAs in mammalian cells, offering a distinct sequence context from the more widely used U6 and H1 promoters.
mechanism: Transcription initiation is governed entirely by upstream external elements — a proximal sequence element (PSE) recruited by the SNAP complex (snRNA-activating protein complex) and a TATA box recruited by TFIIIB — which together assemble the Pol III pre-initiation complex. Because all regulatory elements are upstream of the transcription start site, any desired small RNA sequence can be placed immediately downstream without disrupting promoter activity, and transcription terminates at a run of four or more consecutive thymidines.
reference_plasmids: pRNA-7SK/H1 (shRNA expression vector with 7SK-driven H1 tandem cassette)
known_variants: Minimal 7SK promoter (truncated to core PSE + TATA box, ~150 bp, reduced activity)
Mouse 7SK promoter (murine ortholog, ~88-92% identity to human, functional in rodent cells)
Hybrid 7SK/H1 promoter (chimeric construct combining elements of both type 3 Pol III promoters)
known_misannotations: 7SK snRNA coding sequence sometimes included within the region labeled '7SK promoter', inflating apparent promoter length
Confused with U6 or H1 promoters in vector maps — all are type 3 Pol III promoters but differ significantly in sequence
Occasionally mislabeled as a Pol II promoter due to the presence of a TATA box, which is atypical for Pol III but characteristic of snRNA-type (type 3) Pol III promoters
PSE sometimes annotated as a separate 'enhancer' element rather than as an integral part of the 7SK promoter unit
notes: The 7SK promoter is less commonly used than U6 or H1 but provides a valuable alternative when transcriptional silencing of U6/H1 cassettes is observed (e.g., in stable integration or iPSC contexts). Like all type 3 Pol III promoters, it generates transcripts with a 5'-triphosphate that can trigger innate immune sensors (RIG-I); this is especially relevant for shRNA applications, and ribozyme or Drosha-processing elements are sometimes appended to mitigate immunogenicity. Multiple copies of the 7SK gene exist in the human genome (predominantly on chromosome 6p21.1 within the MHC region), and the promoter is active in essentially all mammalian cell types. Orthologous functional promoters exist across vertebrates but are not active in yeast, insect, or plant systems.

description: The tracrRNA scaffold is the structural RNA component of the single guide RNA (sgRNA) that forms multiple stem-loop hairpins recognized by Cas9 protein. In plasmid engineering, it is fused directly to the 20-nt spacer sequence to create a chimeric sgRNA that directs Cas9 to a genomic target specified by the spacer. It is an essential element in all CRISPR-Cas9 expression cassettes for genome editing, transcriptional regulation (CRISPRa/CRISPRi), and base editing applications.
mechanism: The tracrRNA scaffold folds into a series of stem-loop structures (repeat:anti-repeat duplex, nexus hairpin, and terminal stem-loops) that are recognized and bound by Cas9, inducing the conformational change required for Cas9 to adopt an active, DNA-binding state. The scaffold does not contribute to target specificity; rather, it stabilizes the Cas9-sgRNA ribonucleoprotein complex and positions the spacer for Watson-Crick base pairing with the protospacer adjacent to a PAM site.
known_variants: Minimal scaffold (~67 nt, truncated 3' stem-loop 3; reduced efficiency)
Standard sgRNA scaffold (~80 nt; Jinek et al. 2012 chimeric design)
Extended scaffold (~100 nt; additional 3' stem-loop 3 added by Chen et al. 2013 for improved stability and editing efficiency)
F+E scaffold (extended scaffold with flipped G:U wobble pair corrected to G:C in stem 1 for improved Cas9 binding)
MS2 aptamer-fused scaffold (stem-loop 2 replaced with MS2 hairpin; used in SAM transcriptional activation system)
PP7 aptamer-fused scaffold (used in orthogonal CRISPRa systems)
SaCas9 scaffold (Staphylococcus aureus Cas9; distinct sequence, not interchangeable with SpCas9 scaffold)
AsCas12a scaffold (not a tracrRNA; Cas12a uses pre-crRNA without a tracrRNA — these are sometimes mislabeled)
Codon-optimized or chemically modified sgRNA (for improved nuclease resistance in therapeutic applications)
known_misannotations: Scaffold annotated to include the 20-nt protospacer/spacer, blurring the boundary between the guide sequence and the structural scaffold
Labeled as 'tracrRNA' when the natural dual-RNA system is implied, but actually used as the fused chimeric sgRNA scaffold
U6 promoter or poly-T terminator sequence accidentally included in the annotated scaffold region
SaCas9 scaffold confused with SpCas9 scaffold — sequences are not interchangeable
AsCas12a direct repeat incorrectly called 'tracrRNA scaffold' (Cas12a does not use a tracrRNA)
Minimal and extended scaffold variants annotated interchangeably without specifying which version is present
notes: The standard SpCas9 tracrRNA scaffold originates from Streptococcus pyogenes SF370.
