Scaffold performance is highly context-dependent: the extended scaffold (Chen et al. 2013, Nature Methods) generally outperforms the minimal version in mammalian cells. When inserting aptamer loops for CRISPRa (e.g., SAM system), modification site and loop identity must match the intended effector domain recruitment strategy. Scaffold sequence must be matched to the Cas9 ortholog in use — mixing SpCas9 scaffold with SaCas9 or other orthologs abolishes activity. Chemical modifications at the 5' and 3' ends of synthetic sgRNAs (e.g., 2'-O-methyl, phosphorothioate) improve stability for RNP delivery but are irrelevant in plasmid-expressed contexts.

description: The 5' Long Terminal Repeat of HIV-1 is a ~634 bp regulatory sequence composed of three sub-regions (U3, R, U5) that drives RNA Polymerase II-mediated transcription of downstream viral or transgene sequences. It contains the core HIV-1 promoter with NF-κB enhancer sites, Sp1 binding sites, and a TATA box, and is an essential element in lentiviral expression vectors. In self-inactivating (SIN) vector designs, a ΔU3 deletion in the 3' LTR propagates to both ends after integration, silencing the LTR promoter to reduce genotoxic risk.
mechanism: NF-κB and Sp1 transcription factors bind sites within the U3 sub-region to initiate transcription; the TAR RNA hairpin in the R sub-region then recruits the viral Tat protein together with the host P-TEFb kinase complex, which phosphorylates RNA Pol II CTD to drive efficient transcriptional elongation into the transgene.
known_variants: SIN-LTR (self-inactivating, ΔU3): 3' LTR U3 deletion propagates to both LTRs after integration, abolishing promoter activity and reducing insertional mutagenesis risk
CMV/HIV-1 hybrid LTR: CMV enhancer inserted upstream of HIV-1 U3 to boost transcriptional output in packaging cell lines
RSV/HIV-1 hybrid LTR: RSV U3 replaces HIV-1 U3, used in early-generation lentiviral vectors for Tat-independent production
Tat-independent LTR: TAR mutations or heterologous promoter substitutions that eliminate dependence on Tat for elongation
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'LTR' without specifying 5' or 3' — the two copies are identical in sequence but serve distinct functions (promoter vs. polyadenylation signal suppressed at 5' end)
Confused with the 3' LTR, which in SIN vectors carries a ΔU3 deletion and is transcriptionally inert after integration
CMV enhancer + HIV-1 U3 hybrid constructs sometimes annotated as plain 'HIV-1 LTR', obscuring the chimeric nature
Confused with other retroviral LTRs (MLV LTR, MMLV LTR, RSV LTR) in older vector literature and annotation pipelines
notes: Tat protein is required in trans for full 5' LTR-driven transcription; modern third-generation lentiviral packaging systems eliminate Tat by using hybrid RSV or CMV promoters in the transfer vector. The HIV-1 HXB2 reference LTR (K03455) is 634 bp with U3 of 454 bp, R of 98 bp, and U5 of 82 bp. Activity is restricted to mammalian cells because NF-κB, Sp1, and P-TEFb are required. Regulatory compliance: lentiviral vectors carrying any LTR sequence require biosafety level 2 (BSL-2) handling and replication-incompetence validation.

description: The 3' LTR ΔU3 is a modified HIV-1 long terminal repeat in which most or all of the U3 transcriptional control region has been deleted, yielding a self-inactivating (SIN) lentiviral vector design. It is a defining safety feature of third-generation lentiviral transfer vectors used in gene therapy and stable transgene delivery, first described by Zufferey et al. (1998). Upon viral integration, the deletion propagates to both LTRs of the provirus, silencing LTR-driven transcription and eliminating viral enhancer activity in transduced cells.
mechanism: During lentiviral reverse transcription, a strand-transfer step copies the 3' LTR R region to replace the 5' LTR in the integrated provirus; consequently, a ΔU3 deletion in the 3' LTR template is faithfully propagated to both LTRs post-integration. Removing the U3 enhancer (NF-κB binding sites) and TATA box abolishes promoter activity in the provirus, while the polyadenylation signal at the U3/R boundary is typically retained to allow proper 3'-end processing of vector transcripts during packaging.
known_variants: Minimal ΔU3: complete U3 deletion retaining only R + U5 (~180 bp)
Partial ΔU3: retains ~18 bp of 3' U3 including poly-A signal support (~200 bp)
400 bp ΔU3 deletion as in Zufferey et al. 1998, derived from NL4-3 backbone
Hybrid ΔU3 containing heterologous poly-A signal (e.g., SV40 or BGH) inserted to replace deleted U3 poly-A function
known_misannotations: Annotated simply as '3' LTR' without indicating U3 deletion, making it indistinguishable by name from a wild-type HIV-1 LTR
Confused with the full-length HIV-1 3' LTR (~634 bp) by length-based annotation tools
Labeled 'SIN element' or 'SIN sequence' rather than as a modified LTR, obscuring its structural identity
Occasionally mislabeled as '5' LTR' in reverse-oriented plasmid maps or when vector orientation is unclear
notes: The ΔU3 3' LTR is non-functional as an independent promoter; transgene expression from the integrated provirus must be driven by an internal promoter (e.g., CMV, EF1α, PGK, SFFV). Regulatory agencies (FDA, EMA) require SIN design for clinical lentiviral gene therapy vectors. The exact deletion boundaries vary by vector lineage—most commonly 400 bp is deleted (Zufferey 1998 NL4-3-based) or the entire U3 is removed. This element is used exclusively in the context of packaging a replication-incompetent lentiviral vector and has no activity as an isolated plasmid element.

description: The psi (Ψ) packaging signal is a cis-acting RNA structural element located in the 5' leader of the HIV-1 genome, comprising four stem-loop structures (SL1–SL4) that are essential for selective packaging of viral genomic RNA into assembling virions. In lentiviral vector engineering, the psi element is incorporated into transfer plasmids so that transcribed RNA carrying a transgene is recognized and packaged by Gag proteins supplied in trans from separate packaging plasmids. The extended form (psi+) includes the 5' UTR through a short segment of the gag ORF (with a stop codon introduced) and provides higher packaging efficiency than the minimal core element.
mechanism: The zinc finger domains within the nucleocapsid (NC) region of the Gag polyprotein bind preferentially to the SL3 stem-loop of the psi signal, with additional contacts at SL1 (the dimerization initiation signal, DIS) and SL4; this high-affinity, selective RNA–protein interaction recruits genomic RNA dimers to assembling virions at the plasma membrane while discriminating against the vast excess of cellular mRNAs.
known_variants: Minimal psi (SL1–SL4 core only, ~120 bp): lower packaging efficiency, avoids any gag coding sequence
Extended psi / psi+ (~350–500 bp): includes 5' UTR plus first ~300–360 nt of gag with an introduced stop codon, substantially increases packaging efficiency
SL3-deleted mutant: abolishes packaging, used as negative control in mechanistic studies
Codon-diverged psi constructs: silent mutations introduced to reduce recombination risk in biosafety-improved vector systems
known_misannotations: Extended psi+ annotated as simply 'psi' without indicating the included gag sequence, creating apparent length discrepancies between constructs
Entire HIV-1 5' leader sequence (R+U5+PBS+psi) annotated as 'psi', conflating the packaging signal with flanking 5' UTR elements
Extended psi region labeled 'delta-gag' or 'truncated gag' in some vector maps, obscuring its primary role as a packaging signal
Minimal psi and extended psi+ used interchangeably in plasmid annotations despite measurable differences in titer
notes: The psi signal functions only in the context of a full lentiviral vector system (transfer vector + packaging + envelope plasmids); it has no autonomous activity in standard mammalian expression vectors. Third-generation self-inactivating (SIN) lentiviral vectors retain psi in the transfer plasmid but delete the 3' LTR enhancer, reducing insertional mutagenesis risk. Biosafety considerations require that psi be present only in the transfer vector and absent from packaging constructs to prevent mobilization of packaging sequences.

description: The Rev Response Element (RRE) is a ~351 bp structured RNA element encoded within the HIV-1 env gene that is essential for nuclear export of unspliced and singly spliced viral mRNAs. In lentiviral vector systems, the RRE is incorporated into the transfer vector genome and functions together with the Rev protein (provided in trans on a packaging plasmid) to enable efficient cytoplasmic accumulation of full-length vector RNA and subsequent virion packaging.
mechanism: The RRE folds into a complex multi-stem-loop secondary structure; Rev protein binds cooperatively beginning at the high-affinity stem-loop IIB site and oligomerizes along the RRE to form a ribonucleoprotein complex that recruits CRM1/exportin-1, thereby hijacking the cellular leucine-rich NES export pathway to shuttle RRE-containing transcripts from the nucleus to the cytoplasm.
known_variants: Minimal RRE (~234 bp) retaining full export activity with reduced insert size
Synthetic/codon-context-optimized RRE sequences for improved RNA folding or reduced immunogenicity
HIV-2 RRE (used in alternative lentiviral packaging systems with HIV-2 Rev)
known_misannotations: Labeled simply 'RRE' without specifying HIV-1 vs. HIV-2 origin, conflating non-interchangeable elements
Annotated boundaries vary widely (230–370 bp) across databases due to ambiguity about the minimal functional unit
Occasionally confused with the cPPT/CTS or WPRE elements that also appear in the same multi-element transfer vector cassette
notes: The RRE is an obligate component of first-, second-, and third-generation lentiviral vector systems; its removal abolishes vector RNA packaging. In third-generation self-inactivating (SIN) vectors, Rev is split onto a separate plasmid to reduce recombination risk. Rev-independent alternatives (e.g., Mason-Pfizer monkey virus CTE) exist but yield lower titers. The RRE is located at approximately HXB2 coordinates 7710–8061 (K03455). The Rev–RRE interaction is also a validated antiretroviral drug target, so mutations affecting the IIB stem-loop can ablate function.

description: The central polypurine tract/central termination sequence (cPPT/CTS) is a cis-acting element from HIV-1 that generates a three-stranded DNA flap during plus-strand DNA synthesis in reverse transcription. This DNA flap promotes nuclear import of the lentiviral pre-integration complex, significantly enhancing transduction efficiency — especially in non-dividing cells such as neurons and hematopoietic stem cells. It is routinely included in third-generation lentiviral transfer vectors to improve stable integration frequency.
mechanism: During reverse transcription, plus-strand DNA synthesis initiates at the cPPT and terminates at the CTS, creating a ~99-nucleotide triple-stranded DNA flap. This flap structure facilitates active nuclear import of the pre-integration complex through the nuclear pore complex, enabling efficient transduction independently of cell division.
known_variants: HIV-1 HXB2-derived cPPT/CTS (~118 bp, most common in lentiviral vectors)
SIV-derived cPPT used in SIV-based lentiviral vector systems
Truncated cPPT-only constructs lacking the CTS termination sequence
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'cPPT' alone when the CTS is also present, obscuring the full functional element
Confused with the 3' polypurine tract (PPT) located adjacent to the 3' LTR, which is a distinct element serving a different function
Annotated as 'DNA flap' without specifying the cPPT/CTS origin
Boundary coordinates vary across vectors due to inclusion of differing amounts of flanking HIV-1 pol sequence
notes: Derived from the HIV-1 pol coding region (approximately nt 4773–4890 in HIV-1 HXB2, K03455). The functional benefit in lentiviral vectors is well established experimentally (Zennou et al. 2000, Cell), though the precise mechanism of nuclear import enhancement has been debated. Not required for episomal expression but critical for efficient stable integration in post-mitotic or slowly dividing cells. Absent from gammaretroviral (e.g., MLV-based) vectors, which lack a central PPT.

canonical_name: WPRE (Woodchuck Hepatitis Virus Posttranscriptional Regulatory Element)
aliases: Woodchuck Hepatitis Virus Posttranscriptional Regulatory Element
description: The WPRE is a cis-acting RNA element derived from the woodchuck hepatitis virus (WHV) genome that enhances transgene expression 3–5-fold when placed in the 3′ UTR of an mRNA. It is widely used in lentiviral, AAV, and retroviral vectors to boost the level, nuclear export efficiency, and stability of transgene transcripts in mammalian cells.
mechanism: WPRE folds into a structured RNA secondary structure comprising three stem-loop domains (α, β, γ) that recruit nuclear export factors and RNA-binding proteins, promoting export of unspliced transcripts from the nucleus and increasing mRNA half-life and translational efficiency.
known_variants: WPRE3 — truncated and point-mutated version (~247 bp) with the WHV X ORF disrupted, designed for improved biosafety in clinical gene therapy
WPRE (X-mut) — full-length WPRE with missense mutations in the X gene ATG codons to eliminate cryptic promoter activity while preserving posttranscriptional function
known_misannotations: Confused with HPRE (Hepatitis B Virus Posttranscriptional Regulatory Element), which is functionally analogous but derived from HBV and has a distinct sequence
Mislabeled as a polyadenylation signal because it is positioned immediately upstream of the polyA site in vector maps
Annotated simply as 'PRE' without species attribution, conflating WHV and HBV elements
notes: The wild-type WPRE contains a partial X open reading frame from WHV with oncogenic potential upon insertional mutagenesis; clinical and in vivo gene therapy vectors should use WPRE3 or another ORF-disrupted variant. WPRE must be placed in the 3′ UTR of the transgene, upstream of the polyadenylation signal, to function. First characterized by Zufferey et al. (1999) J Virol 73:2886–2892.

description: The AAV2 Inverted Terminal Repeat (ITR) is a ~145 bp palindromic sequence derived from Adeno-Associated Virus serotype 2 that flanks both ends of the AAV genome and folds into a T-shaped hairpin structure. In recombinant AAV (rAAV) gene therapy vectors, the ITRs are the only cis-acting viral sequences retained — the entire transgene expression cassette is placed between them. They are essential for viral genome replication, packaging into capsids, and episomal persistence or integration in transduced cells.
mechanism: ITRs provide a free 3'-OH group via their hairpin structure to prime second-strand DNA synthesis, and contain Rep Binding Elements (RBE and RBE') recognized by AAV Rep proteins that mediate strand nicking at the terminal resolution site (trs) during rolling hairpin replication. In rAAV vectors lacking Rep, ITRs promote episomal concatemer formation and low-frequency site-specific integration at AAVS1.
known_variants: ΔITR (deleted ITR): trs-mutated ITR lacking the terminal resolution site, used in self-complementary AAV (scAAV) to force hairpin-primed second-strand synthesis
Synthetic/truncated ITR: shortened versions retaining minimal packaging signal, used to increase transgene cargo capacity
Flip and flop orientations: wild-type AAV packages both orientations; recombinant vectors may fix one orientation
known_misannotations: Annotated as plain 'ITR' without specifying AAV serotype — ITRs from AAV1–13 differ slightly and are not interchangeable
Confused with retroviral LTRs (Long Terminal Repeats), which are unrelated in sequence and mechanism
Length reported inconsistently (125 bp, 130 bp, or 145 bp) depending on whether the D-sequence region is included in the annotation
Orientation (5' vs 3') often omitted, though both ITRs in a standard rAAV transfer plasmid are functionally equivalent
notes: AAV2 ITRs are used almost universally in rAAV transfer plasmids regardless of the capsid serotype (AAV1, AAV5, AAV8, AAV9, etc.) — the capsid determines tropism while the ITRs determine replication and packaging. A standard rAAV transfer plasmid carries two AAV2 ITRs flanking a promoter-transgene-polyA cassette with a total capacity of ~4.7 kb including both ITRs (~0.3 kb combined). Self-complementary AAV (scAAV) uses one wild-type and one ΔITR, halves the packaging capacity to ~2.3 kb, but bypasses the rate-limiting second-strand synthesis step for faster and stronger expression.

description: The Inverted Terminal Repeat (ITR) of Adeno-Associated Virus serotype 9 (AAV9) is a 145-nucleotide palindromic sequence present at both termini of the AAV genome that folds into a characteristic T-shaped hairpin structure. ITRs are the only cis-acting sequences required for rAAV genome replication, packaging into capsid, and episomal persistence in transduced cells. In recombinant AAV9 gene therapy vectors, flanking a transgene expression cassette between two ITRs is sufficient to direct packaging, and AAV9 vectors are widely favored for broad CNS transduction following systemic or intracerebroventricular delivery.
mechanism: Rep proteins (Rep78/68) recognize the terminal resolution site (trs) embedded within the ITR hairpin and introduce a site-specific nick to initiate strand-displacement DNA replication. After transduction, the hairpin termini prime second-strand synthesis and mediate intramolecular circularization or head-to-tail concatemerization of the genome, enabling long-term episomal transgene persistence in post-mitotic cells without requiring integration.
known_variants: Self-complementary ITR (scITR / ΔD-ITR): deletion of the D-region and trs in one ITR forces packaging of a self-complementary dsDNA genome, enabling faster onset and higher-level transgene expression
Flip orientation: the B-C palindromes are oriented in one of two equivalent arrangements (flip vs. flop); both are produced by AAV replication and are functionally interchangeable
Flop orientation: the alternate flip/flop orientation, equally packaged and functional
AAV2-derived ITR used in AAV9 vectors: the ITR sequences of AAV9 are nearly identical to AAV2, and most published rAAV9 vectors carry AAV2-origin ITRs flanking the transgene
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'AAV2 ITR' even in bona fide AAV9 vectors, because ITR sequences are nearly identical across serotypes and the same ITR plasmids are routinely reused
Annotated as only 100–125 bp, capturing only the A-palindrome region and omitting the D-sequence, rather than the full functional 145 bp unit
Only one ITR annotated per construct; both 5' and 3' ITRs should be annotated separately as distinct features flanking the expression cassette
Confused with LTR (Long Terminal Repeat) used in retroviral/lentiviral vectors, which are structurally and functionally distinct
notes: AAV9 ITR sequences are essentially identical to AAV2 ITRs; serotype differences in AAV are determined by the capsid (VP1/2/3) genes, not the ITRs. Consequently, the same ITR-flanked transfer plasmid can be cross-packaged with any AAV capsid serotype. For scAAV constructs, one ITR is deliberately mutated (ΔD-ITR) to eliminate the trs, producing a self-complementary genome that bypasses the rate-limiting second-strand synthesis step. Biosafety classification of rAAV9 vectors is typically BSL-1 in the absence of Rep/Cap helper, but institutional guidelines should be consulted as AAV9 crosses the blood-brain barrier efficiently.

description: cHS4 is a 1.2 kb chromatin insulator element derived from the 5' DNase I hypersensitive site 4 of the chicken β-globin locus. It possesses two distinct activities: enhancer blocking (preventing nearby chromosomal enhancers from aberrantly activating the transgene) and barrier activity (shielding the transgene from epigenetic silencing caused by spreading heterochromatin). It is widely deployed in lentiviral and retroviral gene therapy vectors to stabilize long-term transgene expression after genomic integration.
mechanism: Enhancer-blocking activity requires CTCF binding to a specific sequence within the core element, which organizes chromatin loops that insulate promoters from distal enhancers. Barrier activity is conferred by USF1/USF2 binding sites that recruit histone acetyltransferases (PCAF, p300, SET7/9), maintaining an open chromatin domain and opposing heterochromatic spreading from flanking genomic regions.
known_variants: Full-length 1.2 kb cHS4 — contains both CTCF-binding (enhancer-blocking) and USF1/USF2-binding (barrier) sub-elements
250 bp minimal core — CTCF-binding element only; provides enhancer blocking but not full barrier activity
400 bp extended core — includes partial USF1/USF2 sites for partial barrier activity
Double cHS4 (2× copies) — tandem repeats flanking a transgene for enhanced barrier activity in integrating vectors
known_misannotations: Labeled simply 'HS4' without species prefix, creating ambiguity with human HS4 elements from unrelated loci
Minimal 250 bp core and full 1.2 kb element annotated identically despite substantially different functional profiles
Confused with adjacent chicken β-globin locus control region hypersensitive sites HS1, HS2, and HS3
Occasionally mislabeled as 'LCR insulator' or 'globin insulator' without specifying species or hypersensitive site number
notes: Most commonly used as flanking elements in self-inactivating (SIN) lentiviral and gammaretroviral vectors; a copy in the 3' LTR is duplicated to the 5' LTR during reverse transcription, producing symmetric flanking insulation. The full 1.2 kb element is preferred for gene therapy contexts where long-term silencing resistance is critical. Efficacy varies by integration site and cell type. Not typically required or active in transient episomal expression systems; its utility is specific to stably integrating vectors.

description: The HS4 core insulator is an approximately 250 bp element derived from the 5' DNase I-hypersensitive site 4 (HS4) of the chicken β-globin locus. It functions as a chromatin insulator by blocking inappropriate enhancer-promoter interactions and protecting transgenes from transcriptional silencing caused by repressive chromatin in the surrounding genomic context. It is widely incorporated into lentiviral and retroviral gene therapy vectors to achieve stable, position-independent transgene expression.
mechanism: Contains a 42 bp CTCF-binding footprint that recruits the CCCTC-binding factor (CTCF), establishing a chromatin domain boundary that blocks communication between enhancers and promoters on opposite sides of the element. The core fragment retains CTCF-mediated enhancer-blocking activity but lacks the USF1/USF2-dependent barrier activity present in the full 1.2 kb HS4 sequence.
known_variants: 2x HS4 core (~500 bp tandem duplication) — improved enhancer-blocking and partial barrier activity
4x HS4 core (~1 kb, four tandem copies) — strongest insulation used in clinical-grade SIN lentiviral vectors
Full-length HS4 (~1.2 kb) — includes both CTCF-binding (enhancer-blocking) and USF1/USF2-binding (barrier) elements
known_misannotations: Labeled 'HS4 insulator' without specifying core (~250 bp) vs. full-length (~1.2 kb), leading to length ambiguity
Tandem copies (2x or 4x) annotated as a single 'HS4 insulator' without indicating copy number
Confused with the broader chicken beta-globin locus control region (LCR) rather than the specific HS4 hypersensitive site
Sometimes conflated with other vertebrate CTCF-binding insulators unrelated to the globin locus
notes: The HS4 core alone provides enhancer-blocking but minimal barrier activity against heterochromatin spreading; for robust position-effect protection in stable integrations, tandem repeats or the full-length element are preferred. CTCF is conserved in vertebrates, limiting functional activity to vertebrate systems. A standard component of self-inactivating (SIN) lentiviral vectors in hematopoietic gene therapy. Activity can be attenuated by CpG methylation of the CTCF-binding site.

description: ARS1 is the first characterized autonomously replicating sequence from Saccharomyces cerevisiae, originally isolated from a chromosomal fragment containing the TRP1 gene on chromosome IV. It enables episomal maintenance of circular plasmids in yeast by serving as a replication origin recognized by the endogenous replication machinery. ARS1-containing plasmids (YRp vectors) replicate at 5–20 copies per cell but segregate randomly and are mitotically unstable without selection; addition of a centromere element (CEN) converts these to stable low-copy CEN/ARS vectors.
mechanism: ARS1 contains a core ARS consensus sequence (ACS), an ~11-bp AT-rich element (5'-TTTTATTTATA-3'), that recruits the Origin Recognition Complex (ORC) in a sequence-specific manner. ORC binding nucleates assembly of the pre-replication complex including MCM2–7 helicase, which is activated at S phase to initiate bidirectional DNA synthesis. Three auxiliary B sub-elements (B1, B2, B3) flanking the ACS enhance origin efficiency and facilitate chromatin remodeling by recruiting Nhp6 and Abf1.
known_variants: Minimal ARS1 (~150–300 bp, core ACS + B1 element only; reduced firing efficiency relative to full-length fragment)
ARSH4 (alternative designation for a HindIII-derived fragment from the same locus used in pRS-series vectors; functionally equivalent to ARS1)
B-element deletion mutants (B1, B2, or B3 point mutations or deletions; used in mechanistic studies of ORC-dependent origin activation)
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'ARSH4' in pRS-series vectors — the same functional ARS element cloned from a slightly different restriction fragment; the two names are often used interchangeably but can cause confusion

Fragment length varies widely across plasmid lineages (150 bp to >1 kb depending on cloning strategy); shorter fragments lacking B2/B3 show reduced replication efficiency but are sometimes annotated identically
Confused with ARS4/ARS401 or other chromosome IV origins that share the ACS motif but differ in auxiliary element composition and firing efficiency
CEN/ARS plasmids sometimes annotated with 'ARS1' alone, omitting the co-resident centromere (CEN4 or CEN6) that is equally essential for mitotic stability
notes: ARS1 is active only in Saccharomyces cerevisiae and closely related Saccharomyces sensu stricto species; it does not function as a replication origin in bacteria, mammalian cells, or most non-Saccharomyces yeasts. Plasmids with ARS1 alone are high-copy but lose at ~1–5% per generation without selection. Pairing ARS1 with a CEN element yields stable 1–2 copy centromeric plasmids; pairing with the 2-micron ORI and REP genes yields stable high-copy episomes (~40–60 copies). ARS1 is routinely used in yeast two-hybrid, protein expression, and gateway shuttle vectors where transient or low-copy episomal replication is desired.

description: CEN4 is the native centromere sequence from Saccharomyces cerevisiae chromosome IV, comprising three conserved DNA elements — CDEI (8 bp), an AT-rich CDEII (~78–86 bp), and CDEIII (25 bp) — spanning approximately 125 bp in total. It is incorporated into yeast centromeric plasmids (YCp vectors) to confer chromosome-like mitotic and meiotic segregation, stabilizing plasmid copy number at one to two copies per cell. CEN4 was among the first eukaryotic centromere sequences functionally characterized and cloned, and it remains a foundational element in yeast vector engineering.
mechanism: The CDEIII element is recognized by the CBF3 kinetochore complex (Ndc10p/Ctf13p/Skp1p/Cep3p), while CDEI recruits the Cbf1p homodimer; together these nucleate a single kinetochore that attaches to one spindle pole body microtubule, ensuring accurate chromosome segregation during mitosis and meiosis.
known_variants: CEN6 — centromere from chromosome VI, functionally interchangeable with CEN4; used in the widely-deployed pRS series vectors (pRS314/315/316)
Synthetic minimal centromere containing only the CDEI-CDEII-CDEIII core, stripped of flanking sequences
CDEII length variants — natural CDEII ranges from 78–86 bp across different S. cerevisiae strains; length affects but does not abolish function
known_misannotations: Annotated as 'CEN' without specifying chromosome number, creating ambiguity among the 16 S. cerevisiae centromeres
Confused with CEN6 in descriptions of pRS-series vectors, which actually carry CEN6/ARSH4 rather than CEN4
CDEII region alone annotated as the complete centromere, omitting the flanking CDEI and CDEIII elements required for full function
Incorrectly applied to Schizosaccharomyces pombe or Candida albicans vectors, which use entirely different regional centromere architectures
notes: S. cerevisiae point centromeres (~125 bp) are structurally and mechanistically distinct from the regional centromeres (kilobases to megabases) of metazoans and fission yeast. All 16 S. cerevisiae centromeres are functionally interchangeable in plasmid contexts. CEN4 must be paired with an autonomously replicating sequence (ARS) for extrachromosomal maintenance; CEN4/ARS1 is the classic pairing in YCp vectors. CEN-based low-copy plasmids (~1–2 copies/cell) are preferred over 2-micron high-copy vectors for dosage-sensitive genes and for constructs requiring defined stoichiometry.

description: URA3 encodes orotidine-5'-phosphate (OMP) decarboxylase, a key enzyme in the de novo pyrimidine biosynthesis pathway, originally characterized in Saccharomyces cerevisiae (locus YEL021W). It functions as a bidirectional selectable marker: ura3Δ auxotrophic yeast strains require uracil supplementation for growth and are rescued by a URA3 transgene (positive selection on –Ura dropout medium), while cells actively expressing URA3 are killed by 5-fluoroorotic acid (5-FOA), enabling counter-selection to recover cells that have lost or excised the marker.
mechanism: OMP decarboxylase catalyzes the decarboxylation of orotidine-5'-monophosphate to uridine monophosphate (UMP) in the pyrimidine de novo synthesis pathway, complementing uracil auxotrophy. In the presence of 5-FOA, the enzyme converts the drug to the toxic nucleotide analog 5-fluorouridine-5'-monophosphate (ultimately 5-fluorouracil), selectively killing URA3-expressing cells and permitting counter-selection.
known_variants: K. lactis URA3 (KILURA3) — used in Kluyveromyces and Pichia expression systems; ~75% identity to ScURA3
Candida albicans URA3 (CaURA3) — used for Candida genome engineering; native codon usage differs
URA3-d — minimal CYC1 promoter replacement reduces URA3 expression, enabling tighter selection stringency
ura3-52 — a classic S. cerevisiae loss-of-function allele (Ty insertion); not a functional variant but widely used as the recipient background
known_misannotations: Confused with URA5 (orotate phosphoribosyltransferase), which catalyzes the immediately preceding step in UMP synthesis; both are auxotrophic markers but encode different enzymes
KILURA3 (K. lactis) sometimes mislabeled as ScURA3 despite meaningful sequence divergence
Counter-selection property occasionally omitted from annotations, causing plasmids to be treated as positively selectable only
ura3-52 mutant allele sometimes annotated as if it were the functional URA3 marker in recipient strain descriptions
notes: URA3 is the premier bidirectional selectable marker in yeast genetics and synthetic biology, enabling both positive selection (–Ura growth) and counter-selection (5-FOA). It is essential for plasmid shuffling, Delitto Perfetto recombination, and CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing workflows in S. cerevisiae. Standard lab strains (BY4741, BY4742, W303, CEN.PK) carry ura3Δ or ura3-52 loss-of-function alleles. The complete cassette including the native 300–400 bp promoter and ~250 bp CYC1 or ADH1 terminator runs ~1.1–1.3 kb total. URA3 is not functional as a selectable marker in bacteria or mammalian cells under standard conditions.

description: TRP1 encodes phosphoribosylanthranilate isomerase (EC 5.3.1.24), the enzyme catalyzing the third step in tryptophan biosynthesis in Saccharomyces cerevisiae. It is widely used as an auxotrophic selectable marker: plasmids carrying TRP1 are maintained by selecting trp1 mutant host strains on tryptophan-dropout (SC-Trp) synthetic complete media. TRP1 is a core marker in yeast two-hybrid, split-ubiquitin, CEN/ARS shuttle, 2µ episomal, and integrative vector systems.
mechanism: Complements trp1 auxotrophic mutations in yeast by restoring the isomerization of N-(5-phosphoribosyl)anthranilate to 1-(o-carboxyphenylamino)-1-deoxyribulose-5-phosphate, re-enabling de novo tryptophan biosynthesis and growth on tryptophan-deficient media.
known_variants: TRP1-ARS1 fragment: classical minimal element combining the TRP1 coding sequence with the adjacent ARS1 replication origin from chromosome IV
Truncated TRP1 alleles (e.g., trp1Δ63 complementing constructs) used in integrative vectors where ARS1 activity is undesirable
known_misannotations: Confused with bacterial trpC, which encodes a bifunctional InGPS/PAI enzyme in bacteria and catalyzes overlapping but distinct reactions
Annotated generically as 'tryptophan marker' or 'TRP' without specifying the gene or organism of origin
Occasionally mislabeled as encoding anthranilate synthase (TRP2/TRP3 function) rather than phosphoribosylanthranilate isomerase
notes: TRP1 is a Saccharomyces cerevisiae-specific auxotrophic marker and is not functional in bacterial, mammalian, insect, or plant cells. Commonly used with host strain alleles trp1-1, trp1-901, or trp1Δ63. The coding sequence is ~804 bp; functional cassettes in expression vectors include the endogenous promoter and terminator, totaling ~1.4–1.8 kb. The TRP1-ARS1 locus on chromosome IV was one of the first characterized yeast autonomously replicating sequences and is historically significant in the development of yeast episomal vectors. In the canonical yeast two-hybrid system (Fields & Song 1989), TRP1 and LEU2 are the standard bait- and prey-vector marker pair.

description: HIS3 encodes imidazoleglycerol-phosphate dehydratase (IGPD), the enzyme catalyzing the sixth step in histidine biosynthesis in Saccharomyces cerevisiae. It is one of the most widely used auxotrophic selectable markers in yeast genetics: his3Δ strains are histidine auxotrophs that cannot grow on histidine-deficient medium unless complemented by a functional HIS3 gene on a plasmid or integrated at a chromosomal locus. It is also used as a reporter in yeast two-hybrid systems, where protein–protein interaction drives reconstitution of GAL4 and activates an integrated HIS3 reporter.
mechanism: IGPD catalyzes the dehydration of imidazoleglycerol phosphate to imidazoleacetol phosphate in the de novo histidine biosynthetic pathway. Yeast strains carrying his3 loss-of-function alleles (his3Δ1, his3Δ200, his3-11,15) are auxotrophic for histidine; complementation with a plasmid-borne HIS3 gene restores prototrophy and enables growth on synthetic complete medium lacking histidine (SC−His).
known_variants: HIS3MX6 — Kluyveromyces lactis HIS3 (klHIS3) flanked by loxP sites, optimized for PCR-based one-step gene deletion (Longtine et al. 1998); commonly mistaken for S. cerevisiae HIS3
HIS3Δ200 — internal deletion allele frequently present as the chromosomal recipient locus in standard lab strains (e.g., YPH499/501)
his3-11,15 — double point-mutation allele characteristic of the W303 strain background; differs from his3Δ200 and incompatible with some complementation strategies
Codon-optimized HIS3 — versions re-coded for use in Candida albicans or Candida glabrata, which use alternative codon tables
known_misannotations: HIS3MX6 cassette (contains klHIS3 from K. lactis) is routinely annotated as simply 'HIS3', obscuring the species origin and loxP recombination sites
Confusion with bacterial hisB (E. coli) — hisB encodes a bifunctional IGPD/HPP phosphatase and is a completely distinct gene not interchangeable with ScHIS3
Labeling as 'HIS' without specifying gene number — HIS1 through HIS7 encode distinct enzymes in the same pathway and are not functionally equivalent markers
Use as a two-hybrid reporter is sometimes annotated as a 'selection marker' feature on vectors even when it is an integrated genomic reporter, not a plasmid-borne cassette
notes: HIS3 is exclusively functional in yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae and related species); it does not complement histidine auxotrophy in E. coli or provide selection in mammalian systems. Selection stringency can be tuned by adding 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole (3-AT), a competitive inhibitor of IGPD, to SC−His plates; this is standard practice in two-hybrid screens to suppress leaky HIS3 basal expression and reduce false positives. The his3Δ1 and his3Δ200 deletion alleles are preferred over point-mutation alleles because they eliminate reversion events. When used as a counter-selectable marker in some specialized contexts, HIS3 overexpression combined with aminotriazole can create toxic conditions.

description: LEU2 encodes β-isopropylmalate dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.85) from Saccharomyces cerevisiae, an enzyme required for the third step of leucine biosynthesis. It is one of the most widely used auxotrophic selectable markers in yeast molecular biology, enabling growth of leu2 mutant host strains on leucine-deficient (SD-Leu) minimal media when carried on a plasmid. Its utility spans integrative, centromeric (CEN/ARS), and episomal (2μ) vector systems.
mechanism: Leu2p catalyzes the NAD⁺-dependent oxidative decarboxylation of (2R,3S)-3-isopropylmalate to 2-oxo-4-methylpentanoate, a committed intermediate in the leucine biosynthetic pathway. Yeast strains bearing a leu2 null mutation are leucine auxotrophs; complementation by plasmid-borne LEU2 restores prototrophy and allows growth without exogenous leucine.
known_variants: LEU2d: promoter-truncated allele that requires high plasmid copy number (~15–30 copies/cell) for complementation; used to select for high-copy 2μ plasmids and maintain episomal stability
Codon-optimized variants adapted for Kluyveromyces lactis, Pichia pastoris, and other non-Saccharomyces yeasts
Integrative cassette forms flanked by leu2 homology arms for chromosomal integration
known_misannotations: Confused with bacterial leuB (E. coli 3-isopropylmalate dehydrogenase) — same enzymatic step but phylogenetically distinct gene and protein
LEU2d sometimes annotated simply as 'LEU2' in plasmid maps, obscuring the promoter truncation that affects copy-number selection behavior
Occasionally mislabeled 'LEU2-3,112' in older literature, which refers to a specific leu2 host strain allele (leu2-3, leu2-112 double mutant), not a marker variant
notes: LEU2 functions exclusively in yeast and related fungi; it has no utility as a selectable marker in bacterial or mammalian systems. The leu2-3,112 double mutant background (used in BY-series lab strains) is fully complemented by any intact LEU2 allele. LEU2d is specifically designed for 2μ plasmid systems where tight positive selection for high copy number is required; substituting full-length LEU2 in these contexts can result in lower and more variable plasmid copy number.

description: The GAL1 promoter is a strong, tightly regulated inducible promoter from Saccharomyces cerevisiae that controls expression of the GAL1 gene encoding galactokinase. It is induced 100- to 1000-fold by galactose and strongly repressed by glucose, making it one of the most widely used tools for conditional high-level protein expression in yeast. Its exceptional dynamic range makes it particularly valuable for expressing proteins that are toxic or growth-inhibitory when constitutively produced.
mechanism: In the presence of galactose, the transcriptional activator Gal4p binds upstream activation sequences (UAS_GAL) and recruits the Mediator complex to drive strong RNA Pol II transcription. In glucose, the Mig1p repressor directly silences GAL1 transcription and Gal80p sequesters Gal4p, achieving near-complete shutoff.
known_variants: Truncated GAL1 promoter (~312 bp minimal UAS_GAL fragment, reduced but still inducible activity)
Bidirectional GAL1/GAL10 configuration — divergent promoters sharing UAS_GAL, used to co-express two genes simultaneously
Synthetic GAL1 promoter with optimized TATA box for enhanced basal or induced expression
GALp variants with mutated Mig1p binding sites for reduced glucose repression
known_misannotations: Confusion with GAL10 promoter — both share the same UAS_GAL intergenic region in divergent orientation; vectors may label either as 'GAL1 promoter'
Annotation may inadvertently include the GAL1 5' UTR or start of the GAL1 ORF
Sometimes annotated generically as 'GAL promoter' or 'PGAL' without distinguishing GAL1 from GAL10 or GAL7
The shared ~668 bp GAL1/GAL10 intergenic region is occasionally annotated as belonging entirely to one gene
notes: Functional only in Saccharomyces cerevisiae and closely related budding yeasts; not active in other organisms. Standard induction protocol is pre-growth in raffinose (derepressed but not induced) followed by galactose addition to avoid glucose repression lag. The GAL1 and GAL10 genes are divergently transcribed from a shared ~668 bp intergenic region; many dual-expression vectors (e.g., pESC series) exploit this architecture. Full induction requires galactose as sole carbon source; mixed glucose/galactose media significantly attenuates expression.

description: The GAL10 promoter is a galactose-inducible, glucose-repressible promoter from Saccharomyces cerevisiae that naturally drives expression of GAL10 (UDP-glucose 4-epimerase). It is widely used in yeast expression systems to achieve tightly regulated, high-level recombinant protein production, and is particularly valued in dual-expression vectors where it drives a second gene divergently opposite the GAL1 promoter.
mechanism: Activation depends on binding of the Gal4p transcription factor to upstream activating sequences (UASgal) within the shared GAL1-GAL10 intergenic region; in the absence of galactose, the Gal80p co-repressor sequesters the Gal4p activation domain, and galactose-bound Gal3p relieves this repression by sequestering Gal80p, allowing Gal4p to recruit the transcriptional machinery.
known_variants: Full bidirectional GAL1-GAL10 intergenic region (~668 bp) used to drive divergent co-expression of two genes
Truncated GAL10 promoter fragments retaining core UASgal and TATA box
Synthetic UASgal-TATA hybrid promoters with modified or multimerized Gal4p binding sites
known_misannotations: Frequently confused with GAL1 promoter because both share the same bidirectional intergenic regulatory region; clones annotated as one often contain both
The full GAL1-GAL10 intergenic region is sometimes annotated solely as 'GAL1 promoter', omitting the embedded GAL10 promoter orientation
Occasionally mislabeled 'GAL4 promoter' — GAL4 encodes the transcriptional activator that binds this promoter, not the promoter itself
Some depositors annotate only the UASgal element as the promoter, excluding the TATA box and core promoter sequences required for function
notes: GAL10 and GAL1 are divergently transcribed from a shared ~668 bp intergenic region on S. cerevisiae chromosome II containing four Gal4p-binding UASgal sites. GAL10 expression is typically 2–5-fold lower than GAL1 under the same induction conditions. Induction requires galactose as the sole or primary carbon source; glucose causes rapid catabolite repression even in the presence of galactose. The pESC vector series exploits this bidirectionality to co-express two genes from a single compact regulatory region. Functional in Saccharomyces cerevisiae and closely related species; not active in non-Saccharomyces yeasts without additional engineering.

description: The ADH1 promoter is derived from the Saccharomyces cerevisiae alcohol dehydrogenase 1 (ADH1/YOL086C) gene and drives strong constitutive transcription in budding yeast. It is among the most widely used promoters in yeast expression systems for high-level, constitutive production of heterologous proteins. Unlike inducible yeast promoters (e.g., GAL1, AOX1), it requires no inducer addition, making it operationally simple.
mechanism: Contains upstream activating sequences (UAS elements) and a canonical TATA box that recruit the general transcription machinery and RNA polymerase II constitutively. Expression is highest on fermentable carbon sources (glucose) and decreases approximately 3–5-fold on non-fermentable carbon sources such as glycerol or ethanol, reflecting the metabolic role of Adh1p in ethanol production during fermentation.
known_variants: Truncated ~600 bp core ADH1 promoter fragment (retains ~50–70% of full-length activity)
ADH1 promoter from Kluyveromyces lactis (KlADH1p, used in K. lactis expression systems)
Synthetic/mutant UAS variants with altered strength or carbon-source response
known_misannotations: Confused with ADH2 promoter (ScADH2p is glucose-repressible; ADH1p is constitutive)
Annotated as 'ADC1 promoter' — ADC1 is a historical synonym for the ADH1 gene in S. cerevisiae
Mislabeled as 'AOX1 promoter' in Pichia pastoris (Komagataella phaffii) vectors, which are entirely different organisms and regulatory systems
Variable truncation endpoints across labs lead to inconsistent 'ADH1 promoter' lengths in deposits
notes: The ADH1 promoter is organism-specific to Saccharomyces cerevisiae and closely related Saccharomyces species. It does not function in bacteria, mammalian cells, or Pichia pastoris without modification. The Mumberg et al. 1995 (Gene) centromeric/episomal vector series (p416ADH, p426ADH) are the canonical reference vectors. When expression strength is critical, the TEF1 or PGK1 promoters are often compared as alternatives with slightly different carbon-source dependency profiles.

description: The 3' transcription terminator derived from the Saccharomyces cerevisiae ADH1 (Alcohol Dehydrogenase 1) gene, encompassing the polyadenylation signal and downstream regulatory elements. It provides efficient transcription termination, mRNA 3' end processing, and polyadenylation in yeast, making it one of the most widely deployed terminators in yeast synthetic biology, metabolic engineering, and heterologous protein expression.
mechanism: Functions as a eukaryotic RNA polymerase II terminator via poly(A) signal recognition: conserved sequence elements downstream of the stop codon recruit cleavage and polyadenylation factors that cleave the nascent transcript and add a poly(A) tail, triggering transcription termination and stabilizing the mRNA.
known_variants: Short ADH1t (~150 bp): minimal 3'UTR fragment retaining only the core polyadenylation signal
Extended ADH1t (~300+ bp): includes additional downstream genomic sequence for enhanced termination efficiency
Codon-context variants: identical sequence used downstream of codon-optimized transgenes without modification
known_misannotations: Confused with CYC1 terminator — both are common S. cerevisiae terminators and are functionally interchangeable, leading to mislabeling in older vectors
Labeled generically as 'ADH terminator' without specifying ADH1 vs. ADH2, which are distinct loci
Annotated as 'ADH1 3'UTR' rather than 'ADH1 terminator' — these are the same functional element but create inconsistency in databases
Occasionally mislabeled as belonging to ADH2 (the glucose-repressible isoform) due to high sequence similarity
notes: The ADH1 terminator is native to Saccharomyces cerevisiae and functions reliably in closely related yeasts (e.g., Kluyveromyces lactis, Yarrowia lipolytica in some contexts). It is routinely paired with heterologous promoters (GAL1, TEF1, CUP1, etc.) in yeast expression cassettes. The CYC1 terminator is an equally common alternative with comparable efficiency. The ADH1 gene itself resides on S. cerevisiae chromosome IV; the terminator is typically defined as the ~215–260 bp region immediately downstream of the ADH1 stop codon.

description: The CYC1 terminator is derived from the 3' end of the Saccharomyces cerevisiae CYC1 gene encoding iso-1-cytochrome c and is the most widely used transcription terminator in yeast expression vectors. It provides efficient mRNA 3' end cleavage, polyadenylation, and transcription termination, preventing read-through into downstream sequences. It is typically paired with a yeast promoter (e.g., GAL1, TEF1, ADH1) to form a complete expression cassette.
mechanism: Yeast transcription termination proceeds via 3' end cleavage and polyadenylation rather than intrinsic hairpin-based termination used in bacteria; the CYC1 terminator contains AU-rich efficiency elements and a positioning element recognized by the CPF/CFIA cleavage-polyadenylation complex to direct poly(A) addition approximately 247 bp downstream of the stop codon.
known_variants: Minimal CYC1 terminator (~247 bp core efficiency element only)
Extended CYC1 terminator including additional downstream genomic sequence (~320–400 bp)
Synthetic codon-context-adjusted version used in some commercial vectors
known_misannotations: Confused with the CYC1 minimal promoter (a distinct regulatory element from the same locus used to drive basal or activator-dependent transcription)
Labeled simply as 'CYC1' without specifying promoter vs. terminator, causing ambiguity in multi-element maps
Occasionally annotated as 'CYC1 3'UTR' with boundaries that include or exclude the polyadenylation site, leading to length discrepancies across plasmid maps
notes: Functional exclusively in Saccharomyces cerevisiae and closely related budding yeasts (Kluyveromyces, Lachancea); not active in fission yeast (S. pombe), plants, or mammalian cells. The CYC1 terminator is often used in tandem with the CYC1 minimal promoter in bidirectional expression constructs (e.g., pESC series uses GAL1/GAL10 promoters flanked by CYC1 terminators on both sides). Length reported in literature varies (247–320 bp) depending on whether the cloning boundary is taken at the stop codon or further upstream.

description: The AOX1 promoter drives expression of alcohol oxidase I in Pichia pastoris (Komagataella phaffii) and is one of the strongest and most tightly regulated promoters available for recombinant protein production. It is strongly repressed by glucose and glycerol, then induced up to 1,000-fold by methanol, enabling heterologous protein accumulation to 30% or more of total cellular protein. It is the defining element of commercial Pichia expression systems including the pPIC and pPICZ vector series.
mechanism: The AOX1 promoter contains multiple upstream activation sequences (UASs) bound by the transcriptional activator Mxr1/Prm1 during methanol catabolism; carbon catabolite repression mediated by Mig1 and Nrg1 silences the promoter in the presence of glucose or glycerol. Full induction requires both derepression (removal of glucose/glycerol) and active methanol signaling.
known_variants: 940 bp core fragment (most common; 940 bp immediately upstream of AOX1 ATG)
Synthetic truncated variants (200–400 bp) retaining methanol inducibility with reduced regulatory complexity
Hybrid AOX1/GAP promoter fusions for constitutive-plus-inducible expression
Derepressed mutant variants with reduced glucose/glycerol repression for simplified induction protocols
Synthetic versions with shuffled UAS elements for altered methanol response kinetics
known_misannotations: Confused with AOX2 promoter, which is ~20-fold weaker under methanol induction
Labeled generically as 'methanol promoter' without specifying AOX1 vs AOX2
Organism listed as Saccharomyces cerevisiae instead of Pichia pastoris / Komagataella phaffii
Promoter boundaries vary across annotations: some include the 5' UTR or first few codons of AOX1 CDS
Some vectors label this as 'MeOH promoter' or 'Pichia promoter' losing the AOX1 identity
notes: Functional exclusively in Pichia pastoris (Komagataella phaffii) and closely related methylotrophic yeasts (Hansenula polymorpha, Candida boidinii); does not function in S. cerevisiae. Standard fermentation uses a two-stage protocol: glycerol batch phase for biomass accumulation, then methanol feed for induction. The AOX1 locus is also commonly used as a chromosomal integration site, replacing endogenous AOX1 and creating Mut- or MutS phenotypes that affect methanol utilization rate.

description: The polyhedrin (polh) promoter is a very late baculovirus promoter derived from Autographa californica multiple nucleopolyhedrovirus (AcMNPV) that drives exceptionally high-level transcription during the very late phase of viral infection, typically exceeding 24 hours post-infection. In nature it drives production of the occlusion body matrix protein to levels exceeding 50% of total cell protein; in recombinant baculovirus expression vectors (BEVS), the polyhedrin coding sequence is replaced with a gene of interest, enabling large-scale recombinant protein production in insect cells.
mechanism: The promoter contains a conserved TAAG initiator motif and an upstream TATA box that recruit baculovirus-encoded late expression factors (LEFs) and a virus-specific RNA polymerase during the very late phase of infection. Promoter activity is entirely dependent on baculovirus trans-acting factors and is therefore non-functional in the absence of active viral infection.
known_variants: Minimal polyhedrin promoter (~50 bp core TAAG element only, reduced activity)
Modified polh promoter with synthetic optimized 5' UTR for enhanced translation initiation
Dual polh/p10 tandem promoter cassette used in some dual-expression transfer vectors
known_misannotations: Confused with the p10 promoter, a distinct AcMNPV very late promoter of comparable strength driving a different viral gene
Labeled generically as 'baculovirus promoter' without specifying polyhedrin vs. p10 identity
Incorrectly annotated as a constitutive insect cell promoter rather than a virus-dependent very late promoter
Truncated inserts sometimes annotated as full-length promoter when upstream regulatory elements are absent
notes: The polyhedrin promoter is strictly dependent on baculovirus infection and is non-functional in uninfected insect cells or any other host system. Compatible insect cell lines include Sf9 and Sf21 (Spodoptera frugiperda) and High Five (Trichoplusia ni). Because the polyhedrin gene is dispensable for budded virus production, it is the preferred locus for transgene insertion in BEVS. Very late expression peaks at 48–72 h post-infection but the host cell is compromised by this stage, which can affect post-translational processing of complex mammalian proteins.

description: The p10 promoter is a very late-phase promoter from Autographa californica multiple nucleopolyhedrovirus (AcMNPV) that drives high-level transcription during the final stage of baculovirus infection in insect cells. It is widely used in baculovirus expression vector systems (BEVS) alongside the polyhedrin promoter, especially in dual-promoter vectors, to achieve simultaneous high-level expression of two recombinant proteins. The p10 gene product itself forms cytoskeletal fibrous structures but is non-essential for virus replication, making the locus ideal for foreign gene insertion.
mechanism: Activated by the viral very late factor 1 (VLF-1) and other baculovirus-encoded transcription factors that recognize a conserved TAAG motif and an upstream burst element; transcription initiates at the TAAG tetranucleotide and is amplified by viral DNA replication in the very late phase (18–72 h post-infection).
known_variants: Minimal p10 promoter (~150 bp core containing TAAG and proximal burst element only)
Extended p10 promoter (~600 bp including distal upstream enhancer elements)
known_misannotations: Confused with polyhedrin (polh) promoter — both are AcMNPV very late promoters but drive different genes and have distinct sequences
Sometimes labeled generically as 'baculovirus promoter' without specifying p10 vs. polh
Truncated versions lacking the burst element may be annotated as full-length p10 promoter
notes: Active only in insect cells infected with baculovirus (e.g., Sf9, Sf21, High Five / Tni cells); has no activity in uninfected cells or non-insect systems. Very late kinetics mean peak expression occurs 48–72 h post-infection, later than immediate-early or early promoters. In dual-promoter vectors (e.g., pFastBac Dual), p10 and polh are oriented in opposite directions flanking the multiple cloning sites. Expression levels from p10 are generally somewhat lower than from polh but still very high relative to mammalian systems.

description: A homologous recombination arm derived from Autographa californica multiple nucleopolyhedrovirus (AcMNPV) ORF1629, located immediately downstream of the polyhedrin (polh) gene locus. It is incorporated into baculovirus transfer vectors as the 3′ homology arm, enabling site-specific integration of transgenes into the baculovirus genome by homologous recombination in co-transfected insect cells. This element is a core component of many first- and second-generation baculovirus expression vector systems (BEVS).
mechanism: Provides a region of sequence identity flanking the polyhedrin insertion site in baculovirus transfer vectors; when the transfer plasmid is co-transfected with linearized baculovirus DNA (or intact viral DNA) into insect cells, intracellular homologous recombination between the ORF1629 arm and the cognate viral sequence displaces the polyhedrin or marker gene and inserts the expression cassette at the polh locus.
known_variants: Truncated ORF1629 arm used in linearized BaculoGold/BacVector systems to enforce recombination
Extended arm with additional multiple cloning site flanking sequences (pVL1393 vs pVL1392)
known_misannotations: Annotated generically as 'baculovirus genomic sequence' without identifying the ORF1629 origin
Confused with the polyhedrin promoter (a distinct upstream element) when the full polh locus region is annotated as a single block
Labeled as ORF1629 coding sequence (structural protein) rather than the non-coding flanking recombination arm
notes: In AcMNPV, ORF1629 itself encodes a large structural protein; in BEVS vector nomenclature, the 'ORF1629 recombination region' refers specifically to the downstream flanking genomic sequence used as a homology arm, not the ORF coding region. Functional only in insect cell lines (Sf9, Sf21, High Five/Hi5). Not active as a recombination site in E. coli, yeast, or mammalian cells. Linearized viral DNA systems (BaculoGold, BacVector 3000) use a deletion that spans into this region to enforce recombinant selection.

description: The multiple cloning site (MCS) is a short synthetic DNA segment containing a series of unique restriction enzyme recognition sequences clustered in a compact region of a cloning vector. It provides multiple entry points for inserting foreign DNA fragments, allowing researchers to choose restriction enzymes compatible with both vector and insert ends. In many common vectors such as the pUC series, the MCS is embedded within the lacZα coding sequence, enabling blue-white colony screening to identify successful clones.
mechanism: The MCS presents a set of unique restriction endonuclease recognition sites, each of which can be cut individually to generate compatible cohesive or blunt ends for ligation with an insert fragment. Because each site is designed to be unique within the vector, digestion at any single site yields a single linearized molecule that can be directionally or non-directionally joined to a compatibly digested insert via DNA ligase.
known_variants: pUC18 MCS (same restriction sites as pUC19 but in inverted orientation relative to the lacZα reading frame)
pBluescript MCS (extended pUC MCS flanked by T3 and T7 promoter primer sites)
Dual MCS vectors (e.g., pACYC-Duet, pRSF-Duet) with two independent MCS regions each driven by a separate promoter
Gateway-converted vectors in which MCS replaced by attR recombination cassette
LIC (ligation-independent cloning) MCS with 5' T4-exonuclease-generated single-stranded overhangs instead of restriction sites
known_misannotations: Frequently recorded as generic 'misc_feature' with a /note qualifier rather than an explicit MCS feature, obscuring recognition in automated pipelines
Individual restriction sites within the MCS annotated as separate 'rep_origin' or 'misc_feature' entries rather than as part of a unified MCS span
pUC18 and pUC19 MCS orientation confusion — the two are inverted relative to each other, causing wrong-strand annotation when one is used as a reference for the other
MCS boundaries called inconsistently: some annotations include flanking primer sites (M13 fwd/rev, T7, SP6), others exclude them
notes: The MCS is a synthetic vector element with no intrinsic biological activity; its utility depends entirely on which restriction enzymes the experimenter selects. GC content and length vary considerably between vectors depending on the restriction sites included. Standard sequencing primers binding the MCS-flanking regions (M13/pUC forward, M13/pUC reverse, T7, SP6, T3) are widely used to sequence inserts. Many modern cloning strategies (Gibson Assembly, Golden Gate, Gateway, TOPO) bypass or replace traditional MCS-based cloning.

description: The rop gene encodes the Rop (Repressor of Primer) protein, a 63-amino-acid RNA-binding protein that controls copy number of ColE1-type plasmids by facilitating RNAI–RNAII antisense pairing. In plasmid engineering, rop is frequently deleted to generate high-copy-number vectors — its removal is the primary molecular difference between pBR322 (medium copy, ~20 copies/cell) and the pUC series (high copy, ~500 copies/cell).
mechanism: Rop stabilizes the transient kissing-loop interaction between the antisense regulatory RNA (RNAI) and the replication primer precursor (RNAII), promoting conversion to a stable extended duplex that blocks RNAII processing by RNase H and thereby suppresses initiation of plasmid replication.
known_misannotations: Labeled 'ROM' (RNA One Modulator) in many annotations — ROM and Rop are the same protein encoded by the same gene
Often unannotated in ColE1-derived vectors despite the ORF sequence being present in the backbone
Occasionally subsumed into the ori region annotation rather than marked as a distinct ORF
notes: rop is present in the pBR322/ColE1 lineage and absent from most modern cloning vectors (pUC, pTZ, pGEM) where deliberate deletion increases copy number 5–10 fold. Its presence is a reliable indicator of a medium-copy ColE1-type origin. The Rop homodimer forms an antiparallel four-helix bundle and is one of the smallest known regulatory proteins (~7 kDa monomer). Rop acts only in cis with compatible RNAI/RNAII sequences and is not a general plasmid copy-number regulator.

description: The bom (basis of mobility) is a cis-acting DNA sequence in ColE1-type plasmids that serves as the site required for plasmid mobilization by conjugative helper plasmids. It contains a specific nick site (nic) recognized by Mob relaxase proteins encoded in trans by conjugative plasmids such as RP4. Plasmid vectors retaining bom can be mobilized between bacteria when a conjugative plasmid is present, which has both biosafety and experimental implications.
mechanism: The Mob relaxase binds the bom site and introduces a site-specific single-strand nick at the nic sequence, forming a covalent protein-DNA relaxosome complex. This complex is recruited to the conjugative pore apparatus and the nicked strand is transferred in a 5′-to-3′ fashion into the recipient cell, where it is re-circularized.
known_variants: Deleted in pUC series plasmids (pUC18, pUC19) to prevent mobilization — a deliberate biosafety modification
Minimal bom (~200 bp, nic site plus flanking ~50 bp each side) sufficient for relaxosome formation in some contexts
known_misannotations: Labeled as 'oriT' — functionally equivalent in ColE1 plasmids but oriT is the broader IUPAC/SO term; 'bom' is the ColE1-specific historical name
Confused with 'mob' — mob refers to the trans-acting mobilization protein genes, while bom is the cis-acting DNA site they act upon
Omitted from annotations entirely in pBR322-derived vectors when annotators focus only on resistance markers and ori
notes: The bom site is present in pBR322 and many of its derivatives but was intentionally deleted in the pUC plasmid series to create non-mobilizable, higher-biosafety-containment vectors. Its presence in a vector does not confer any phenotype unless a conjugative plasmid (supplying Mob and Tra functions in trans) is also present in the same cell. In regulatory contexts (e.g., NIH biosafety guidelines), vectors retaining bom may require additional containment considerations.

description: Par (partition) systems are DNA segregation machinery encoded by low-copy plasmids that ensure accurate distribution of plasmid copies to daughter cells during cell division, preventing plasmid loss through random assortment. They function analogously to eukaryotic centromere-kinetochore systems and are essential for stable maintenance of low-copy vectors such as pSC101 (5 copies/cell) and P1 prophage derivatives. In plasmid engineering, par systems are incorporated into vectors requiring stable low-copy maintenance, including BAC vectors used for genomic library construction.
mechanism: Type I systems (parABS, most common) use a Walker-type ATPase (ParA) that oscillates along the nucleoid as a DNA-bound gradient and interacts with ParB protein bound to centromere-like parS sequences, pulling plasmid copies toward opposite cell poles before division. Type II systems (parMRC, e.g., R1 plasmid) use an actin-like ATPase (ParM) that polymerizes bidirectionally between two ParR-bound plasmid complexes, pushing them to opposite poles in a spindle-like mechanism.
reference_plasmids: P1 prophage (parABS, used in BAC vectors)
pBeloBAC11 (BAC vector with P1 par elements)
known_variants: Type I parABS (Walker-type ATPase ParA + DNA-binding ParB + centromere-like parS) — predominant in gram-negative plasmids
Type II parMRC (actin-like ParM ATPase + ParR DNA-binding + parC centromere site) — R1, pB171
Type III tubZRC (tubulin-like TubZ ATPase) — Bacillus plasmids
sop system (F plasmid SopA/SopB/sopC) — functionally Type I, different gene names
Minimal parS (synthetic centromere site only, without parAB genes, paired with trans-supplied proteins)
known_misannotations: Confusion with pAD1 'par' locus, which is a toxin-antitoxin addiction system, not a partition system
'par' label applied inconsistently — may annotate only the cis-acting parS centromere site (~200–400 bp), only the parAB coding region (~1.5–3 kb), or the complete operon
F plasmid sop genes (sopA, sopB, sopC) not recognized as a par system; sometimes labeled 'plasmid stability' without partition classification
Confusion with hok/sok and other post-segregational killing systems, which stabilize plasmids by a distinct mechanism (toxin-antitoxin, not active partition)
notes: Par systems are relevant only for low-copy plasmids; high-copy ColE1-derived vectors (pUC, pBluescript, pET) do not require partition because random assortment at high copy number rarely produces plasmid-free daughters. The expected_length_bp range is wide because 'par' annotations span from the minimal centromere site alone (~200–400 bp for parS/sopC) to the complete partition operon including trans-acting protein genes (~2–5 kb). When reviewing GenBank annotations, always check which components are included. The pSC101 par system is unusual in that it also requires the plasmid-encoded IHF-binding site and involves host IHF protein for full activity.

description: The reverse tetracycline transactivator (rtTA) is the central regulatory protein of the Tet-On inducible expression system, consisting of a mutated Tet repressor (TetR) DNA-binding domain fused to the VP16 transactivation domain of herpes simplex virus. Unlike the original tTA (Tet-Off), rtTA binds tetracycline response elements (TRE) only when doxycycline is present, enabling doxycycline-dependent activation of downstream gene expression. It is widely used for inducible transgene expression in mammalian cells, enabling temporal and dose-dependent control of genes of interest without permanently altering cell state.
mechanism: Mutations in the TetR DNA-binding domain (canonically E71K, D95N, L101S, G102D) invert its allosteric response so that doxycycline binding drives a conformational change that permits, rather than prevents, TRE binding; once bound, the fused VP16 activation domain recruits the general transcription machinery to drive expression from any downstream TRE-controlled promoter.
known_variants: rtTA original (Gossen 1995): 4 TetR mutations, functional but higher background leakiness
rtTA2S-M2 (Urlinger 2000): additional TetR mutations improving doxycycline sensitivity and reducing basal activity
rtTA3: third-generation variant with minimal leaky expression and improved fold-induction ratio
rtTA-Advanced (Takara/Clontech): commercially optimized variant with lower background
humanized rtTA (hrtTA): codon-optimized for mammalian expression to increase protein output
rtTA-VP64 and rtTA-p65 fusions: VP16 replaced with stronger synthetic (VP64) or p65 NF-κB activation domains for higher induction levels
Tet3G / Tet-On 3G: third-generation rtTA with further mutations for improved performance at lower doxycycline concentrations
known_misannotations: Confused with tTA (Tet-Off transactivator): tTA is inhibited by doxycycline, rtTA is activated — opposite regulatory logic
Annotated as 'TetR' alone, omitting the VP16 transactivation domain that is essential for the activator function
TRE promoter element annotated as 'rtTA': the TRE is the DNA target site for rtTA, not the transactivator protein itself
Confused with rTetR (reverse tet repressor): rTetR also binds DNA in the presence of doxycycline but lacks the VP16 domain and acts as a repressor, not an activator
Generation not specified: 'rtTA' without version label is ambiguous given the substantially different performance characteristics of rtTA, rtTA2S-M2, and rtTA3
notes: rtTA is nearly always used in a two-component system: the rtTA expression cassette (driven by a constitutive promoter such as CMV or EF1α) on one plasmid or genomic locus, and the TRE-driven gene of interest on another. Leaky (doxycycline-independent) expression is the most common practical problem; rtTA2S-M2 and rtTA3 substantially reduce this. Doxycycline is strongly preferred over tetracycline due to its superior cell and tissue permeability and longer half-life. At high doxycycline concentrations, some cytotoxicity has been reported in sensitive cell lines, which should be titrated. rtTA is also functional in Drosophila, zebrafish, and mouse in vivo models in addition to cell culture.

description: The tetracycline transactivator (tTA) is a chimeric transcriptional activator composed of the DNA-binding domain of the E. coli Tn10 tetracycline repressor (TetR) fused to the VP16 transcriptional activation domain from Herpes Simplex Virus. It is the core component of the Tet-Off inducible gene expression system, where it drives strong transcription from tetracycline response element (TRE) promoters in the absence of tetracycline or doxycycline. Addition of doxycycline (or tetracycline) silences downstream transgene expression, making tTA useful for conditional, titratable gene regulation in mammalian cells and transgenic organisms.
mechanism: In the absence of tetracycline or doxycycline, tTA binds tetO operator sequences within the TRE promoter via its TetR domain and activates RNA polymerase II recruitment through its VP16 activation domain. Tetracycline or doxycycline binding to the TetR moiety induces a conformational change that abolishes tetO DNA binding, preventing transactivation and silencing the downstream gene.
known_variants: tTA2 — second-generation tTA with improved transcriptional activity and reduced leakiness
tTA-S2 — mutations reducing basal (leaky) transcription in the absence of induction
rtTA (reverse tTA) — mutations in TetR (e.g., S12G, E19G, A56P) that invert the response so doxycycline activates rather than silences expression (Tet-On system)
rtTA2s-M2 — improved reverse transactivator with tighter doxycycline dependence
codon-optimized tTA — human codon-optimized version for enhanced mammalian expression
tTA-advanced (Takara Bio) — proprietary optimized variant with reduced leakiness
known_misannotations: Frequently confused or conflated with rtTA (reverse tetracycline transactivator); tTA is Tet-Off (expression ON without drug), rtTA is Tet-On (expression ON with drug)
Sometimes annotated as 'TetR' alone, omitting the VP16 activation domain that defines tTA
Entire TRE-tTA bidirectional cassette occasionally annotated as a single tTA feature
VP16 activation domain subregion may be separately annotated as 'VP16' in the same construct, creating redundant overlapping annotations
tTA-S2 and tTA2 variants sometimes labeled generically as 'tTA' without distinguishing the improved generation
notes: tTA is the original 'Tet-Off' transactivator (Gossen & Bujard, 1992, PNAS); transgene expression is ON by default and switched OFF by doxycycline addition—the opposite logic of the Tet-On (rtTA) system. A matched TRE-containing responder plasmid is required in trans. High tTA expression levels can be cytotoxic in some cell lines; titering the tTA vector or using weaker promoters to drive tTA is often necessary. Doxycycline is preferred over tetracycline in practice due to superior cell permeability, lower toxicity, and faster clearance. For inducible systems in new designs, rtTA-based Tet-On systems are now more commonly chosen because the 'drug-on' logic is experimentally more convenient.

description: Cre (Cyclization Recombination) recombinase is a tyrosine site-specific recombinase from bacteriophage P1 that catalyzes recombination between 34-bp loxP recognition sites. In plasmid engineering it is used to excise, invert, or integrate DNA flanked by loxP sites, enabling conditional knockouts, floxed allele deletion, reporter activation, and inducible expression systems. The Cre/lox system is one of the most widely deployed recombination tools in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic genetic engineering.
mechanism: Cre binds as a homodimer to each loxP half-site and assembles a synaptic tetramer that makes staggered single-strand cuts at the 8-bp spacer, exchanges strands via a covalent 3′-phosphotyrosine intermediate, and ligates to complete recombination. Directly oriented loxP sites yield excision/deletion; inversely oriented sites yield inversion; sites on separate molecules yield integration.
known_variants: iCre — codon-optimized for mammalian expression, reduced CpG content, improved nuclear localization
CreERT2 — Cre fused to triple-mutant estrogen receptor LBD (G400V/M543A/L544A); tamoxifen-inducible, cytoplasmic sequestration until ligand addition
CreERT — earlier single-mutant ER-LBD fusion; less stringent tamoxifen dependence than ERT2
Cre-PR — fusion to progesterone receptor LBD for mifepristone-inducible activation
Split-Cre — two inactive fragments reconstituted by protein-protein interaction or chemical inducers
SLiCE Cre — bacterial cell lysate-based in vitro Cre for seamless cloning
Cre-2A fusion constructs — Cre linked via self-cleaving peptide to fluorescent reporters
known_misannotations: iCre frequently labeled as 'Cre' without indicating codon optimization, causing GC-content and sequence mismatches during auto-annotation
CreERT and CreERT2 conflated despite distinct tamoxifen sensitivities and leakiness profiles
NLS-Cre (nuclear localization signal fused) annotated as plain 'Cre', obscuring the larger ORF size
Cre-ERT2 fusion ORF (~2.7 kb) sometimes split into separate 'Cre' and 'ERT2' feature annotations by parsers that expect the 1029-bp ORF
P1 phage Cre CDS occasionally mis-strand-annotated when extracted from the complementary strand of P1 genome records
notes: The wild-type Cre ORF is exactly 1029 bp (343 aa). loxP sites (required substrates, not part of the Cre ORF) are 34 bp each and must be present in the target for recombination to occur. Cre has low toxicity in bacteria and mice but high-level constitutive expression can cause chromosomal rearrangements at cryptic lox-like sites in mammalian genomes; inducible CreERT2 is preferred for in vivo use. Cre is active without cofactors or ATP across all tested organisms.

description: The Flp (flippase) recombinase is a tyrosine family site-specific recombinase encoded by the 2-micron plasmid of Saccharomyces cerevisiae. It catalyzes recombination between FRT (Flp Recognition Target) sequences, enabling gene excision, inversion, or cassette exchange depending on FRT site orientation and placement. It is widely used in conditional gene knockout strategies, Flp-In stable cell line generation, and inducible combinatorial expression systems.
mechanism: Flp binds as a dimer to each 13-bp inverted repeat of the 34-bp FRT site; two dimers synapse to form a tetrameric complex that introduces staggered double-strand breaks within the 8-bp asymmetric spacer via a covalent 3′-phosphotyrosine intermediate, with strand exchange and ligation completing recombination without requiring ATP, metal cofactors, or high-energy molecules.
known_variants: Flpe — enhanced variant with four point mutations conferring higher thermostability and activity at 37°C; codon-optimized for mammalian expression (Buchholz et al. 1998)
FlpO — independently codon-optimized for mammalian expression with distinct codon usage from Flpe, used in Rosa26-targeted alleles
Flp(opt) — generic term for various additional human or mammalian codon-optimized versions with differing efficacies
Flp-ERT2 — fusion with mutant estrogen ligand-binding domain for tamoxifen-inducible nuclear translocation
known_misannotations: Often annotated simply as 'FLP' without specifying native yeast vs. mammalian-optimized variant (Flpe/FlpO), obscuring expected 37°C activity
FRT recognition site and Flp recombinase sometimes co-annotated or conflated under a single 'Flp/FRT' label
Flpe and FlpO frequently interchanged in annotations despite distinct codon usage and slightly different activity profiles
Occasionally mislabeled as Cre recombinase due to similar function; the two enzymes share no sequence homology and use different recognition sites (FRT vs. loxP)
notes: Native Flp has suboptimal recombination efficiency at 37°C due to reduced thermostability; Flpe and FlpO are the preferred variants for mammalian applications. The Thermo Fisher Flp-In system uses pOG44 (expressing Flpe) to integrate a GOI at a pre-inserted FRT site. Recombination leaves a characteristic 34-bp FRT scar. Minimal functional FRT is ~48 bp including flanking sequence context. Unlike Cre-lox, Flp/FRT is commonly used for marker excision in bacterial and yeast genetics as well as mammalian conditional systems.

description: PhiC31 integrase is a serine integrase from the Streptomyces bacteriophage ΦC31 that catalyzes site-specific recombination between attB and attP recognition sequences. It is widely used in plasmid engineering and gene therapy for stable genomic integration, directing unidirectional insertion of an attB-containing donor plasmid into pseudo-attP sites in the host genome.
